text:
EDMUND. I know not why, save that he covets gold.
He brands me base, a cuckoo in the nest.
But whether I was strictly bred or no,
That burden rests upon my mother’s soul.
Yet that I am as stoutly framed, my lord—
Blessings upon the blood that fashioned me!—
Survey our statures and decide the truth.
If ailing Lord Roland sired us both,
And were our sire, and this boy takes his shape,
O frail Lord Roland, father, on my knees
I praise the fates I was not stamped like thee!
DUKE VINCENT. What brazen spirit hath the heavens sent?
DUCHESS CLARA. He wears the very cast of Count Valerius;
The bold demeanor of his eye matches.
Do you not trace some shadow of that lord
In the broad shoulders of this gentleman?
DUKE VINCENT. My sight hath measured his proportions well,
And finds them absolute Valerius. Boy, declare,
What drives thee to demand thy brother’s wealth?
EDMUND. Because he bears a hollow frame, like Roland.
With half my girth he claims the whole estate:
A brittle reed commands ten thousand crowns!
ARTHUR. My sovereign lord, when my late father lived,
The Count Valerius kept my father close—
EDMUND. Soft, sir, this argument wins not the gold.
Your tale must prove how close he kept my mother.
ARTHUR. And once dispatched him to the northern front,
To Scottish borders, there to hold the line
And parley with the rebels of the march.
The Count took profit of his distant post,
And in the interim lodged at our estate;
How he conquered there I blush to tell;
But facts remain: vast tracts of hills and streams
Between my father and my mother lay,
As I have heard my father swear himself,
When this robust adventurer was conceived.
Upon his dying bed he drafted terms
To leave his wealth to me, and swore an oath
That this my mother’s child was none of his;
And if he were, he tumbled to the earth
Some sixteen weeks before the natural term.
Therefore, my liege, grant me my rightful due,
My father’s wealth, as was my father’s will.
DUKE VINCENT. Sirrah, your brother is a lawful child;
Your father’s lady bore him within bonds,
And if she strayed, the trespass was her own;
A peril native to the common lot
Of wedded men. Tell me, if this great Count,
Who, as you claim, took pains to forge this youth,
Had of your father sought to take the boy?
In truth, good youth, your father might have held
This colt, dropped in his barn, from all the earth;
Indeed, he might; thus, if he were the Count’s,
The Count could not compel him; nor your sire,
Though none of his, discard him. Thus it stands:
Your mother’s son did prove your father’s heir;
Your father’s heir must hold your father’s gold.
ARTHUR. Shall then my father’s testament be void
To cast aside a rogue that shares no blood?
EDMUND. Of no more force to cast me out, good sir,
Than was his frail intent to sire me, I think.
DUCHESS CLARA. Which path wouldst thou prefer: to be a Roland,
And like thy brother, gorge upon the wealth,
Or the acknowledged blood of brave Valerius,
Master of nought but thine own consequence?
EDMUND. Madam, if my dear brother wore my flesh
And I wore his, Lord Roland’s heir, like him;
If my firm legs were merely walking-sticks,
My arms like withered vines, my cheek so pale
That I would fear to wear a heavy helm
Lest men should cry “The wind will snap him double!”
And, to his form, were heir to all this gold,
May I be rooted to this marble floor,
I’d give the coin to keep this rugged face.
I would not be Lord Twig for all the world.
DUCHESS CLARA. Thy spirit pleases me. Wilt leave thy gold,
Resign the manor to him, and ride with me?
I march for Spain and need a captain’s sword.
EDMUND. Brother, digest the gold, I’ll carve my fate.
Your brittle bones have bought ten thousand crowns,
Though auction off your frame, you’d fetch a groat.
Madam, I am your servant to the grave.
DUCHESS CLARA. Come then, and walk before me to the court.
EDMUND. A soldier’s grace yields up the path to rank.
DUKE VINCENT. What name dost bear?
EDMUND. Edmund, my sovereign, thus my name begins;
Edmund, the lady Roland’s eldest son.

KING CHARLES. I am a sovereign, and I will be heard.
MORTIMER. My lord, the grievances that brought us armed
Cannot be mended by a monarch’s breath;
Therefore be mute.
PERCY. Then, executioner, make bare thy blade.
By all the saints that watch the turning world,
I swear Lord Mortimer is brave in words alone.
FREDERICK. Tell me, King Charles, wilt thou resign my right?
A thousand men stand fasting on this plain
Who shall not sup until they see thee fall.
SALISBURY. Deny us, and their slaughter is thine own;
For Frederick wears the armor of his truth.
PRINCE ARTHUR. If treason be the truth that Salisbury boasts,
Then hell is heaven, and every sin is pure.
PERCY. Whoever sired thee, boy, thy mother speaks;
For well I know that shrill and biting tone.
QUEEN ELEANOR. But thou art shaped like nothing natural,
A crooked shadow, nature's foul mistake,
Branded by God to warn the honest man,
Like venomous vipers or the rabid hound.
PERCY. O brass of Spain, masked in our English gold!
Whose father claims the dukedom of a patch,
As if a puddle should be called the sea.
Hast thou no shame, remembering thy descent,
To bare the malice of thy beggar’s heart?
FREDERICK. A beggar's rags were worth a kingly ransom
To teach this arrogant harpy what she is.
False Cressida was truer far than thou,
Though thy weak husband play the cuckold's part.
Never was Troy so ruined by a face
As this whole kingdom has been wrecked by thee.
His father marched triumphant through the East,
And made the foreign emperors bow their heads;
And had he wed a woman of his rank,
He might have left a peaceful realm to us.
But when he took this viper to his breast,
And gave thy ruined sire a golden seat,
That very hour he bred the bloody storm
That stripped his father's conquests from our hands
And seeded dark rebellion in our streets.
What caused this march of iron but thy pride?
Hadst thou been humble, we had never stirred;
And out of pity for this feeble king,
We would have let our title sleep in peace.
MORTIMER. But when we saw thy winter blight our spring,
And thy dark shadow choke our ripening fields,
We brought the hatchet to thy poisoned root.
And though the falling branches strike ourselves,
Know this: since we have raised our hands to strike,
We will not sheathe our weapons till thou fall,
Or water the dry earth with noble blood.
FREDERICK. In this resolve, I fling my challenge down;
I will not waste another breath on thee,
Since thou forbidd'st the wretched King to speak.
Sound out the horns! Let bloody banners fly;
We win the crown, or on this field we die!
QUEEN ELEANOR. Hold, Frederick.
FREDERICK. No, brawling queen, our talking time is done.
Thy bitter tongue hath cost ten thousand lives.
[Exeunt.]
SCENE III. A field of battle near Ravenspur
Alarums. Excursions. Enter Salisbury.
SALISBURY. Out of all breath, like hounds that lose the scent,
I lean upon my sword a little while;
For heavy blows received, and heavy dealt,
Have drained the marrow from my shaking limbs.
Despite my will, I must draw breath or die.
Enter Frederick, running.
FREDERICK. Weep, gentle skies, or strike me, cruel earth!
The day is lost, and Frederick's star falls down.
SALISBURY. What news, my lord? Is there no ray of hope?
Enter Percy.
PERCY. Our hope is dust, our fortune is a grave;
The lines are shattered, and the vanguard breaks.
What is your counsel? Whither do we run?
FREDERICK. Flight is in vain; their horses run like wind.
We are too spent to outpace their revenge.
Enter Mortimer.
MORTIMER. Ah, Salisbury, why didst thou leave the flank?
The thirsty soil has drunk thy nephew's blood,
Pierced by the heavy spike of Arthur's spear.
And in the bitter agony of death
He cried aloud, ringing across the din:
“Salisbury, avenge! Uncle, avenge my fall!”
Then, crushed beneath the trampling of the hooves,
That dyed their iron shoes in his red life,
The valiant youth surrendered up his soul.
SALISBURY. Then let the meadows gorge upon our blood!
I will unhorse myself, I will not flee.
Why stand we weeping like a frightened child,
Mourning our ruin while the butchers laugh,
And staring merely, as if all this woe
Were but a pageant played upon a stage?
Here on the bloodied grass I swear to God,
I will not take a step, nor yield an inch,
Until the dark night seals my heavy eyes,
Or Heaven grants me vengeance on my foes.

VALERIUS. Why, didst thou not descend from the high clouds?
PEASANT. The clouds? By my faith, sir, I have never been so high. God save me from climbing to the clouds before my time. I am taking these capons to the lord justice, to settle a bloody fray between my cousin and the king's tax-gatherer.
CASSIUS. Why, brother, this is the perfect vessel for your suit. Let him present the capons to the King.
VALERIUS. Tell me, friend, can you offer a petition to the King with proper reverence?
PEASANT. Truly, sir, I have never petitioned any man but the tavern-keeper.
VALERIUS. Sirrah, step closer. Make no delay,
But give thy plump fowl to his Majesty.
Through me thou shalt find favor at his court.
Take this; here is good copper for thy pains.
Give me the wax and seal. Sirrah, canst thou
With a bold front deliver up my plea?
PEASANT. Aye, my lord.
VALERIUS. Then hold this scroll. And when you come to him, kneel upon the stones, kiss his signet ring, yield up the birds, and wait for your great reward. I will be near; do it without fear.
PEASANT. Leave it to me, sir; I warrant you.
VALERIUS. Sirrah, hast thou a gauntlet? Let me see it.
Here, Cassius, bind it up within the scroll,
For thou hast penned it like a humble slave.
And when thou hast presented it to the King,
Find me at the tavern and report his words.
PEASANT. God keep you, sir; I shall. [Exit.]
VALERIUS. Come, Cassius, let us walk. Lucius, attend me. [Exeunt.]
SCENE IV. The Royal Court.
Enter King Leontes, Queen Isabella, her sons Ferdinand and Roderigo, with Attendants. The King holds torn placards in his fist.
LEONTES. Look here, my lords, what insolence is this!
Was ever any sovereign of this realm
So openly abused, confronted thus,
And for the simple execution of the law,
Held up to such contempt? You know it well,
As do the watchful heavens above our heads,
Despite what these dissenters whisper round,
Nothing hath passed but strict and proper law
Against the treasonous nephews of Valerius.
What if his grief hath alienated his mind?
Must we endure his daily mockeries,
His sudden fits, his rage, and bitter taunts?
And now he posts his letters to the ghosts!
See, here "To Justice," and "To the Fates,"
This "To the Underworld," this to the sun.
Sweet broadsides to be plastered on our walls!
What is this but a libel on the crown,
Declaring our corruption to the mob?
A merry jest, is it not, gentlemen?
As if to say, there is no justice here.
But by my life, his counterfeit disease
Shall be no shield to these rebellions;
He and his faction soon shall know my rule
Is absolute; and if my justice sleeps,
He’ll wake it, and I'll strike the traitor down.
ISABELLA. My gracious sovereign, noble King Leontes,
The very sun and center of my heart,
Be patient with the old Valerius' faults;
They are the fruits of sorrow for his kin,
Whose bloody ends have pierced his ancient soul.
Far better to ignore his manic grief
Than punish an old warrior for his tears.
[Aside.] Thus must Isabella play the gentle dove.
But old Valerius, I have set the snare;
Once you are dead, and Roderigo safe,
The realm is ours, and all my fears are gone.
Enter Peasant.
How now, good fellow, seekest thou the court?
PEASANT. Yes, truly, if your ladyship be the queenship.
ISABELLA. I am the Queen, but yonder sits the King.
PEASANT. That's him. God and Saint George give you good morrow. I have brought you a parchment and a brace of capons here.
[Leontes reads the scroll.]
LEONTES. Guards, drag this villain out and sever his head!
PEASANT. How much is the reward for that?
ISABELLA. Come, fool, you are for the block.
PEASANT. The block! By my troth, I should have kept the capons and brought my own head in the basket. [Exit guarded.]
LEONTES. Outrageous and unbearable affronts!
Shall I sit by and stomach this abuse?
I know the architect of this foul jest.
He’d have the world believe his rebel kin,
Who lawfully were shortened by the axe,
Were slaughtered by my tyranny and hate!

After much weeping, a verbal pact was sworn between us: first, that I would not squander the southern estate and would remain her lawful husband; secondly, that my visits to the city would never exceed a month without her blessing; thirdly, that I should not parade any permanent mistresses in our province; fourthly, in exchange for this, Elena Ivanovna permitted my liaisons with the local widows, provided it was done with the utmost discretion; fifthly, heaven forbid I should ever seek a formal separation; sixthly, should I—which God forbid—be struck by a genuine, consuming infatuation, I was obliged to confess it to her immediately. On this final point, however, Elena Ivanovna felt entirely secure. She possessed a coldly logical mind, and so she naturally viewed me as a jaded cynic, thoroughly incapable of true devotion. But a logical mind and a wounded vanity clash terribly, and that is precisely where our misery began. To judge certain individuals fairly, one must suspend ordinary moralizing and discard the conventional attitudes of society. I trust your intellect more than the judgment of the crowd. Perhaps you have already absorbed a great deal of scandalous nonsense regarding Elena Ivanovna. She certainly had her foolish eccentricities, but I tell you in all honesty that I genuinely regret the profound miseries into which I dragged her. Well, I suppose that is a sufficient eulogy for the most tolerant wife of a deeply intolerable husband. When she raged, I usually stared out the window and smoked in silence; this passive gentlemanly cruelty rarely failed to achieve its aim, and eventually, it even made her feel she had won. But your cousin she could not abide, at least not at the end. How she ever decided to bring such an extraordinary creature into our suffocating home as a music teacher, I will never understand. My only explanation is that Elena Ivanovna was a prone to sudden, dramatic obsessions and simply became enamored—platonically, of course—with your cousin. And who wouldn't? Just look at Natalya Pavlovna! I recognized the hazard instantly, and believe it or not, I resolved to feign complete blindness to her charms. But Natalya Pavlovna herself initiated the conversation, can you imagine? Would you also believe that Elena Ivanovna was genuinely furious with me at first for not echoing her rapturous, daily compliments about the girl? I don’t know what she expected! Naturally, Elena Ivanovna poured out all my darkest secrets to Natalya Pavlovna. She had a wretched compulsion to broadcast our private miseries to anyone who would listen; how could she spare her charming new confidante? They must have spent hours dissecting my character, and Natalya Pavlovna undoubtedly absorbed all those grotesque legends that circulate about my youth... I am willing to wager you have caught wind of them too?”
“I have. Ivanov claimed you drove a young cadet to suicide. Is that the truth?”
“Spare me those melodramatic inventions, I implore you,” said Grigory Ilyich, his face darkening with sudden revulsion. “If you are so desperate to wallow in that nonsense, I shall enlighten you another time, but for now...”
“They also spoke of a serf on your brother's estate whom you ordered beaten.”
“I ask you to abandon this line of questioning,” Grigory Ilyich cut in, his voice tightening with obvious impatience. “Was that the same serf whose ghost supposedly haunts my study?... You mentioned him yourself.”
Alexei's expression grew visibly darker. Grigory Ilyich studied his face, and Alexei thought he perceived a glimmer of malicious amusement in the older man's eyes. But Grigory Ilyich smoothed his features and replied with impeccable politeness:
“Yes, exactly. I perceive that your curiosity is quite inflamed, and I promise to gratify it fully when the moment is right. Good heavens! I realize that I must cut quite the tragic, romantic figure in the eyes of provincial society. Imagine my gratitude to Elena Ivanovna for feeding Natalya Pavlovna such lurid and captivating myths about my past. I cannot presume to know precisely how it affected her, but it undeniably served my purposes. Despite Natalya Pavlovna's innate purity and my own thoroughly repulsive demeanor—she was moved, at the very least, by a profound compassion for a ruined man.”

Alarum. Enter Titus.
TITUS. Lost, lost, all lost! I cannot look upon it.
The sapphire galley, the Phoenician pride,
With all her fifty vessels, turns the prow.
My sight is cursed to see it.
Enter Marcellus.
MARCELLUS. Gods and demons,
The entire host of them!
TITUS. What moves your rage?
MARCELLUS. The better fraction of the earth is thrown
To absolute folly. We have bartered away
Empires and territories.
TITUS. How stands the vanguard?
MARCELLUS. On our flank, like a doomed contagion,
Where ruin is assured. That painted sovereign of the East,
Whom plagues devour, in the very heat of war,
When victory stood balanced on a sword—
Or leaning to our favor—the panic takes her,
Like a frightened deer, raises the oars and flies.
TITUS. I saw the same.
My eyes recoiled from it and could not bear
To watch a moment more.
MARCELLUS. She once departed,
The shattered monument of her thrall, Valerius,
Spreads out his sails and, like a blinded gull,
Abandoning the clash at its very peak,
Speeds after her. I never witnessed a retreat
Of such disgrace. Wisdom, valor, discipline,
Never yet did so debase themselves.
TITUS. Alas, alas!
Enter Lucan.
LUCAN. Our destiny upon the waves is drowned
And sinks most bitterly. Had our commander
Been who he was born to be, we had prevailed.
Oh, he has set a pattern for our cowardice
Most vilely by his own!
TITUS. Aye, is your mind set there?
Why, then, the day is done.
LUCAN. Toward the northern straits they are escaped.
MARCELLUS. The wind is fair for it, and there I’ll wait
To see what follows.
LUCAN. To the usurper will I yield
My banners and my steel. Five captains already
Show me the path of treason.
TITUS. I’ll yet pursue
The broken fate of great Valerius,
Though my better sense cries out against me.
[Exeunt.]
SCENE XI. A Chamber in the Citadel.
Enter Valerius with attendants.
VALERIUS. Listen, the earth commands I tread no more upon its dirt.
It blushes to sustain me. Comrades, gather here.
I am so shadowed in this fading world that I
Have lost the sun forever. I have a vault
Bursting with silver. Take it, share it out. Flee,
And make your treaty with the foe.
ALL. We flee? Not us.
VALERIUS. I have run myself, and taught the bravest men
To turn and show their backs. Companions, go.
I have myself determined on a path
Which requires no escort. Begone.
My silver’s in the fortress. Take it. O,
I chased a sight that burns my eyes to see.
My very veins make war, for my old blood
Mocks my youthful fire, and they rebuke each other
For cowardice and lust. Good friends, depart.
You shall have seals from me to wealthy lords that will
Secure your safety. I pray you, do not weep,
Nor speak of your devotion. Take the cue
That my dark ruin offers. Leave the man
Who has forsaken himself. To the harbor, now.
I will bestow upon you ships and coin.
Give me some peace, I beg of you—I pray you, go;
Nay, do it; for truly I have lost the right to order.
Therefore I beg you. I will join you shortly.
[Sits down.]
Enter Xanthia led by Livia, Flavia, and Pindarus.
PINDARUS. Nay, gentle lady, go to him! Comfort him.
FLAVIA. Do, my sovereign.
LIVIA. Speak! What else remains?
XANTHIA. Let me rest here. O Fates!
VALERIUS. No, no, no, no, no.
PINDARUS. Do you see, my lord?
VALERIUS. O, shame, shame, shame!
LIVIA. Madam.
FLAVIA. Madam, O sweet queen!
PINDARUS. Lord, my lord!
VALERIUS. Yes, the conqueror, yes. He at the river kept
His blade like an ornament, while I hewed down
The fierce and seasoned Thracians, and 'twas I
That the wild rebels broke. He only traded
In his deputies, and had no footing
In the bloody dust of war. But now—it matters not.
XANTHIA. Ah, stand aside.
PINDARUS. The Queen, my lord, the Queen!
FLAVIA. Approach him, madam; give him words.
He is undone with sheer disgrace.
XANTHIA. Well then, support me. O!
PINDARUS. Most mighty general, stand. The Queen draws near.
Her face is fallen, and despair will claim her unless
Your pity grants a reprieve.
VALERIUS. I have murdered reputation,
A most unmanly betrayal.
PINDARUS. Sir, your Queen.
VALERIUS. O, where have you dragged me, Sovereign? See
How I must hide my ruin from your gaze,
By looking back at what I left behind,
Crushed in my disgrace.
XANTHIA. O my lord, my lord,
Forgive my trembling fleet! I never dreamed
That you would track my flight.
VALERIUS. Sovereign, you knew too well
My soul was to your canvas tethered tight,
And you would drag me with you.

“It appears a most disastrous turn of events.”
“You must speak to Clara at once. Inform her that you demand her compliance in this marriage.”
“Let her come down. I shall deliver my verdict.”
Mrs. Sterling tugged the bell-pull, and Miss Clara was requested in the study. “Step forward, my dear,” declared her father as she entered. “I have summoned you on a matter of the utmost gravity. I am told that Mr. Pendleton has requested your hand. Is this accurate?”
Clara confirmed that it was.
“Very good—and this proposal of marriage you have declined?”
“I have, father.”
“Very good. We arrive at the crux of the matter. Your mother insists that you accept him. Is that correct, Mrs. Sterling?”
“Indeed, or I shall cast her out entirely.”
“A tragic dilemma stands before you, Clara. From this moment, you must lose one of your parents. Your mother will cast you out if you do not marry Mr. Pendleton, and I shall do the same if you do.”
Clara struggled to suppress a laugh at such a sudden turn; but Mrs. Sterling, who had firmly believed her husband would act as she dictated, was profoundly outraged. “Whatever do you mean, Mr. Sterling, speaking in such a manner? You gave me your word to compel her to the match.”
“My dear,” her husband replied calmly, “I have but two modest requests. First, that you permit me the liberty of my own judgment in this affair; and, second, the liberty of my study. I should be most grateful to have the room to myself at your earliest convenience.”
Despite her fury at her husband, Mrs. Sterling did not abandon her campaign. She badgered Clara relentlessly, employing flattery and menace in equal measure. She attempted to recruit her eldest daughter, Mary, to her cause, but Mary gently refused to intervene; and Clara, fluctuating between grave sincerity and lighthearted amusement, withstood the barrage. Her tone shifted, but her resolve remained absolute. Mr. Pendleton, in the interim, was brooding alone over the morning’s events. He held too high an opinion of his own consequence to understand why his cousin might reject him; and though his vanity was bruised, his heart was entirely unaffected. His affection for her had been merely theoretical, and the prospect of her incurring her mother’s wrath stifled any lingering disappointment.
Amidst this domestic upheaval, Miss Eleanor Hayes arrived to call upon the family. She was intercepted in the hall by Beatrice, who rushed forward and whispered excitedly, “I am so thrilled you are here, for we are in such an uproar! What do you suppose occurred this morning? Mr. Pendleton offered for Clara, and she flatly refused him.”
Eleanor had scarce a moment to react before they were met by Sarah, who eagerly repeated the tale. Upon entering the sitting room, where Mrs. Sterling sat in isolation, the matriarch instantly seized upon the topic, pleading with Miss Hayes to pity her and to sway her obstinate friend to obey her family’s commands. “I implore you, dear Miss Hayes,” she lamented with a theatrical sigh; “no one supports my cause, no one champions my side; I am treated dreadfully, and no one considers my fragile constitution.”
The entrance of Mary and Clara spared Eleanor from answering. “Ah, here she is,” Mrs. Sterling carried on, “appearing entirely unbothered, caring no more for our survival than if we resided in London, so long as her own whims are satisfied. But heed my words, Miss Clara, if you persist in dismissing every eligible suitor in this fashion, you shall never secure a match—and I cannot fathom who will support you when your father passes. I certainly shall not have the means, so consider yourself warned. I wash my hands of you from this moment. I stated in the study that I would never speak to you again, and I am a woman of my word. I find no joy in conversing with ungrateful offspring. Truthfully, I find little joy in conversing with anyone at all. Those who endure such nervous afflictions as I do possess very little appetite for conversation.”

VALERIUS. Do you perceive the scandal?
CASSIUS. I cannot wish the scandal undone, the fruit of it being so handsome.
VALERIUS. Yet I have a lawful heir, sir, some year older than this boy, who is no more cherished in my heart. Though this rogue slipped into the world before the priest gave leave, his mother was a beauty; there was much merriment at his forging, and the bastard must be claimed. Do you know this valiant youth, Marcus?
MARCUS. I do not, my lord.
VALERIUS. My Lord of Cassius: hold him henceforth as my esteemed friend.
MARCUS. My humble duty to your lordship.
CASSIUS. I must embrace you, and hope to know you better.
MARCUS. Sir, I shall strive to be worthy.
VALERIUS. He hath been abroad these nine years, and shall depart again. The King approaches.
[Trumpets sound.]
Enter King Leontes, Duke of Milan, Duke of Venice, Isabella, Beatrice, Diana, and Attendants.
LEONTES. Escort the lords of Aragon and Naples, Valerius.
VALERIUS. I shall, your grace.
[Exeunt Valerius and Marcus.]
LEONTES. In the interim, we shall reveal our secret design. Fetch the map. Let it be known we have severed our empire into three. It is our firm resolve to cast the heavy burdens of state from our tired shoulders, bestowing them on younger backs, while we crawl unencumbered to our grave. Our son of Milan, and you, our equally beloved son of Venice, we intend this very hour to announce our daughters' respective inheritances, so that future bloodshed may be thwarted now. The princes of Aragon and Naples, fierce rivals for our youngest daughter's hand, have tarried long in our court and demand their answer. Tell me, my daughters—since we now strip ourselves of power, lands, and the cares of the crown—which of you shall we say loves us the most? We wish to deal our greatest generosity where natural affection and merit align. Isabella, our firstborn, speak first.
ISABELLA. Sire, I love you beyond what mere words can measure; more dearly than my own sight, the open air, or my freedom. Beyond all treasures, however rare or costly; as much as life itself, matched with health, grace, and honor. As deeply as any child has ever loved, or any father has ever been loved. A devotion that renders my breath short and my tongue tied; beyond all limits of comparison, I love you.
DIANA. [Aside.] What shall Diana say? Love, and keep silent.
LEONTES. Of all these borders, from this river to this valley, rich with dark woods and fertile plains, with abundant streams and sprawling pastures, we make you sovereign. To your and Milan's heirs, let this be everlasting. What says our second daughter, our beloved Beatrice, wife of Venice? Speak.
BEATRICE. Sire, I am forged of the exact same steel as my sister, and value myself at her equal. In my honest heart, I find she has spoken the very shape of my love; only she falls short, in that I declare myself a sworn enemy to every other earthly joy that the senses can perceive, and find my only true happiness in your royal affection.
DIANA. [Aside.] Then poor Diana. And yet, not truly poor, for my love is far heavier than my hollow words.
LEONTES. To you and your heirs forever we bequeath this wealthy third of our grand realm; no less in scope, revenue, and beauty than what was granted to Isabella. Now, our final joy, though the youngest and smallest; for whose tender affections the vineyards of Aragon and the gold of Naples bitterly compete. What can you say to claim a third more magnificent than your sisters'? Speak.
DIANA. Nothing, my lord.
LEONTES. Nothing?
DIANA. Nothing.
LEONTES. Nothing will be born of nothing. Speak again.
DIANA. Wretched as I am, I cannot drag my heart into my mouth. I love your majesty according to my duty; no more, and no less.
LEONTES. How now, Diana? Fix your speech a little, lest you ruin your own fortune.
DIANA. My good liege, you fathered me, raised me, and loved me. I return those obligations exactly as is proper: I obey you, I love you, and I honor you above all. Why do my sisters have husbands if they claim they love only you? Perhaps, when I take a husband, that lord who accepts my vows shall take half my love with him, and half my care and duty. I am certain I shall never wed like my sisters, to give my father all my love.
LEONTES. But does your heart truly agree with this?
DIANA. It does, my good lord.
LEONTES. So young, and so hard?
DIANA. So young, my lord, and truthful.

She commands Prudence to be constantly vigilant in calculating future yields, ensuring that Wealth may increase and remain secure. Justice she directs to settle all debts and observe contracts strictly, not from a love of fairness, but to avoid the penalties of the magistrate and to secure the commercial alliances necessary for acquiring more gold. Fortitude she orders to endure the bitterest colds and the most exhausting labors, keeping the mind firmly fixed on the promise of future profits, so that the pain of present toil is swallowed up by the anticipation of a full treasury. Temperance she restricts to a frugal diet and modest apparel, lest excessive spending diminish the hoard, and thus Wealth, which the avaricious elevate as the highest good, be tragically diminished. Thus the noble virtues, stripped of their inherent majesty, are reduced to the grovelling servants of Wealth, acting as the slaves of a cruel and barren master. There is nothing, our scholars argue, more degraded and perverse than this arrangement, nor anything that right-minded men should find more repulsive. And they are entirely correct. But I do not believe this hierarchy would be any more acceptable even if the virtues were made subservient to Intellectual Prestige; for although that prestige is not a crass material obsession, it is nevertheless bloated with arrogance and overflowing with vanity. Therefore, it is beneath the eternal dignity of the virtues to be yoked to academic pride, wherein Prudence learns nothing, Justice balances nothing, and Temperance restrains nothing, except to ensure that a man is lauded by his peers and his own ego is inflated. Nor can such men acquit themselves of hypocrisy when they, claiming to despise the applause of the crowd, retreat inward to admire their own intellects, feeling entirely satisfied with themselves. For this supposed virtue—if it can be called virtue at all—is merely another form of human flattery; he who seeks to impress himself is still bowing to a man. Conversely, the man of genuine piety, whose faith and love are anchored in God, focuses his scrutiny on his own spiritual poverty, finding displeasure in himself so that he might find pleasure in the truth. He attributes whatever goodness he possesses not to his own intellect, but to the mercy of Him whom he reverences, offering gratitude for the afflictions that have been healed, and ceaselessly praying for the purification of those that remain unhealed.
	•	That worldly prosperity is distributed by Him who holds all authority, and by whose divine plan the universe is governed.
This being the case, we do not ascribe the accumulation of national wealth and the flourishing of empires to anyone other than the true God. He reserves the eternal treasures of heaven exclusively for the righteous, yet He distributes earthly riches to both the righteous and the wicked, according to His own will, which is always perfectly just. For although we may apprehend fragments of the design by which He governs the world, whenever He chooses to reveal them, it is ultimately beyond our finite capacity to dissect the secret motives of human hearts and perfectly measure why one kingdom prospers while another falls into ruin. Therefore, the one true God, who never abandons humanity to absolute chaos but provides just governance, granted immense riches and maritime supremacy to the Carthaginians when He saw fit, just as He did to the Greeks, and even to the Egyptians, who, as their own monuments demonstrate, worshipped beasts and rivers—to say nothing of the Hebrew people, whom I have already discussed, who worshipped only the true God during their time of sovereignty. The same God, therefore, who gave the Egyptians overflowing harvests, though they did not offer sacrifices to the goddess Ceres, and who bestowed the treasures of the earth, though they did not venerate the many spirits which the Romans believed to preside over the soil—He, I affirm, granted them economic dominion, despite their absolute ignorance of the very deities to whom other nations foolishly attributed their prosperity.

“…and so she invited the two Woodwards to attend: but Amelia was unwell, and so Charlotte was compelled to arrive alone; and then, what do you suppose we contrived? We adorned young Mr. Townsend in a velvet bonnet and silk pelisse, expressly to present him as a visiting dowager,—only imagine the amusement! Not a living soul was privy to the jest but Captain and Mrs. Lennox, and Florence and myself, except my aunt, for we were obliged to utilize her wardrobe; and you cannot conceive how elegant he appeared! When Lieutenant Davies, and Carter, and Smith, and a few others walked in, they were entirely deceived. Heavens! How I giggled! And so did Mrs. Lennox. I was certain I should expire from laughing. And that caused the gentlemen to suspect a trick, and they presently unraveled the mystery.”
With such accounts of their lively gatherings and pleasantries did Florence, aided by the prompting of her younger sister, occupy her companions all the way to Willowbourne. Eleanor tuned them out as best she could, but there was no escaping the frequent utterance of Carter’s name. Their welcome at the manor was exceptionally warm. Mrs. Crawford rejoiced to see Jane looking as radiant as ever; and more than once during the meal did Mr. Crawford remark to Eleanor,——
“I am exceedingly glad you have returned, Eleanor.”
Their gathering in the dining-room was considerable, for nearly all the neighboring Winslows came to greet the travelers and hear the tidings; and various were the topics that engaged them: Lady Winslow was inquiring of Jane, across the table, regarding the health and gardens of her married sister; Mrs. Crawford was doubly occupied, on one side gathering a report of the latest metropolitan fashions from Jane, and on the other, relaying them to the younger Miss Winslows; and Florence, in a tone far more piercing than anyone else’s, was detailing the morning's exploits to whomever would listen.
“Oh, Beatrice,” said she, “I do wish you had accompanied us, for it was such sport! As we rode along we drew all the curtains tight, and pretended the carriage was entirely vacant; and I would have kept it so the entire journey, if my sister had not felt faint; and when we stopped at the Red Lion, I believe we acted very generously, for we treated the others to the most delightful pastries imaginable, and had you been there, we should have treated you as well. And leaving the inn was just as riotous! I thought we should never manage to climb back into the carriage. I was overcome with merriment. And then we were so boisterous the whole way home! We chattered and laughed with such volume that the whole county might have heard us!”
To this, Beatrice very solemnly answered, “Far be it from me, my dear sister, to disparage such amusements. They are undoubtedly suited to the general disposition of youthful minds. But I must admit they hold no attraction for me. I should vastly prefer the quiet study of a volume.”
But Florence did not register a single word of this rebuttal. She rarely paid attention to anyone for more than a moment, and never heeded Beatrice under any circumstance. In the afternoon Florence was insistent that the young ladies should stroll down to the village, and observe what the townsfolk were about; but Eleanor firmly resisted the proposal. It must not be rumored that the Crawford sisters could not abide at home for half a day before seeking out the militia officers. There was an additional motive, too, for her refusal. She deeply dreaded crossing paths with Carter again, and was determined to evade him for as long as she could. The relief she felt at the regiment’s impending departure was beyond description. In less than a month they would march away, and once they were gone, she prayed there would be no further distress on his account. She had not been situated at home for very long before she perceived that the seaside scheme, which Florence had alluded to at the inn, was the subject of constant debate between her parents. Eleanor recognized instantly that her father had no intention of conceding; but his responses were simultaneously so evasive and ambiguous, that her mother, though frequently frustrated, never abandoned the hope of ultimate triumph.
CHAPTER XX.
Eleanor’s desperation to inform Jane of the remarkable events could no longer be contained; and finally deciding to omit every detail in which her sister was implicated, and warning her to expect a shock, she recounted the following morning the bulk of the encounter between Mr. Sterling and herself.

As fittingly might these stone markers rest in the catacombs of Rome as here. In what register of creation the lost sailors of the world are counted; why it is that a common adage warns that the sea keeps her secrets, though she holds more treasures than the vaults of emperors; how it is that we don the darkest weeds of mourning for a friend who departed but yesterday, yet wear bright colors if he merely sets sail for the distant orient; why we weep with such bitter despair for those whom we vehemently declare are walking the golden streets of paradise; wherefore a sudden shadow in the water will strike terror into the stoutest heart. All these contradictions carry their own weight. Yet Belief, like a scavenger bird, circles the wrecks, and even from these drowned despairs she pulls her most sustaining nourishment. It requires little imagination to guess with what sentiments, on the night before my departure for the Arctic fisheries, I viewed those granite monuments, and by the dim glow of that weeping, dismal afternoon read the doom of the mariners who had ventured before me. Yes, Elias, the same doom may await thee. But somehow my spirit rallied. Marvelous incentives to weigh anchor, excellent prospects for glory, indeed—why, a crushed hull will make me an angel by default. Yes, there is a freezing demise in this trade of the ice—a breathtakingly sudden plunge of a man into the Abyss. But what of it? It seems to me we have entirely misunderstood this business of flesh and spirit. It seems to me that what they call my physical form here on earth is merely my shadow. It seems to me that in viewing eternity, we are too much like barnacles staring at the moon through the brine, and supposing that thick brine to be the clearest ether. It seems to me my body is but the heavy diving-bell of my true intellect. In truth, let the sea take my body if she will, take it I say, it is not my essence. And therefore hurrah for the frozen North; and let the crushing ice and the broken timbers come when they please, for crush my soul, Neptune himself cannot.
CHAPTER 9. The Chancel.
I had not been settled very long before a man of a certain weathered dignity arrived; instantly as the wind-blown door slammed shut behind him, a rapid respectful gaze by all the gathered townsfolk sufficiently proved that this rugged elder was the minister. Yes, it was the renowned Reverend Silas, so named by the sealers, among whom he was held in the highest esteem. He had been a navigator and a harpooner in his youth, but for several decades past had devoted his days to the church. At the time I now speak of, Reverend Silas was in the frosty winter of a vigorous old age; that sort of age which seems to harbor a second burning youth, for among all the deep crevices of his weathered face, there glowed certain warm embers of a renewed vitality—the summer sun burning bright even amidst December’s frost. No one knowing his past could for the first time look upon Reverend Silas without profound curiosity, for there were certain ingrained nautical habits about him, born of that perilous oceanic existence he had survived. When he stepped inside I noted that he bore no parasol, and surely had not ridden in a carriage, for his canvas sou’wester dripped with freezing rain, and his massive wool coat appeared to drag him downward with the sheer weight of the ocean it had drunk. Nevertheless, hat and coat and heavy boots were one by one shed, and hung up in a small alcove by the vestibule; then, dressed in a solemn black suit, he steadily advanced toward the chancel. Like most antique chancels, it was elevated considerably, and since a standard set of steps to such a peak would, by its gradual incline across the floor, severely restrict the already cramped seating of the chapel, the builder, it appeared, had taken a suggestion from Reverend Silas, and completed the chancel without stairs, substituting a slanted gangway plank, much like those used in boarding a frigate from the docks. The widow of a shipwright had furnished the chapel with a beautiful set of braided hemp handrails for this gangway, which, being itself varnished and polished to a rich oak hue, the whole arrangement, considering the character of the church, seemed in no way inappropriate.

For as the apostle writes, "Set your minds on things that are above, not on things that are on earth; for you have died, and your life is hidden with Christ in God." They who have directed their ambitions in such a manner have found comfort during sudden political ruins and exiles, feeling more joy in the celestial honors they have secured by humbling themselves, than sorrow in the worldly titles they forfeited by a desperate and prideful clinging to them. For nothing truly perishes in the collapse of kingdoms save that which is bound to the dust of the earth. Our Lord's command is clear, "Do not fear those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul; rather fear him who can destroy both soul and body in hell." And those who have heeded this warning have discovered in the hour of persecution how wise they were to trust this supreme physician, and most invincible defender of their true life. If many rejoiced that their physical bodies were sheltered in fortresses the marauders failed to breach, how much more profound was the peace of those who, guided by their Creator, had anchored their existence in a sanctuary no human blade can pierce! Thus our venerable Martin, leader of his flock, who willingly relinquished the prestige of the magistrate's chair and embraced the lowly state of a servant, though immensely exalted in grace, when the usurpers stormed the province and bound him in chains, would quietly pray, as he later recounted to us, "O Lord, let me not tremble for my station or my flesh, for my true dignity is held entirely by Thee." For all his honor was deposited where he had been instructed to entrust it by Him who warned that thrones would topple and nations rage. Consequently, those individuals who heeded their Master when He taught them where to seek glory, did not lose their essential nobility even in the degradation of the prisons; while those who now lament their failure to obey Him have discovered the proper estimation of human praise, if not by the humility that would have spared their pride, at least by the experience of the humiliation that shattered it. But some devout and faithful men have been cast into dungeons, that they might be compelled to renounce their allegiances or confess to treasons. They could not, in truth, forfeit that inward loyalty which made them faithful. If, however, they preferred the agonizing rack to the surrender of their political ambitions, then I maintain they were not truly devout. Rather, they should have been instructed that, if they endured such extreme torment for the sake of a fleeting crown, they ought to bear all suffering, if necessary, for the King of Heaven; so they might learn to adore Him who crowns with everlasting glory all who bleed for Him, and not the favor of monarchs, for which it was tragic to suffer, whether they retained it by a treacherous oath, or lost it by an honest word. For under the whip, no one lost Christ by proclaiming Him, no one preserved earthly power except by abandoning their soul. So that possibly the imprisonment which demonstrated that they must fix their hopes on a kingdom they could not lose, was far more beneficial than those high offices which, lacking any eternal merit, only burdened and endangered their anxious occupants. But we are told that some were imprisoned who possessed no political influence to surrender, yet their captors refused to believe them when they said so. These individuals, however, perhaps harbored a secret lust for power, and did not accept their humble station with a sanctified peace; and to them it had to be revealed that not only the actual possession, but the mere craving for worldly authority, merited such bitter correction. And even if they were completely devoid of any hidden thirst for rank, because they lived entirely for the kingdom to come—I cannot say for certain if any such soul was tormented under the false assumption that they held political sway; but if they were, then surely in proclaiming, when put to the question, a holy obscurity, they confessed Christ.

"Hast thou tracked the Shadow Leviathan?" demanded Elias, when the skiff pitched closer.
"Reflect, reflect upon thy shattered timbers and drowned men! Beware the sweeping fin!"
"I declare to thee again, Malachi, that—" But once more the small boat was propelled forward as if by unseen specters. Nothing was said for several moments, while a chaotic series of rogue waves surged past, which by some strange humor of the ocean were churning, not swelling. Meanwhile, the suspended carcass of the pilot whale pitched about furiously, and Malachi was observed glaring at it with a dread that scarcely fit his self-proclaimed holy mantle. When this violent motion subsided, Captain Thorne commenced a grim recounting of the Shadow Leviathan; not, however, without constant interjections from Malachi, whenever the creature's name was uttered, and the turbulent sea that appeared to do its bidding.
It emerged that the Providence had only recently passed the Cape, when upon hailing a merchantman, her crew was reliably informed of the Shadow Leviathan's existence, and the ruin it had wrought. Eagerly drinking in this dark news, Malachi fiercely cautioned the captain against pursuing the beast, should it appear; in his fevered delusions, proclaiming the Leviathan to be the Wrath of the Almighty made flesh; the very instrument of divine judgment. But when, some months later, the Leviathan was distinctly spied from the rigging, Silas, the first mate, burned with ambition to strike it; and the captain being somewhat inclined to grant him the chance, despite all the prophet’s condemnations and dire omens, Silas succeeded in rallying six men to lower his boat. With them he launched forth; and, after an exhausting pursuit, and several dangerous, failed approaches, he at last managed to secure one line fast.
Meanwhile, Malachi, climbing to the highest cross-trees, was waving his arms in frenzied motions, and screaming predictions of imminent ruin upon the wicked attackers of his idol. Now, while Silas, the mate, stood tall in the prow, and with all the reckless vigor of his kind was shouting his fierce cries at the beast, and maneuvering to strike with his raised lance, behold! a massive dark shape breached from the depths; by its sudden, sweeping displacement, temporarily pulling the breath from the lungs of the rowers. In the next instant, the doomed mate, so brimming with violent energy, was launched bodily into the sky, and tracing a high curve in his fall, plunged into the waves some forty yards away. Not a plank of the craft was splintered, nor a hair on any rower's head harmed; but the mate sank forever.
It is well to note here, that of the lethal accidents in the deep-sea fisheries, this specific tragedy is perhaps almost as frequent as any. Sometimes, nothing is damaged save the man who is thus extinguished; oftener the boat’s stem is shattered, or the bracing-plank, against which the harpooneer leans, is wrenched away and falls with the corpse. But most peculiar of all is the circumstance, that in more instances than one, when the body has been retrieved, not a single bruise or mark of violence is visible; the man being entirely lifeless.
The entire disaster, with the plummeting figure of Silas, was clearly witnessed from the deck. Raising a piercing shriek—"The Seventh Seal! The Seal is broken!" Malachi commanded the terrified crew to abandon the further hunting of the beast. This horrific event cloaked the prophet with increased authority; because his superstitious followers believed that he had exactly fore-announced it, instead of merely shouting a broad prophecy, which any madman might have done, and so happened to strike one of many marks in the vast unpredictability of the sea. He became a nameless terror aboard the ship.
Thorne having concluded his narration, Elias put such inquiries to him, that the visiting captain could not forbear asking whether he intended to chase the Shadow Leviathan, should opportunity offer.
To which Elias answered—"Aye."
Straightway, then, Malachi leapt up once more, glaring fiercely at the scarred captain, and passionately cried, with a downward pointed finger—"Think, think of the doomed mate—dead, and rotting below!—beware the sinner's grave!"
Elias impassively turned his head; then said to Thorne, "Captain, I am just reminded of my dispatch-box; there is a parcel for one of thy crew, if my memory serves. Mr. Vance, fetch the mail."
Every voyaging vessel takes out a goodly number of letters for various ships, whose delivery to the intended hands relies upon the mere chance of encountering them in the four oceans.

"What possible benefit can there be in alienating Mr. Hawthorne? You will certainly not elevate yourself in his friend's esteem by such a public display."
Her quiet admonitions, however, carried no weight. Her mother continued to broadcast her ambitions in the same penetrating volume. Eleanor felt her cheeks burn with successive waves of humiliation. She could not resist casting anxious glances toward Mr. Hawthorne, though every look confirmed her worst fears; for while he did not constantly stare at her mother, Eleanor was certain his focus was entirely captured by the spectacle. The disposition of his features shifted slowly from disdainful shock to a mask of absolute, chilling stoicism. Eventually, however, Mrs. Stanton had no more breath to expend; and Lady Bertram, who had spent the last hour stifling yawns over the recital of triumphs she saw no likelihood of ever sharing, was abandoned to the consolations of roast pheasant and wine. Eleanor began to revive.
But her reprieve was remarkably short; for the moment supper concluded, musical entertainment was proposed, and she suffered the mortification of watching Beatrice, needing barely a whisper of encouragement, preparing to command the room. Through desperate glares and silent pleas did Eleanor attempt to halt this display of eager compliance—but without success; Beatrice would not interpret them; the chance to demonstrate her accomplishments was too intoxicating, and she commenced her ballad. Eleanor’s eyes were locked on her, consumed by acute distress; she tracked the progression of the verses with an impatience that was bitterly rewarded at their conclusion; for Beatrice, interpreting the polite thanks of the table as a hope that she might be prevailed upon to sing again, paused only for half a minute before launching into a second melody. Beatrice’s abilities were completely inadequate for such an exhibition; her pitch was wavering, and her delivery hopelessly affected. Eleanor was in agony. She glanced at Charlotte to gauge how she bore it; but Charlotte was peacefully absorbed in conversation with Mr. Alden. She looked at his two sisters, and saw them exchanging smirks of derision, and at Hawthorne, who maintained his impenetrable granite facade. She looked at her father to entreat his interference, lest Beatrice should be screeching all night. He comprehended the hint, and, the instant Beatrice concluded her second effort, he announced aloud,—
"That will do perfectly well, my dear. You have entertained us long enough. Allow the other young women an opportunity to exhibit."
Beatrice, though attempting to feign deafness to the slight, was visibly flustered; and Eleanor, pitying her sister, and regretting her father's tactless intervention, feared her anxiety had only worsened the situation. Others in the party were now addressed.
"If Providence," declared Mr. Whitfield, "had blessed me with a singing voice, I should be exceedingly glad, I assure you, to favor the company with a hymn; for I regard musical exertion as a perfectly innocent pastime, and entirely suitable for a man of the cloth. I do not suggest, naturally, that we are justified in dedicating excessive hours to the pursuit, for there are certainly graver responsibilities to attend to. The vicar of a parish carries a heavy burden. Primarily, he must negotiate his tithes in a manner profitable to himself and not offensive to his patron. He must compose his own sermons; and the hours that remain will not be too much for his pastoral duties, and the improvement of his parsonage, which he cannot be excused from making as comfortable as possible. And I do not consider it of light importance that he exhibit an attentive and subservient demeanor to everyone, most particularly to those to whom he owes his preferment. I cannot acquit him of that duty; nor could I respect a man who should neglect an occasion to broadcast his reverence toward anyone connected to the patron's noble line." And with a sweeping bow toward Mr. Hawthorne, he concluded his speech, which had been delivered so loudly as to be heard by half the parlor.
Many gaped—many smiled; but nobody looked more entertained than Mr. Stanton himself, while his wife earnestly praised Mr. Whitfield for having spoken so sensibly, and observed, in a half-whisper to Lady Bertram, that he was a staggeringly clever, good kind of young gentleman. To Eleanor, it appeared that had her relatives made a formal pact to expose themselves to the greatest possible degree during the evening, they could not have played their parts with more spirit, or finer success; and she considered it a profound mercy for Mr. Alden and her sister that much of the exhibition had escaped his notice, and that his mild feelings were not of a sort to be deeply distressed by the folly he was forced to witness.

When the grandfather clock chimed four, Eleanor recognized that her visit must conclude, and expressed her need to depart with obvious reluctance. Miss Harrington proposed the use of the carriage, and Eleanor required only a fraction of encouragement to accept it, when Margaret exhibited such distress at the prospect of separation that Miss Harrington was compelled to transform the offer of transport into a formal invitation to remain at Rosewood for the time being. Eleanor accepted with profound gratitude, and a footman was immediately dispatched to Willowbourne, to inform her parents of the arrangement and to procure a trunk of necessary garments.
CHAPTER IX.
At half-past five the ladies withdrew to change their attire, and shortly after seven Eleanor was summoned to the dining room. To the polite inquiries which immediately surrounded her, among which she could easily distinguish the far more genuine concern of Mr. Charles Harrington, she was unable to provide a comforting reply. Margaret was in no way improved. The two sisters, upon receiving this news, declared repeatedly how devastated they were, how dreadful a severe fever could be, and how vehemently they detested falling ill themselves; and having said this, they dismissed the subject from their minds: and their complete apathy toward Margaret, when she was not directly in their sight, revived all of Eleanor's initial antipathy toward them. Their brother, indeed, was the singular member of the household whom she could view with any charity. His distress for Margaret was unmistakable, and his polite efforts toward Eleanor were entirely soothing; they served to mitigate her persistent feeling that she was an unwanted interloper among the rest of the company. She received little attention from anyone but him. Miss Harrington was entirely absorbed by Mr. Sterling, and her sister Augusta scarcely less devoted to the same pursuit; and as for Augusta’s husband, Mr. Vance, beside whom Eleanor was seated, he was a sluggish gentleman whose sole passions were roasted meats and billiards, who, upon observing that she preferred a simple broth to the rich ragout, found her entirely unworthy of conversation.
Once the meal was concluded, she hastened immediately back to Margaret's chamber, and Miss Harrington commenced a thorough critique of her the moment the door was shut. Her deportment was judged to be exceedingly coarse—a combination of arrogance and lack of polish: she possessed no wit, no elegance, no refinement, no charm. Augusta was of the exact same mind, and supplemented the attack,—
“She has absolutely nothing to recommend her, save a capacity for vigorous hiking. I shall not soon recover from her arrival this afternoon. She looked practically feral.”
“She truly did, Augusta. I was forced to look away to hide my shock. What utter nonsense to march over here at all! Why must she be tramping across the fields, merely because her sister has caught a chill? Her hair was so dishevelled, so utterly chaotic!”
“Yes, and her pelisse; I pray you noticed her pelisse, splattered with mire up to the calves, I am perfectly certain, and the cloak she wore attempting to conceal it entirely failing in its duty.”
“Your observation may be precise, Augusta,” remarked Charles; “but I confess I saw none of it. I thought Miss Eleanor looked exceptionally vibrant when she entered the parlor today. The mud on her hem was entirely lost on me.”
“You must have noted it, Mr. Sterling, I am convinced,” stated Miss Harrington; “and I am quite certain you would never desire your sister to make such a public spectacle.”
“Undoubtedly not.”
“To march two or three miles, or whatever the distance might be, up to her shins in the muck, and without a chaperone, completely unaccompanied! What on earth could be her justification? To me, it demonstrates a dreadful sort of arrogant rebellion, a most vulgar, provincial disregard for propriety.”
“It demonstrates a devotion to her sister that is highly commendable,” Charles countered.
“I deeply fear, Mr. Sterling,” murmured Miss Harrington in a lowered voice, “that this wild escapade has somewhat lessened your appreciation for her brilliant eyes.”
“Not in the slightest,” he answered: “they were only made brighter by the brisk air.”
A brief silence succeeded this remark, before Augusta resumed the assault,—
“I harbor a profound fondness for Margaret,—she is genuinely a delightful creature,—and I desire nothing more than to see her make a respectable match. But with such parents, and such appallingly low relations, I fear there is zero probability of it.”
“Did I not hear you mention that their uncle is a mere clerk in the village?”
“Indeed; and there is another who trades in textiles somewhere near Cheapside.”
“How marvelous,” added her sister; and the two women shared a long and hearty laugh.

LEONORA. I beg you, tell me who it is at once, and speak swiftly. I wish you suffered from a stutter, so that you might force this secret man out of your mouth the way water escapes a narrow jug—either a great flood all at once, or nothing at all. I beg you, unseal your lips so I may drink down your news.
JULIA. Careful, or you might swallow a man whole.
LEONORA. Is he a proper creature? What kind of man is he? Is his head fit for a crown, or his chin fit for a shave?
JULIA. Well, he has but a shadow of a beard.
LEONORA. Why, time will grant him more if he is patient. I will forgive his lack of a beard if you do not delay telling me the owner of the chin.
JULIA. It is young Sebastian, who knocked the champion from his horse and stole your heart in the very same afternoon.
LEONORA. Stop, may the devil take your teasing! Speak to me with a serious face and a true heart.
JULIA. Upon my honor, cousin, it is he.
LEONORA. Sebastian?
JULIA. Sebastian.
LEONORA. Heavens above, what am I to do dressed in these stable-boy clothes? What was he doing when you spied him? What did he say? How did he appear? What was he wearing? Why is he in this province? Did he inquire after me? Where is he staying? How did you take your leave of him? And when will you cross his path again? Answer me in a single breath.
JULIA. You must find me a dragon’s mouth first. The answer is too massive for any ordinary mortal's jaw. To simply say yes or no to your demands would be longer than reciting a holy text.
LEONORA. But does he realize I am hiding in this valley, wearing a man’s coat? Does he look as glorious as he did the day of the joust?
JULIA. It is easier to count the grains of sand on the shore than to satisfy the curiosity of a woman in love. But take a small sample of my discovery, and listen closely. I found him resting beneath a willow, like a fallen branch.
LEONORA. It must be a sacred tree to drop such magnificent timber.
JULIA. Give me the floor, good madam.
LEONORA. Proceed.
JULIA. There he lay, stretched out across the grass like a weary soldier.
LEONORA. A pitiful sight, perhaps, but one that surely blessed the earth beneath him.
JULIA. Call a halt to your tongue, I beg you. It runs wildly off the path. He was dressed like a traveling merchant.
LEONORA. Oh, tragic! He means to purchase my soul.
JULIA. I would like to finish my tale without a chorus. You throw me entirely off my rhythm.
LEONORA. Do you forget I am a woman? When a thought strikes me, I must voice it. Sweet cousin, continue.
Enter Sebastian and Cassius.
JULIA. Now you have silenced me. Look softly, is that not him approaching?
LEONORA. It is! Step into the shadows, and let us watch.
[Leonora and Julia step aside.]
CASSIUS. I thank you for walking with me, but truthfully, I would have much preferred my own solitude.
SEBASTIAN. I feel exactly the same, but for the sake of politeness, I will thank you for your companionship.
CASSIUS. Heaven keep you; let us arrange to meet as infrequently as possible.
SEBASTIAN. I sincerely hope we might become absolute strangers.
CASSIUS. I ask that you stop ruining the tavern walls by carving your tragic love poems into the wood.
SEBASTIAN. I ask that you stop ruining my poems by reading them with such a sour voice.
CASSIUS. Leonora is the name of your beloved?
SEBASTIAN. Yes, exactly.
CASSIUS. I find it a deeply unpleasant name.
SEBASTIAN. Nobody was thinking of your preferences on the day she was baptized.
CASSIUS. What height is she?
SEBASTIAN. Exactly as high as the heavens.
CASSIUS. You are full of rehearsed retorts. Have you been spending time with street performers, memorizing their cheap jokes?
SEBASTIAN. Hardly; but I answer you with the same painted-on wit that you use to ask your tiresome questions.
CASSIUS. You have a quick tongue. I imagine it was forged from quicksilver. Will you sit by the river with me? We can complain together about the wretchedness of the world and our own miserable lives.
SEBASTIAN. I refuse to criticize any living, breathing soul in this world except for myself, since I know my own flaws best.
CASSIUS. The most glaring flaw you possess is being in love.
SEBASTIAN. It is a flaw I would never exchange for your greatest virtue. I am thoroughly tired of you.
CASSIUS. I swear to you, I was wandering about looking for a fool when I bumped into you.
SEBASTIAN. He drowned in the river. Just look into the water, and you will spot him.
CASSIUS. If I look in the water, I will only see my own reflection.
SEBASTIAN. Which I consider to be either a fool or a total zero.

FALLOW. That is bread and butter to me now. I have seen the great mastiff unleashed a dozen times, and held him by the leather collar. But I swear, the ladies wept and shrieked so loudly it was deafening. Truly, women cannot stomach such beasts; they are rough, ugly brutes.
Enter Sterling.
STERLING. Come along, good Master Fallow, come. We wait upon your presence.
FALLOW. I have no appetite, I thank you, sir.
STERLING. By the saints, you shall not refuse us, sir! Come along.
FALLOW. Nay, I pray you, walk ahead.
STERLING. Come on, sir.
FALLOW. Mistress Clara, you must proceed first.
CLARA. Not I, sir; I pray you, continue.
FALLOW. Truly, I will not walk before you; truly, no! I will not offer you such an insult.
CLARA. I insist, sir.
FALLOW. I would rather be considered rude than a nuisance. You do yourself an injustice, truly, no!
[Exeunt.]
SCENE II. The same
Enter Father Clement and Tobias.
CLEMENT. Go your way, and ask at the apothecary’s shop for the proper path. And there resides one Dame Prattle, who serves as his housekeeper, or his nursemaid, or his baker, or his laundress, folding and scrubbing.
TOBIAS. Very well, sir.
CLEMENT. Nay, there is more. Deliver her this parchment. For she is a woman holding great sway with Mistress Clara Sterling; and the letter is to command and beg her to advance your master’s suit to Mistress Clara. I pray you, hurry. I must finish my supper; there are roasted apples and wine yet to come.
[Exeunt.]
SCENE III. A room in the Boar's Head Tavern
Enter Captain Stone, Innkeeper, Tankard, Slicer, and Flint.
STONE. My good host of the Boar's Head!
INNKEEPER. What says my stout general? Speak with wit and wisdom.
STONE. Truly, mine host, I must dismiss some of my retinue.
INNKEEPER. Cast them off, brave Samson; discharge them. Let them wander; away, away.
STONE. My lodgings cost me five pounds a week.
INNKEEPER. Thou art a king—a sultan, a czar, a ruler. I will employ Tankard. He shall pour the ale, he shall draw the taps. Do I speak well, mighty Hector?
STONE. Do so, my excellent host.
INNKEEPER. The word is given, let him follow.—Let me see you pour and skim. I am a man of my word, follow.
[Exit Innkeeper.]
STONE. Tankard, go with him. A barkeep is a noble calling. An old coat makes a fine tavern-rag; a stale rogue makes a fresh pourer. Go, farewell.
TANKARD. It is the very life I have dreamed of. I shall prosper.
FLINT. Oh, miserable vagabond, wilt thou stoop to a wooden spout?
[Exit Tankard.]
SLICER. He was conceived in a wine barrel. Is the jest not amusing?
STONE. I am relieved to be rid of that clumsy thief. His pickpocketing was too obvious. His thievery was like a lame dancer, entirely out of step.
SLICER. The proper jest is to swipe the coin on the downbeat.
FLINT. "Acquire," the learned men say. "Steal?" Bah! A curse upon the word!
STONE. Well, gentlemen, my purse is completely empty.
FLINT. Why, then, let hunger follow.
STONE. There is no other way, I must cheat, I must scheme.
FLINT. Hungry wolves must feed.
STONE. Which of you is acquainted with Mercer of this village?
FLINT. I know the fellow; his vaults are heavy with gold.
STONE. My loyal knaves, I shall reveal my plot to you.
FLINT. A mile wide, and more.
STONE. No jests now, Flint. Truly, I am a mile wide in the belly, but I have no time for belly-laughs; I must find coin. In short, I intend to woo Mercer’s wife. I see an opening there. She chats, she smiles, she casts a welcoming eye. I can translate the language of her bold manners; and the loudest decree of her conduct, translated plainly, is "I belong to Captain Stone."
FLINT. He has read her mind and decoded her intent—from virtue into plain speech.
SLICER. The plot deepens. Will the trick succeed?
STONE. Now, rumor holds that she controls every penny of her husband’s wealth. He possesses a mountain of gold coins.
FLINT. A mountain of demons awaits, and "Attack her, brave soldier," say I.
SLICER. The scheme ripens; it is excellent. Fetch me those coins.
STONE. I have drafted a letter to her here; and yet another to Sterling’s wife, who just today gave me a favorable look as well, surveying my figure with the most calculating glances. At one moment her gaze illuminated my sturdy boot, and at the next my majestic stomach.
FLINT. Thus the golden sun beams upon a heap of manure.
SLICER.

VALENTINE. So you would argue that a fool and his gold are never truly parted?
TRANIO. Verily, sir; for the gold only leaves his purse to buy a mirror, wherein he finds a greater fool.
VALENTINE. That is a hollow reason for a heavy purse's emptying.
TRANIO. Let me patch it, then: the mirror shatters, the gold is spent, and the fool is left with nothing but his own reflections, which are worthless to the world's end.
VALENTINE. A shattered conclusion, indeed. But soft, who comes toward us with such purpose?
Enter Isabella and Beatrice.
ISABELLA. Yes, Valentine, cast your eyes away and play the stranger.
Some newer affection claims your gentle looks.
I am no longer Isabella, nor your lawful bride.
There was a time when you would freely swear
That no music was so sweet as my voice,
No sight so fair as my approaching shadow,
No touch so warm as my hand in yours,
Unless I spoke, or smiled, or walked beside you.
How is it now, my lord, oh, how can it be,
That you are so divided from your own heart?
I call it your heart, though it is frozen to me,
Because we two, united in holy bonds,
Make up one single, indivisible soul.
Do not rend yourself away from my side;
For know this, my love: you might as well cast
A cup of wine into the roaring sea,
And try to scoop that selfsame vintage back
Without a drop of brine corrupting it,
As try to separate your life from mine.
How deeply would it wound your noble pride,
If you should hear that I had played you false?
That this chaste bed, kept wholly for your sake,
By some vile stranger's touch was made impure?
Would you not curse my name, and cast me out,
And hurl the title 'wife' back in my teeth,
And strip the silken garments from my back,
And shatter our gold ring upon the stones?
I know you would; therefore, execute your justice.
For I am tainted by a borrowed sin;
My honor is darkened by your lustful crimes;
For if we two are one, and you stray far,
I drink the bitter venom of your deeds,
Made foul by your contagious treachery.
Keep, then, your vows sacred to our true home,
Lest I live blackened, while you walk unblamed.
VALENTINE. Do you address this plea to me, fair lady?
I know you not. In Mantua I am but newly arrived,
As foreign to your streets as to your speech,
Which, though I weigh each syllable with care,
Defies my wits to grasp its true intent.
BEATRICE. Shame on you, brother, to alter so drastically.
When did you ever mock my sister thus?
She sent your man Tranio to bring you to the feast.
VALENTINE. By Tranio?
TRANIO. By me?
ISABELLA. By you; and you brought back this cruel report:
That he did strike you, and amidst his blows,
Swore he knew not my house, nor me as wife.
VALENTINE. Did you parley, sir, with this noblewoman?
What is the meaning of this secret pact?
TRANIO. I, sir? I never laid eyes on her till this moment.
VALENTINE. Rogue, you lie, for her exact complaints
You shouted at me in the market square.
TRANIO. I never spoke a word to her in all my days.
VALENTINE. How can she, then, address us by our names?
Unless she reads it by some dark enchantment.
ISABELLA. How poorly it suits your dignity and age
To plot so basely with your serving-man,
Encouraging his lies to vex my patience.
Though you do me wrong, I cannot cast you off,
But do not mock my grief with added scorn.
Come, I will bind my arm unto your sleeve.
You are the sturdy oak, my lord, I the clinging vine,
Whose fragile nature, bound to your great trunk,
Draws strength and life from your foundation:
If any pestilence takes you from my hold,
It is but worthless rot, or choking weeds,
Which, lacking the gardener's blade, infect
Your noble roots, and thrive on your decay.
VALENTINE. She speaks to me; I am the center of her tale.
Was I wedded to this woman in my sleep?
Or am I slumbering now, and dreaming this?
What madness plays upon our mortal senses?
Until I find the truth beneath this fog,
I will accept the illusion she presents.
BEATRICE. Tranio, run ahead and tell them to pour the wine.
TRANIO. O, fetch a priest! I cross myself against the devil.

“However highly I esteem them, I assure you my thoughts were entirely occupied by you.”
Beatrice found herself too overwhelmed by emotion to utter a single syllable. After a brief hesitation, her companion continued, “You possess too much goodness to keep me in suspense. If your disposition remains unchanged from last November, I beg you to declare it. My devotion and intentions are entirely resolute; but a single word of refusal from you will conclude this matter forever.”
Beatrice, acutely aware of the extraordinary tension and vulnerability of his position, compelled herself to reply; and swiftly, though with a faltering voice, conveyed to him that her feelings had undergone so profound a transformation since the time he referenced, that she accepted his present declarations with the utmost gratitude and joy. The sheer elation this answer inspired in him was unlike anything he had ever experienced; and he articulated his happiness with all the eloquence and fervor that a gentleman deeply in love might muster. Had Beatrice possessed the courage to meet his gaze, she would have witnessed how handsomely the glow of pure joy illuminated his features: but though she dared not look, she could attend to his words; and he spoke of an affection that, by demonstrating her absolute importance to his happiness, rendered his devotion more precious with every passing moment.
They continued their stroll without the slightest awareness of their destination. Their minds were too crowded with reflections, emotions, and confessions to spare a glance for their surroundings. She soon discovered that their present felicity was entirely due to the interference of his overbearing aunt, Lady Westbourne, who had visited him upon her return to town, detailing her journey to the country, its purpose, and the precise nature of her confrontation with Beatrice. The lady had emphasized every word of Beatrice’s responses, which, in her Ladyship’s mind, perfectly illustrated her insolence and audacity, fully expecting that this report would persuade her nephew to abandon his pursuit. But, fortunately for his Ladyship’s intended victim, the result was entirely the reverse.
“It gave me reason to hope,” he explained, “as I had scarcely dared to hope for months. I understood your character well enough to know that, had you been absolutely and irrevocably set against me, you would have confessed it to Lady Westbourne without the slightest hesitation.”
Beatrice blushed and smiled as she answered, “Indeed, you are well acquainted with my frankness to suppose me capable of that. After rebuking you so terribly to your face, I could hardly shrink from offending your relations.”
“But what did you say of me that I had not fully earned? For although your charges were drawn from false impressions, my demeanor toward you at that time warranted the sharpest condemnation. It was inexcusable. I shudder to recall it.”
“Let us not argue over who bears the greater fault for that dreadful evening,” said Beatrice. “Neither of our actions, if subjected to scrutiny, would be found faultless; but since then, I trust we have both improved in our manners.”
“I cannot forgive myself so readily. The memory of my words, my arrogance, and my posture during that entire exchange has been a source of unutterable torment to me. Your reprimand, so perfectly deserved, will never leave me: ‘Had you conducted yourself as a true gentleman.’ Those were your exact words. You cannot possibly fathom how they have haunted me; though it required some time, I must admit, before my pride would acknowledge their truth.”
“I was certainly not anticipating that they would leave such a lasting mark. I never imagined you would feel them so acutely.”
“I can well believe it. You believed me entirely devoid of any honorable sentiment, I am certain. I shall never forget the expression on your face when you declared that no form of address could have ever persuaded you to accept my hand.”
“Oh, I beg you, do not repeat my past foolishness. Those memories are too painful to bear. I promise you I have long repented of that speech.”
Mr. Carlisle then brought up the subject of his letter. “Did it,” he asked, “did it quickly alter your opinion of my character? Did you grant any truth to its claims upon your first reading?”
She proceeded to describe the profound effect it had upon her, and how, by slow degrees, all her former resentments had been washed away.

"But their entire approach was brazen and sloppy."
"If they possessed hard evidence—I mean, indisputable proof—or even a solid foundation for their suspicions, they would have masked their intentions to lure you in further (they would have obtained a warrant and searched your room by now). But they have nothing, not a single thread. It is all smoke—all conjecture. Just a hovering theory. So they attempt to rattle you with their brazenness. And perhaps, he was frustrated by his lack of evidence, and let it slip in his anger—or perhaps he is executing a larger strategy... he appears to be a cunning fellow. Perhaps he intended to unnerve you by feigning omnipotence. They operate on a distinct psychological plane, my friend. But it is nauseating to dissect it. Enough!"
"And it is degrading, utterly degrading! I hear you. But... since we are laying our cards on the table (and it is a relief that we finally are—I am truly glad), I will confess now that I perceived this undercurrent in them days ago. Merely a whisper, of course—an implication—but why even an implication? How possess they the audacity? What grounds do they stand upon? If you only knew the rage that has consumed me. Just imagine! Simply because an impoverished clerk, driven mad by hunger and nervous exhaustion, standing on the precipice of a violent fever (mark that well), sensitive, proud, isolated, who has not exchanged a word with a living soul for a quarter of a year, wearing a threadbare coat and tattered shoes, is forced to endure the mockery of petty officials; and then a sudden demand for rent shoved in his face, the landlord's threats, the smell of fresh varnish, the stifling heat of the precinct, a mob of strangers, the sudden chatter about the robbery of a merchant he had visited that very morning, and all this on an empty stomach—he might naturally succumb to a swoon!
