And that, that single moment of weakness is the entirety of their case! Curse them! I comprehend how maddening it is, but if I were you, Leonid, I would mock them, or better yet, spit right in their smug faces, and spit a hundred times over. I would strike back at them, swiftly and decisively, and put a stop to it. Curse them! Do not let them break your spirit. It is an outrage!"
"He actually frames it quite convincingly," Leonid thought. "Curse them? But the summons for tomorrow?" he muttered with dark resentment. "Am I truly forced to justify myself to them again? I am already disgusted that I lowered myself to exchange pleasantries with that rat Sokolov yesterday in the tavern...."
"To hell with it! I shall go to Lebedev myself. I will wring the truth out of him, as an old acquaintance: he must lay out his entire hand! And as for Sokolov..."
"He finally sees him for what he is!" thought Leonid.
"Wait!" cried Viktor, grabbing him by the arm. "Wait! You miscalculated. I have worked it out. You are completely wrong! How could that be a snare? You claim the question about the shattered mirror was a snare. But if you had committed the act, would you have admitted to seeing the broken glass... and the blood? On the contrary, you would have claimed ignorance, even if you had stared right at it. Who would deliberately implicate himself?"
"If I had committed the act, I would undoubtedly have stated that I had seen the glass and the room," Leonid replied, with a heavy, visible revulsion. "But why testify against your own interests?"
"Because only simpletons, or the most frightened amateurs, deny every detail outright during an interrogation. If a man possesses even a fraction of intellect and worldly experience, he will inevitably admit to all the physical realities that cannot be disproven, but he will invent alternative justifications for them, he will introduce some clever, unexpected angle, that will alter their meaning and cast them in an innocent light. Lebedev would naturally calculate that I should respond exactly so, and claim I had seen the mirror to project an aura of honesty, and then weave some excuse."
"But he would have informed you immediately that the mirror was not broken until Tuesday evening, and that therefore you must have been present at the exact hour of the crime. And in that manner, he would have trapped you on a technicality."
"Precisely, that is what he was anticipating, that I would lack the time to deliberate, and would rush to offer the most plausible-sounding defense, and thus forget that the mirror had been perfectly whole until Tuesday."
"But how could a man forget such a thing?"
"It is the easiest thing in the world."

I enjoyed rare moments of genuine joy: perhaps once every seven days. Initially, I anticipated a grueling struggle to convince you to permit me to honor my promise to my cousin; for I had sworn to return the following day, once we parted from him. However, as you remained confined to your quarters the next morning, I avoided that conflict entirely. As the groom, Silas, was busy securing the gate to the estate that afternoon, I managed to secure the key, and explained to him how my cousin yearned for my company because he was infirm and unable to travel to the manor. I told him how my father would surely forbid the excursion, and then we struck a bargain regarding the chestnut mare. Silas is a man of letters, and intends to seek employment elsewhere to settle down; therefore, he proposed that if I supplied him with volumes from the library, he would assist me. I preferred to gift him my own collection, which seemed to satisfy him more completely.
"During my subsequent visit, my cousin appeared remarkably cheerful; and Martha, their housekeeper, tidied a chamber and built a roaring fire for us. She noted that as the master was away at a sermon and the stable-hand was out with his hounds—plundering our private grounds for game, I later discovered—we were free to amuse ourselves. She served us mulled cider and cakes, appearing quite kind; my cousin rested in the velvet chair, while I took the low stool by the hearth, and we conversed with such levity, finding endless subjects to explore; we mapped out our travels and our ambitions for the coming season. I shall not recount the details, for you would deem them trivial.
"On one occasion, however, we bordered on a dispute. He argued that the most blissful way to pass a sweltering August afternoon was to recline from dawn until dusk on a patch of wildflowers in the center of the heath, with the insects droning drowsily among the petals, the skylarks calling from the heights, and the azure sky and searing sun fixed and motionless. That was his absolute vision of paradise. My own was to swing in a swaying oak, with a brisk northerly breeze stirring the leaves, and shimmering clouds racing across the firmament; with not only skylarks, but thrushes, and finches, and nightingales erupting in a chorus of song on every side. I wanted the wild hills in the distance, falling into shadowy valleys; but near me, vast meadows rippling like emerald tides in the gale, with forests and rushing streams—the entire earth awake and vibrant with delight. He desired to remain in a state of tranquil stillness; I desired for everything to glitter and leap in a triumphant festival. I remarked that his paradise would be half-dormant; he retorted that mine would be chaotic. I argued I would drift into slumber in his; he insisted he could not catch his breath in mine, and grew quite irritable. Eventually, we resolved to experience both, once the season turned; and then we embraced and reconciled.
"After remaining quiet for an hour, I surveyed the grand hall with its polished, barren floor, and mused on how delightful it would be to run about if we shifted the furniture; I implored him to summon Martha to aid us, and suggested a round of tag—the kind we used to play, Ellen. He refused; he saw no merit in such activity; but he agreed to play with a pair of hoops we discovered in a trunk, nestled among a pile of forgotten playthings, wooden blocks, and battledores. One was engraved with a 'C', the other with an 'E'. I desired the 'C', as it represented my name, and the 'E' might signify Earnshaw, the cousin's surname; but the wood was rotting on the 'E', and he disliked it. I defeated him repeatedly; he grew sullen once more, suffered a fit of coughing, and retreated to his chair. That evening, though, he easily regained his cheer; he was enthralled by a few soft melodies—those same songs you taught me, Ellen; and when the time arrived for my departure, he implored me to return the following twilight; which I vowed to do. My mare and I galloped back, buoyant as the breeze; and I drifted into dreams of the Heights and my beloved, gentle cousin, until the dawn."

EDMUND. I possess no less claim to our father's blood than you, though I grant that age has placed you before me.
ALARIC. What insolence is this?
EDMUND. Brother, do not mistake firmness for insolence.
ALARIC. Will you challenge me in my own house?
EDMUND. I challenge only injustice. I am the son of Sir Geoffrey, as surely as you are. Whoever speaks dishonourably of his sons condemns the father who raised them.
MARTIN. [Stepping forward.] Good masters, remember your father's memory. Let peace stand between you.
ALARIC. Release me.
EDMUND. Not until you hear me. Our father left instructions that I should be brought up according to my station. Instead, I have been neglected, denied learning and exercise, while you enjoyed every advantage. Yet I feel my father's spirit within me, and I will not endure this treatment longer. Give me the education proper to my birth, or grant me the inheritance left in my name. With it I shall seek my own fortune.
ALARIC. And when that money is gone, what then? Will you wander the roads begging? Very well. You shall have something. Now leave me.
EDMUND. I ask only what is mine by right.
ALARIC. Away with him. And take this old servant too.
MARTIN. So "old servant" is all I have earned? My years were spent faithfully in this house. Your father would never have spoken so coldly.
Exeunt Edmund and Martin.
ALARIC. So. The boy grows bold. I must cure that ambition before it spreads further. Ho there! Stephen!
Enter Stephen.
STEPHEN. My lord?
ALARIC. Was not Victor, the duke's champion, seeking audience with me?
STEPHEN. He waits at the gate and asks leave to enter.
ALARIC. Admit him.
Exit Stephen.
The timing serves me well. Tomorrow's contest may solve more than one problem.
Enter Victor.
VICTOR. Good day, my lord.
ALARIC. Welcome, Victor. What news travels from court?
VICTOR. Little beyond what everyone already knows. The elder duke remains in exile, and those loyal to him have followed him into the northern woods. Their estates now enrich the younger duke.
ALARIC. And his daughter? Did she accompany him?
VICTOR. No. The duke's niece would not be separated from her cousin. Their affection is so strong that neither would remain without the other. Both ladies continue at court and are greatly admired.
ALARIC. Where does the exiled duke now reside?
VICTOR. In the Greenwood Forest, they say. Many gentlemen join him there. They hunt, feast, and pass their days with remarkable freedom.
ALARIC. You compete tomorrow before the duke?
VICTOR. I do. That is partly why I came. I have learned that your younger brother intends to enter the contest against me under disguise.
ALARIC. Indeed?
VICTOR. He is young, and I would regret harming him. Yet in a public match I cannot spare him without dishonouring myself. If he steps into the ring, he risks injury. I thought it proper to warn you.
ALARIC. Your concern does you credit. I have already attempted to dissuade him, but he refuses all advice. He is stubborn beyond reason.
VICTOR. Then whatever follows will be of his own choosing.
ALARIC. So it will.

SCENE. A market square before the city gates.
Alarums and shouting within. Enter ROWE, a rebel captain, with his followers.
ROWE. Come, friends, the bell hath rung for liberty. Why stand ye idle? Some to the guildhall, and some unto the custom house. Let no account-books survive this day. Down with the ledgers that have weighed men's backs more heavily than iron.
MARTIN. Captain, I crave a favor of your grace.
ROWE. Grace? Thou hast made me nobler with that word than any king could do. Speak thy desire.
MARTIN. Only that no tax be laid save by the consent of those that pay it.
POTTER. [Aside.] Then shall the treasury be as lean as a winter hound.
COOPER. [Aside.] Ay, and the clerks will learn to reckon upon their fingers.
ROWE. It is resolved. Henceforth no parchment shall command where honest speech may suffice. Let every grievance be heard in the open square.
POTTER. Then we shall have long judgments, for every man loves the sound of his own complaint.
ROWE. Better that than secret decrees hatched behind locked doors. From this hour, the city belongs to its people.
Enter a MESSENGER in haste.
MESSENGER. Captain, a capture! A capture! Here is Lord Varney, chief councillor to the Regent, who gathered the levies and doubled the tolls upon the roads.
Enter HUGH with LORD VARNEY guarded.
ROWE. Well found. Bring him forth. My lord, you stand where rank affords no shelter. What answer make you to those whose harvests paid for your feasts? What say you to widows whose sons were taken for wars they never understood?
VARNEY. I shall answer what is true, if truth be permitted here.
ROWE. Truth? We have heard much of truth from men in velvet. Yet the granaries emptied while your household prospered. The roads decayed while new statues rose before the palace. The poor were taught patience, and the rich exemption.
HUGH. Aye, and every year another levy.
MARTIN. And another promise.
ROWE. It shall be proved that you surrounded yourself with scholars and secretaries who measured the kingdom by columns of figures and knew not the faces of the people whose lives those figures marked. You built offices, commissions, and courts. You sent summonses to men who could scarce write their names. When they failed to answer in the language of lawyers, they were fined. When they could not pay, they were imprisoned.
VARNEY. The law must be administered.
COOPER. Administered? Like physic to a healthy man till he dies of the cure.
ROWE. Tell us, my lord, how many days have you labored with your own hands?
VARNEY. Few, for my office required another service.
ROWE. Another service indeed. Your horse wears silver trappings while laborers patch their boots with rope.
MARTIN. And some go barefoot altogether.
VARNEY. Good people, hear me. You judge by appearances and by rumors sharpened in the telling. The tolls maintained bridges. The levies defended borders. The courts restrained thieves and protected contracts. A kingdom is not governed by wishes but by difficult choices.
ROWE. Fine words come easily to practiced tongues.
VARNEY. Then weigh deeds instead. When famine threatened the northern counties, whose stores were opened? Mine. When floods swept the valley farms, whose treasury paid for repairs? Mine. When merchants sought to abandon the harbor, who persuaded them to remain? I did, for the benefit of the city and its trade.
HUGH. He speaks smoothly.
MARTIN. Smoothly enough to oil a gallows rope.
VARNEY. If I have erred, I am a man and not a saint. Yet consider what follows if every record burns and every magistrate flees. Debts will be disputed, boundaries forgotten, inheritances contested. Neighbors will quarrel where judges once decided. The strongest voice will become the law.
ROWE. And what is the law now, but the strongest voice made permanent?
VARNEY. No. The law, imperfect though it be, is a shield placed between power and vengeance. Break it utterly, and you may cheer today, but tomorrow you will search among the ruins for what protected you.
A murmur passes through the crowd.
POTTER. There is reason in some part of what he says.
COOPER. And guilt in another.
ROWE. Silence. My lord, you plead well for one whose cause stands upon such uncertain ground.
VARNEY. I plead not for myself alone. I plead for the city. Anger may overthrow a government in an afternoon; wisdom is required to build another.
ROWE. We shall determine whether wisdom or anger rules this day. Guard him well. The people shall hear all charges before sentence is pronounced.
HUGH. Come, my lord.
VARNEY. I am ready. May judgment be fairer than rumor.

SCENE I. A Physician’s Shop.
Enter ADRIAN.
ADRIAN.
The night grows old, yet sleep comes not to me.
As through the poorer quarter of the town
I pass'd but now, my eye did light upon
A dwelling mean enough to shelter want.
There lives, thought I, a man whom hunger's hand
Hath taught obedience unto desperate needs.
If ever grief should drive a soul to seek
Some dangerous remedy forbidden by law,
That man would furnish it. And now, alas,
The thought returns to serve my present turn.
This should be the place. The shutters are drawn close.
Ho there! Good physician!
Enter PHYSICIAN.
PHYSICIAN.
Who calls at such an hour?
ADRIAN.
Come nearer, friend.
I know thy state and will not mock thy poverty.
Here is a purse. Within it lies enough
To ease thy household for a month or more.
Give me a draught that works both swift and sure.
PHYSICIAN.
You ask a fearful thing. The city's law
Condemns the seller and the buyer both.
ADRIAN.
The city's law? What comfort hath it brought thee?
I see want written plainly in thy face.
Your garments plead against your fortune's health.
The law preserves the rich who fashioned it;
It leaves the poor to wrestle with the cold.
PHYSICIAN.
Yet still it is the law.
ADRIAN.
And still thou starv'st.
Take this reward and make an end of doubt.
PHYSICIAN.
My conscience struggles, sir.
ADRIAN.
Let hunger judge.
PHYSICIAN.
Then hear me. I possess a certain powder.
Mix it with wine, and ere an hour has pass'd
The strongest heart shall cease its beating.
ADRIAN.
Give it me.
PHYSICIAN.
Take it, then. Heaven forgive us both.
ADRIAN.
There is thy gold. Strange metal, yellow gold,
How many crimes are hidden in thy shine.
More lives are ruin'd by the thirst of thee
Than by the deadliest mixtures ever brewed.
Farewell. Spend wisely what necessity
Hath wrung from me and purchased from thy hand.
Exit ADRIAN.
PHYSICIAN.
Would that my need had been less great tonight.
Exit.
SCENE II. A Scholar’s Chamber.
Enter BROTHER MATTHIAS with a letter.
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
Brother Sebastian! Ho!
Enter BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
You are return'd at last. What news is brought?
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
Ill fortune crossed me. Seeking out a guide
To bear this letter to the northern road,
I came upon a village under watch.
A fever had arisen in the place.
The magistrates, fearing the spread of death,
Forbade all travel through the guarded streets
And held us there three days against our will.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
Then was the message never carried forth?
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
It was not. Here it is, seal'd as before.
No messenger would take it from my hand;
Such terror had the sickness bred in them.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
This is unhappy news. That letter bore
Instructions of great weight and consequence.
Its failure may produce a grievous end.
Go quickly now. Bring me a lantern, friend,
And tools to force a rusted chapel door.
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
I shall return with speed.
Exit.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
The hours run fast.
Before the dawn, the maiden hidden there
Will wake and find herself alone and fearful.
Had Adrian known what fortune has delay'd,
His heart might yet have been preserv'd from harm.
I must away at once and trust that time,
Though wounded, may be heal'd before it dies.
Exit.
SCENE III. A Burial Ground.
Enter LORD RENALD and a PAGE with flowers and a lantern.
RENALD.
Give me the light. Stand further off, good boy.
Yet keep thy hearing sharp. Beneath that elm
Lie close and watch the pathway to the tomb.
If any traveler should approach this place,
Whistle at once and let me know of it.
PAGE.
My lord, the churchyard chills me.
RENALD.
Fear it not.
Take these commands and follow them.
PAGE retires.
RENALD.
Poor Isabel, untimely laid to rest,
I bring these flowers where happier gifts were due.
The earth receives what should have graced a throne.
Each night I come and water this cold stone
With tears that answer not my grief at all.
Would I had died before I saw this day.
The PAGE whistles.
RENALD.
Ha! That is warning. Someone draws near.
I will conceal myself and learn who comes.
He withdraws into the shadows.

SCENE V. A blasted moor. Thunder. Enter three ENCHANTRESSES meeting MORWENA.
FIRST ENCHANTRESS.
Mistress, you seem displeased.
MORWENA.
And well I may. What boldness led you forth
To meddle with the fate of Lord Arcturus,
Speaking dark promises into his ear,
Yet never once consulting me, whose hand
Directs the hidden workings of our craft?
SECOND ENCHANTRESS.
We meant no slight.
MORWENA.
Yet slight it was.
You toyed with matters touching crowns and blood,
And all for one ambitious mortal man
Who values power above those who assist him.
Such men use wonders as they use their swords:
To serve themselves, then cast them by forgotten.
THIRD ENCHANTRESS.
What would you have us do?
MORWENA.
Repair the fault.
At dawn assemble by the sunken well
That lies beneath the blackthorn grove. He comes.
His thoughts already race beyond his station.
He longs to know what fortune waits ahead.
Prepare your phials and enchanted roots.
Bring charms collected under moonless skies.
This night I labor toward a greater end.
Upon the western wind there rides a shade,
Half born of dream and half of mortal fear.
Before the morning star ascends the heavens,
I shall compel it to assume a shape
And whisper false assurances to him.
He shall believe himself beyond all danger.
He shall mistake desire for destiny,
And confidence for wisdom. Thus he falls.
For nothing serves destruction more completely
Than a man persuaded he cannot fail.
Music within.
I hear my summons. See, my familiar waits
Among the drifting vapors of the marsh.
Exit.
FIRST ENCHANTRESS.
Come. We have much to gather ere she returns.
Exeunt.
SCENE VI. A chamber in the royal fortress.
Enter VARREN and another NOBLE.
VARREN.
My thoughts and yours, I think, have traveled alike.
The kingdom bears strange burdens these last months.
NOBLE.
It does indeed.
VARREN.
Our gracious sovereign died most suddenly.
Lord Arcturus lamented him with tears;
Yet somehow every road of consequence
Has led directly to Lord Arcturus' gain.
The late commander perished on a hunt.
His son fled shortly after. Many say
Flight proves the crime, though proof is thin enough.
NOBLE.
Few dare examine such conclusions now.
VARREN.
Wisely, perhaps.
The new-made ruler punishes suspicion
Almost as swiftly as rebellion itself.
Those absent from his feasts attract remark.
Those speaking freely earn unwelcome notice.
NOBLE.
Have you heard news of Lord Cedric?
VARREN.
Only rumors.
NOBLE.
Then hear this. He has crossed the sea in secret
To seek the rightful heir.
VARREN.
The prince lives still?
NOBLE.
In safety, so it is reported.
A neighboring king extends protection to him,
Together with such counsel and support
As fortune still allows.
VARREN.
Then hope survives.
NOBLE.
More than survives. Lord Cedric seeks assistance.
If foreign swords and loyal hearts unite,
The realm may yet recover from its wounds.
We hunger now for ordinary blessings:
Peace at our tables, rest throughout the night,
Justice untainted by fear or favor.
VARREN.
And what says Arcturus to these reports?
NOBLE.
He gathers troops. Messengers ride daily.
Something is brewing.
VARREN.
Cedric was summoned home?
NOBLE.
He was.
VARREN.
And answered?
NOBLE.
With a firm refusal. The envoy returned
Bearing little more than cold civility
And warnings left unspoken.
VARREN.
A prudent choice.
NOBLE.
A necessary one.
VARREN.
May some good spirit speed across the sea
And aid the prince before the storm arrives.
This suffering land has endured enough.
NOBLE.
Amen to that. My prayers shall follow him.
Exeunt.
ACT IV
SCENE I. A cavern beneath a ruined tower. In the center, a brazier burning with green flame. Thunder. Enter the three ENCHANTRESSES.
FIRST ENCHANTRESS.
Twice hath the raven croaked at midnight's hour.
SECOND ENCHANTRESS.
Twice hath the marsh-hound answered from afar.
THIRD ENCHANTRESS.
And now the appointed moment draws near.ADRIAN.
No rest tonight. My thoughts drive sleep away.
As I came through the poorer streets, I passed a narrow house whose walls seemed ready to fall about their owner. I remember thinking then that misery makes bargains prosperity would never entertain. If a desperate man required some forbidden thing, there lived one who might provide it.
And now I find myself at his door.
This should be the place.
Ho! Is anyone within?
Enter PHYSICIAN.
PHYSICIAN.
Who calls?
ADRIAN.
A friend, if money can purchase friendship. Come nearer.
I see enough in your face to know your circumstances. Here—take this purse. In return, I ask for a certain remedy.
PHYSICIAN.
What remedy?
ADRIAN.
One that leaves no time for suffering.
PHYSICIAN.
Sir, speak lower.
Such medicines exist, but the law forbids them. Were I discovered selling one, it would cost me my life.
ADRIAN.
And what sort of life is left to you now?
Your coat is worn thin. Hunger has hollowed your cheeks. The law threatens you with death, yet it has done little to preserve your living.
PHYSICIAN.
You press a hard argument.
ADRIAN.
Need presses harder.
Take the gold.
PHYSICIAN.
I do not like this business.
ADRIAN.
Nor I. Yet here we stand.
PHYSICIAN.
Then listen carefully. I have a powder. Mixed into wine or water, it works swiftly. A healthy man would not survive the hour.
ADRIAN.
That is enough.
PHYSICIAN.
May Heaven forgive me.
ADRIAN.
And me as well.
There is your payment. Strange thing, gold. Men condemn poisons and outlaw them, yet this metal has ruined more souls than any apothecary's vial. It has bought betrayals, murders, wars, and false friendships.
Keep it. Buy food. Mend your household.
Farewell.
Exit ADRIAN.
PHYSICIAN.
Poor comfort. Yet poor comfort is sometimes all that poverty receives.
Exit.
SCENE II. A Scholar's Chamber.
Enter BROTHER MATTHIAS carrying a sealed letter.
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
Brother Sebastian!
Enter BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
You have returned at last. Was the message delivered?
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
No.
I had scarcely begun my journey when I was detained outside the western quarter. A sickness had broken out there. The authorities closed the roads and posted guards at every entrance.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
For how long?
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
Three days.
No one was permitted to leave. No messenger would accept a letter from our hands. Fear traveled faster than the disease itself.
He gives him the letter.
Here. It remains unopened.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
This is grave news.
The contents were urgent. Had they been merely courteous words, the delay would matter little. Instead, every hour lost increases the danger.
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
What is to be done?
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
We must act without waiting.
Fetch me a lantern. Bring also the iron tools from the storeroom. Quickly.
BROTHER MATTHIAS.
I shall return directly.
Exit.
BROTHER SEBASTIAN.
What cruel fortune chooses its moments so precisely.
Before dawn, the young woman concealed within the chapel will wake. Adrian should already know the truth. Instead he remains ignorant, and ignorance is often more dangerous than malice.
There is no time left for wishing.
I must go myself.
Exit.
SCENE III. A Burial Ground.
Enter LORD RENALD and a PAGE carrying flowers and a lantern.
RENALD.
Give me the lantern.
Now go and wait beneath that elm tree. Stay where you can watch the path. If anyone approaches the tomb, whistle to me at once.
PAGE.
My lord, I would rather be anywhere else than in a churchyard at night.
RENALD.
You are not the first to feel so.
Take courage and do as I ask.
PAGE.
I will.
He retires.
RENALD.
Isabel.
I bring flowers again, though they are poor substitutes for the life that should have been yours. This stone receives them gladly enough, but it returns nothing.
Every night I come here. Every night I tell myself it eases the grief.
It does not.
Still I return.
He lays down the flowers.
The earth has claimed too fair a treasure. Had fate shown a little mercy, these blossoms would have adorned a wedding feast rather than a grave.
The PAGE whistles.
RENALD.
Someone comes.
Either a mourner or a wanderer. I would know which.
He withdraws into the shadows.
Exeunt.

LORD VAREK.
Away with your remedies. I have no use for them.
Bring me my sword. The breastplate too. Make haste.
Captain, see the watch is doubled on the walls.
PHYSICIAN.
My lord—
VAREK.
No more. The nobles leave me one by one.
If you possessed such skill as men report,
You would not spend it curing fevers only.
Look rather on this realm. There lies the sickness.
Its veins are clogged with treason and with fear.
Find me a draught to cleanse the kingdom whole,
And I shall praise your art above all others.
PHYSICIAN.
The city speaks of armies drawing near.
VAREK.
So much I know.
A thousand rumors blow about the land,
Yet walls are stone, and stone is not persuaded
By every wind that passes overhead.
Bring my armor after me.
Exit VAREK with attendants.
PHYSICIAN.
Were I beyond these gates before the dawn,
No purse could tempt me back.
Exit.
SCENE IV. A plain before the fortress. Hills in the distance.
Enter PRINCE ALDRIC, GENERAL ROWAN, LORD EDRIC, LORD MARSH, and Soldiers.
ALDRIC.
The end approaches. I can almost feel it.
EDRIC.
The men believe the same.
ROWAN.
What ridge lies yonder?
MARSH.
The ridge of Ashen Hill.
ALDRIC.
Then let each soldier cut a branch and bear it.
The enemy watches from the battlements.
Let us disguise our number till we stand
Almost beneath their walls.
SOLDIERS.
It shall be done.
ROWAN.
We hear the tyrant remains within the fortress.
MARSH.
He trusts its strength.
ALDRIC.
Because little else remains to him.
Many who once proclaimed their loyalty
Have vanished at the hour they were most needed.
Those who remain obey from habit or necessity.
Few hearts are truly his.
EDRIC.
The cause will soon be judged by steel, not words.
ROWAN.
Aye. Speculation serves until the battle.
Thereafter deeds pronounce the final sentence.
ALDRIC.
Forward, then.
Exeunt marching.
SCENE V. Within the fortress.
Enter LORD VAREK, CAPTAIN, and Soldiers.
VAREK.
Raise every banner. Let them see our colors.
The cry continues: “They approach, they approach.”
Let them.
These walls have endured storms before.
We shall outlast this one as well.
Had those who owed me service kept their faith,
We might have met the rebels in the field
And scattered them before they reached these gates.
A cry is heard within.
What sound was that?
CAPTAIN.
The women of the household, my lord.
Exit CAPTAIN.
VAREK.
Once such a noise would have disturbed my blood.
There was a time when midnight tales of murder
Could chill me where I stood. No longer.
I have lived too long among calamities.
Disaster has become a familiar guest.
The mind grows accustomed even to horrors.
Re-enter CAPTAIN.
Why do they cry?
CAPTAIN.
Your sister, my lord, is dead.
VAREK.
Dead?
A strange word. It waits for every house,
Yet when it enters one's own door it seems
An unexpected visitor.
There would have been an hour to mourn her properly.
Now time itself feels broken into fragments.
What are our years? A handful of brief moments
Joined together and mistaken for a life.
We labor, scheme, contend, acquire, and lose;
Then vanish. Another takes our place.
The world remembers little.
Enter a SCOUT.
SCOUT.
My lord, I bring a report.
VAREK.
Speak quickly.
SCOUT.
I scarcely trust my own eyes.
VAREK.
Then trust your tongue.
SCOUT.
From the western tower I looked toward Ashen Hill.
At first I thought the forest stirring in the wind.
But soon I saw it move as one body.
VAREK.
What nonsense is this?
SCOUT.
No nonsense, my lord.
Within an hour you may observe it plainly.
The trees advance.
VAREK.
The trees?
SCOUT.
Or men concealed among them.
VAREK.
If this proves false, your reward shall be a rope.
SCOUT.
Then I accept the risk.
VAREK.
Leave me.
Exit SCOUT.
The signs return to trouble me at last.
I trusted too readily in assurances,
Mistaking confidence for certainty.
Yet whether prophecy deceived me or not,
The choice before me is unchanged.
Sound the alarm.
Arm every man who can still bear a weapon.
If fate has come at last, let it find me standing.
Exeunt.

LEON.
You have turned my words upside down.
FELIX.
Then they stand upon their heads and see the world better.
LEON.
A strange philosophy.
FELIX.
The only kind worth having.
LEON.
You would make wisdom out of nonsense.
FELIX.
Nay, wisdom is but nonsense that hath prospered.
LEON.
There is a sentence fit for hanging in a tavern.
FELIX.
Then let it hang there. It shall keep better company than in a church.
LEON.
Your wit runs loose today.
FELIX.
Like a hound slipped from the leash.
LEON.
Take care it does not run itself lame.
FELIX.
Fear not. It feeds upon its own speed.
LEON.
A lean diet.
FELIX.
Yet enough to keep it alive.
MARCUS.
Gentlemen, if words were arrows, the town would need rebuilding.
FELIX.
And if silence were gold, you would be the richest man in it.
MARCUS.
A poor fortune indeed.
FELIX.
Poor? Silence costs nothing and is seldom purchased.
LEON.
You praise silence loudly.
FELIX.
Someone must speak for it.
Enter HOUSEKEEPER and SERVANT.
LEON.
See there. A ship enters harbor.
FELIX.
A ship? I see rather the harbor entering the ship.
HOUSEKEEPER.
Thomas!
SERVANT.
Madam?
HOUSEKEEPER.
My basket.
FELIX.
Good Thomas, hide thy face behind it. The basket bears itself more bravely.
HOUSEKEEPER.
Good day, gentlemen.
FELIX.
And a good day returned.
HOUSEKEEPER.
You seem merry.
FELIX.
We labor to keep sadness unemployed.
HOUSEKEEPER.
Then you are diligent workers.
LEON.
Especially he.
HOUSEKEEPER.
Can either of you direct me to young Master Leon?
LEON.
That depends. If you seek him quickly, he is near. If wisely, he may be difficult to find.
HOUSEKEEPER.
A plain answer would serve.
LEON.
Then I am the man.
HOUSEKEEPER.
So I suspected.
FELIX.
A triumph of investigation.
HOUSEKEEPER.
Sir, I would speak privately.
MARCUS.
There is mystery in that request.
FELIX.
Or debt.
LEON.
You think all messages concern debt.
FELIX.
Most do. The remainder concern marriage, which is debt with ceremony.
HOUSEKEEPER.
You are an impertinent fellow.
FELIX.
Madam, I strive for consistency.
LEON.
Come, enough.
FELIX.
Very well. I leave you to your secrets. Marcus, shall we seek dinner?
MARCUS.
Gladly.
FELIX.
Farewell, worthy lady. Farewell, basket. Farewell, Thomas, guardian of baskets.
Exeunt FELIX and MARCUS.
HOUSEKEEPER.
Who is that man who spends words as though they cost him nothing?
LEON.
A friend. He speaks first and reflects afterward.
HOUSEKEEPER.
I noticed as much.
If he had mocked me further, I should have answered him.
SERVANT.
I was ready, madam.
HOUSEKEEPER.
You were ready to do nothing.
SERVANT.
I was ready to begin doing something.
HOUSEKEEPER.
That is what you always say.
SERVANT.
And one day it shall be true.

A herald sounds within.
HERALD. Make way there! The noble challengers approach the field.
Enter a Knight and his Squire.
KNIGHT. Good sir, announce me to the king.
HERALD. Your name and country?
KNIGHT. I am a lord of Macedon, come from distant lands to prove my worth before the princess.
HERALD. Stand forth, then.
Enter King Leontes, Princess Helena, Lords, and Attendants.
LEONTES. What knight is this that seeks our favour?
KNIGHT. My gracious lord, though fortune hath not smiled on me of late, yet courage remains. Hearing of your daughter's beauty and virtue, I have ventured hither to contend among these worthy men.
HELENA. Sir knight, your courtesy becomes you well. What device bear you on your shield?
SQUIRE. A silver falcon mounting through the clouds toward a star; beneath is written, Spes super astra.
LEONTES. A noble emblem. It speaks of one whose hopes rise higher than his condition.
KNIGHT. My lord, a man may be poor in treasure and yet rich in honour. If I prevail, let merit be my witness; if I fail, I shall still esteem the journey well spent.
LEONTES. You answer wisely. Many boast before the contest begins; few speak so modestly.
FIRST LORD. The other challengers are assembled and await your command.
LEONTES. Then let the trumpets sound. This day shall prove what metal lies beneath bright armour.
Trumpets flourish.
HELENA. Father, I confess these spectacles delight me less for victory than for the virtues they reveal. True worth is not always known until it is tried.
LEONTES. Well said, daughter. The furnace discovers the gold, and trial the man.
HERALD. Let the lists be opened!
The Knights prepare themselves.
KNIGHT. Whatever fortune grants, I thank the heavens for this occasion. A brave attempt is itself a kind of triumph.
LEONTES. Go then, and may honour attend the deserving.
Exeunt toward the tournament.

Enter Lord Aurelian and Sir Edmund.
AURELIAN. Is our sovereign yet awake, good sir?
EDMUND. Not so, my lord. He charged me to attend him at first light, yet I fear the hour runs fast.
AURELIAN. I shall conduct you to his chamber.
EDMUND. The duty is a welcome burden, though a burden still.
AURELIAN. Service performed with love sits lightly on the shoulders. Here is the door.
EDMUND. Then with your leave I’ll call upon him.
Exit Edmund.
RICHARD. Will the king depart this morning?
AURELIAN. So he intended when last we spoke.
RICHARD. The heavens were troubled through the night. Fierce winds battered the towers, and many swore they heard strange cries upon the air. Dogs howled without cease, and the ravens gathered before dawn as though they scented calamity.
AURELIAN. The storm was harsh indeed.
RICHARD. In all my years I do not recall its equal.
Re-enter Edmund.
EDMUND. O miserable sight! O dreadful, dreadful day!
AURELIAN, RICHARD. What has befallen?
EDMUND. Ruin has entered where honour slept. A most foul crime has struck at majesty itself and robbed this kingdom of its living heart.
AURELIAN. Explain yourself!
RICHARD. Speak plainly, man.
EDMUND. Go to the chamber and behold it with your own eyes. My tongue refuses what my eyes have witnessed.
Aurelian and Richard exit.
Awake! Awake! Ring the bell! Treason! Murder! Let every loyal soul arise! Prince Henry! Prince Edward! Wake from sleep and look upon the horror that has visited this house!
A bell rings.
Enter Queen Eleanor.
ELEANOR. What summons breaks the silence of the dawn? Why does this fearful noise disturb the household?
EDMUND. Madam, forgive me. The news is too terrible for gentle ears.
Enter Lord Stanley.
STANLEY. What means this uproar?
EDMUND. Our gracious king is slain!
ELEANOR. Heaven defend us! Slain?
STANLEY. No, say not so. Let those words be false.
Re-enter Aurelian and Richard with Lords.
AURELIAN. Had death taken me before this hour, I should have counted myself fortunate. From this moment all joy seems hollow. Honour lies dead beside our sovereign.
Enter Prince Henry and Prince Edward.
HENRY. What disaster has occurred?
AURELIAN. The source from which your lives were nourished is cut off. Your father is no more.
EDMUND. The king has been murdered.
HENRY. By whose hand?
RICHARD. His attendants appeared guilty. Their garments were stained with blood, and the weapons were found beside them.
AURELIAN. In grief and rage I struck them down before they could answer.
EDMUND. Why did you act so quickly?
AURELIAN. Who could remain calm before such a sight? There lay our noble king, his wounds crying out for vengeance. Reason yielded to loyalty and sorrow.
ELEANOR. I grow faint.
STANLEY. Attend the queen.
HENRY. Why do we stand silent while danger gathers around us?
EDWARD. We know not where the blow was forged. It may be aimed at us next.
STANLEY. Let us withdraw for a time, compose ourselves, and then seek the truth. Fear clouds every judgment.
HENRY. Agreed. We must learn who profits from this evil deed.
STANLEY. Until then, I place my trust in Heaven and stand ready against whatever treachery remains concealed.
Exeunt.

The second Knight passes by, and his Squire presents his shield to Princess Helena.
KING LEONTES. Who is this second champion?
HELENA. A duke from Sicily, honoured father;
Upon his shield appears a soaring eagle
That climbs above the storm and seeks the sun;
The motto, Virtus superat fata.
LEONTES. A noble thought. True courage oft aspires
Beyond the reach of fortune's harsh command.
The third Knight passes by, and his Squire presents his shield.
LEONTES. And who comes next?
HELENA. A lord of Venice.
His device is a laurel growing from a rock;
The word, Per dura ad gloriam.
LEONTES. A fitting emblem for a steadfast man.
The fourth Knight passes.
HELENA. The fourth bears a broken sword
Bound fast with golden cords;
The motto, Fides reparat.
LEONTES. Loyalty can mend what force destroys.
The fifth Knight passes.
HELENA. Upon the fifth knight's shield
A ship rides safely through a violent sea;
Beneath is written, Spem sequor.
LEONTES. Hope is a pilot many men have trusted.
The sixth Knight enters alone, his armour plain and weathered. He presents his shield himself.
LEONTES. And what says this last device,
Which the knight delivers with such modest grace?
HELENA. A single flower rising through the snow;
The motto, Dum vivo spero.
LEONTES. A simple lesson, yet a pleasing one.
Though winter presses hard upon his state,
He trusts that spring may yet return.
FIRST LORD. His appearance promises but little.
His armour seems more acquainted with neglect
Than with the honours of the field.
SECOND LORD. Yet strangers often wear disguises
That hide their worth.
THIRD LORD. Or perhaps he kept that rust
To polish it with glory in the lists.
LEONTES. Men judge too swiftly by what they can see.
The garment does not always reveal the man.
But look—the contest ends. Let us retire.
Exeunt. Great shouts within. Voices cry, “The Stranger Knight! The Stranger Knight!”

SCENE III. A Banquet Hall
Enter King Leontes, Helena, Lords, Attendants, and Knights.
LEONTES. My lords and noble knights,
To welcome you would waste unnecessary words.
Your deeds have spoken loudly for themselves.
This hall is prepared for joy, and joy becomes
Such company as yours.
Take now your places.
HELENA. And you, good sir,
To whom this garland of victory belongs,
Receive it as the honour of the day.
STRANGER KNIGHT. Lady, fortune has been kinder than my merits.
LEONTES. Call it fortune if you wish;
The prize remains deserved.
Not all men are fashioned in equal measure.
Some achieve honour, while others seem born
To surpass it.
Come, daughter, lead the feast.
KNIGHTS. We thank Your Majesty.
LEONTES. You do us honour by your presence.
MARSHAL. Sir, your seat awaits.
STRANGER KNIGHT. Another may deserve it more.
FIRST KNIGHT. Not so.
We who have lost bear no resentment,
Nor should excellence be denied its due.
STRANGER KNIGHT. Your generosity exceeds my deserts.
LEONTES. Sit, sir, sit.
Aside.
By heaven, I marvel at my daughter.
The feast stands before her untouched,
Yet all her thoughts seem fixed elsewhere.
HELENA. Aside.
The richest dishes lose their savour.
My eyes have feasted where my heart would dine.
Surely this knight possesses uncommon virtue.
LEONTES. Aside.
He is but a gentleman,
No different from the rest.
A broken lance or two has earned him praise.
Let that suffice.
HELENA. Aside.
To compare the others with him
Is to compare crystal with a star.
STRANGER KNIGHT. Aside.
That king recalls my father's image,
Majestic in the days of his prosperity.
Around him gathered nobles like bright planets
Circling a sun.
Now I remain, diminished by misfortune,
A lonely spark where once a fire burned.
Thus time exalts and humbles whom it pleases;
It grants its gifts, then asks them back again.

BALTHAZAR. He hears us not, nor answers to our call. Love hath transformed him into some strange creature.
LUCIUS. Let him alone. Such men are better companions to their thoughts than to their friends.
BALTHAZAR. Yet I shall summon him once more. I conjure thee by Celia's golden hair, by her bright smile and gentle voice, by every grace that nature placed within her, appear before us and confess thy bondage.
LUCIUS. If he hears that, he'll curse thee for it.
BALTHAZAR. Not so. A lover is never angered by praise of his beloved. He would sooner quarrel with the wind than with the sound of her name.
LUCIUS. Look there. He has hidden himself beside the garden wall. The night suits his humour.
BALTHAZAR. Love delights in shadows. Yet if love cannot see, how comes it always to find trouble?
LUCIUS. Come, let us leave him. He seeks solitude, and we waste our breath.
BALTHAZAR. Agreed. Good night to our wandering friend.
Exeunt.
SCENE II. A Garden by Moonlight
Enter ADRIAN.
ADRIAN. They laugh at longing who have never suffered it.
A window opens above. CELIA appears.
What radiance breaks upon the darkness there?
The moon grows pale beside her.
She stands above the garden like a star
That has forgotten heaven and wandered here.
If beauty had a voice, it would be hers.
Yet she speaks not.
Her silence itself is music.
How softly she rests her hand upon her cheek.
Would that I were the breeze that touched her face.
CELIA. Alas.
ADRIAN. She speaks. Let every other sound be still.
CELIA. Why must affection choose such difficult paths?
Why should the one I love belong to those
Whom my family calls enemies?
Were he named differently,
Would he be less kind,
Less worthy,
Less himself?
A name is but a word.
The flower blooming in the garden
Would smell as sweet if called by any other title.
So too the man I love.
What power lies in a name
That it should divide two hearts?
ADRIAN. Aside.
Shall I remain silent, or reveal myself?
CELIA. If he would cast aside that hated name,
I would surrender all hesitation.
ADRIAN. Then hear me.
Call me what you will,
And I shall answer.
The name I bear matters little
If it displeases you.
CELIA. Who speaks from the darkness?
ADRIAN. One who fears his own name
Only because it troubles you.
Were it written before me,
I would gladly blot it out.
CELIA. Though I have heard but little,
The voice is known to me.
You are Adrian.
ADRIAN. And happier now than ever that I am.

PISTOL.
Curse thee and all thy counsel! Take thy friendship and choke on it!
FLUELLEN.
That is spoken. It shall be remembered.
PISTOL.
A Spanish fig for thee and thy discipline!
[Exit.]
FLUELLEN.
Very well. He is gone in anger.
GOWER.
That fellow is no soldier, but a wanderer in borrowed armor. I know him now. A thief of words and a thief of coin both.
FLUELLEN.
He did speak stoutly enough upon the rampart, I will maintain that much. Yet words and deeds are not one thing, no.
GOWER.
He is one of those idle companions who haunt the wars only to adorn themselves with other men’s honor. They learn the names of captains as children learn songs, and later sing them at taverns as if they were present at the deeds.
FLUELLEN.
If I find him again, I shall examine his courage more closely. There may be cracks in his show of valor.
[Drums heard.]
GOWER.
The King approaches.
FLUELLEN.
From the bridge?
GOWER.
Aye.
Enter KING HENRY, GLOUCESTER, and Soldiers.
FLUELLEN.
God preserve your Majesty! The passage of the bridge is most nobly kept.
KING HENRY.
Speak, Fluellen. How stands the field?
FLUELLEN.
The enemy has withdrawn, your Majesty, with some disorder. Our men held firm. The Duke of Exeter behaved with great resolution, as any captain might desire.
KING HENRY.
What loss have we sustained?
FLUELLEN.
Very little in truth. A few wounded, none of principal account.
Though there is one matter, if it please your Majesty.
KING HENRY.
Proceed.
FLUELLEN.
A certain soldier, one Bardolph, was taken in theft against a church, and by the law of war he hath been executed.
KING HENRY.
So.
FLUELLEN.
He was a man of loud countenance, his face much inflamed, as if fire were lodged within it. Yet such outward appearance is no defense before justice.
KING HENRY.
Let it be known throughout the army: no plundering, no violence against the people of France. What is taken shall be paid for. Discipline shall be our strength, not disorder.
FLUELLEN.
It shall be done.
[Tucket sounded.]
Enter MONTJOY.
MONTJOY.
I come from my sovereign with message.
KING HENRY.
Speak freely. I know thy office.
MONTJOY.
Thus says the King of France: though fortune has not favored us in the field, the matter is not yet concluded. He bids you consider the losses you have sustained, and the long labor of your march, and to weigh whether further resistance serves your advantage.
He offers terms, though not generous ones, for the return of your forces.
KING HENRY.
Is this all?
MONTJOY.
There is more. He reminds you that your position grows weaker with time, and that endurance is not victory. He urges you to accept composition before greater ruin follows.
KING HENRY.
And what of his own condition?
MONTJOY.
He stands prepared for war, yet would prefer resolution by treaty.
KING HENRY.
Then return to him and say this: we are not yet diminished to the point he imagines. Our strength is measured not only in numbers, but in will.
Tell him England has not yet spent her breath. And though we bleed, we do not bend for threats.
As for his terms, we shall consider them in our own time, not his.
MONTJOY.
I shall carry your answer.
KING HENRY.
Go then.
[Exit MONTJOY.]
GLOUCESTER.
Your Majesty speaks firmly.
KING HENRY.
Necessity teaches the tongue its shape.
Come, let us see what the next hour brings. War does not pause for negotiation, nor does honor wait upon convenience.
[Exeunt.]

SERVANT TO AURELIUS.
You speak more wisely than you look.
FOOL.
And thou look’st wiser than thou speak’st, which is a harder deceit to maintain.
SERVANT.
You answer quickly for one held a fool.
FOOL.
Speed is the last refuge of those who have no estate in argument.
SERVANT.
Here comes Lord Aurelius.
Enter AURELIUS and MARCUS.
MARCUS.
Keep near him. I would speak with you apart.
FOOL.
Apart is the safest company.
SERVANT.
I had rather stand aside than be found standing wrong.
[Exeunt Servants.]
AURELIUS.
I marvel at it still. Why was my estate not set before me in full,
that I might measure my spending against my means?
I was made to believe the ground beneath me firmer than it proved.
MARCUS.
My lord, I spoke at several times, and in as plain a manner as I could devise.
AURELIUS.
You spoke, yes. Yet never at the right hour, or so it seemed to me then.
Some occasions found me unwilling, others found me distracted, and your counsel was set aside as if it were no more than noise at a feast.
MARCUS.
I brought accounts before you. I laid them open without disguise.
You would sometimes cast them back and say they were written to frighten you rather than to inform you.
When gifts were asked for, I hesitated, and you thought it refusal out of neglect. Yet I often begged you to hold your hand, to consider the drain upon your coffers.
AURELIUS.
And now you speak of coffers as if they still held breath.
MARCUS.
They do not, my lord. They are emptied. Some lands are already pledged, others lost outright. What remains can scarcely answer the debts that press upon the present hour.
AURELIUS.
Then let the remainder go also. I would not be haunted by fragments.
MARCUS.
It is already nearly nothing to sell.
AURELIUS.
Nothing? I was told I possessed abundance.
MARCUS.
Abundance that moved too freely is soon called memory rather than possession.
AURELIUS.
So I am poor without knowing the passage into poverty.
MARCUS.
Poverty is not a sudden arrival. It advances while a man is looking elsewhere.
AURELIUS.
And what would you have me do now? Defend the gap between what I am and what I thought myself to be?
MARCUS.
I would have you pause. Consider those to whom you owe. Consider also those who depend upon your house.
AURELIUS.
Pause. That word comes always too late.
MARCUS.
Better late than never spoken.
AURELIUS.
Yet spoken is not the same as answered.
MARCUS.
True.
Enter a MESSENGER.
MESSENGER.
My lord, the creditors press again. They demand settlement without delay.
AURELIUS.
Let them press. There is little left to yield.
MESSENGER.
They threaten suit.
AURELIUS.
Suit may be made of cloth or complaint; I possess neither in plenty now.
MESSENGER.
They ask for what is owed.
AURELIUS.
And I ask for what is possible.
[Exit MESSENGER.]
MARCUS.
The hour grows sharper.
AURELIUS.
Then let it sharpen me. I have been soft too long in my own judgement.
If I trusted too easily, it was not without reason. Men spoke fairly. Promises stood like pillars in my hearing. It is only now, when the roof leans, that I begin to question the strength of those supports.
MARCUS.
The roof has not fallen yet.
AURELIUS.
Not yet. But I hear it complain.
MARCUS.
There is still time to rebuild if you will allow it.
AURELIUS.
Rebuild from what? From absence?
MARCUS.
From what remains.
AURELIUS.
Very little remains.
MARCUS.
Enough to begin.
AURELIUS.
Then begin carefully. I would not lose even the act of beginning.
[Exeunt.]

SCENE I. A court of judgement.
HERALD.
By sentence of the realm it is determined: the matter stands concluded, and no appeal shall alter it.
SOMERSET.
I accept the King’s grace and judgment.
HORNER.
And I accept the trial appointed me, without reluctance.
PETER.
Alas, my lord, I am no fighter. I cannot stand in combat. Have pity upon me, for I am undone before I begin. My limbs fail me, and my heart will not serve me. O Lord, deliver me!
GLOUCESTER.
You shall either fight or suffer the law.
KING HENRY.
Take them hence to prison. The day appointed for combat shall fall upon the last day of next month.
Come, Somerset, you shall be conveyed away.
[Flourish. Exeunt.]

SCENE II. A garden within Gloucester’s house. Night.
Enter MARGERY, HUME, SOUTHWELL, and BOLINGBROKE.
HUME.
The duchess awaits what was promised. There must be no delay.
BOLINGBROKE.
All instruments are prepared. Nothing is lacking.
HUME.
She will indeed be present to witness this work?
BOLINGBROKE.
She will. Though you need not fear her. Her courage is spoken of everywhere.
HUME.
Then I shall leave you to it. I will not be seen when the matter begins.
[Exit Hume.]
BOLINGBROKE.
Mother Jourdain, lie close and attend the ground. Southwell, begin your reading when all is set.
JOURDAIN.
Proceed, my masters. I have long desired this hour.
SOUTHWELL.
The time is suitable. Night itself lends authority to such operations.
BOLINGBROKE.
Set the circle. Make it firm. Let nothing pass its bounds.
[The Duchess appears above; Hume follows unseen.]
DUCHESS.
You are bold men to call upon powers you do not understand.
SOUTHWELL.
Stand ready.
[Ceremonies performed. Thunder.]
SPIRIT.
I am here. Speak.
JOURDAIN.
By the power we invoke, answer truly what is demanded of thee.
SPIRIT.
Ask.
BOLINGBROKE.
What becomes of the King?
SPIRIT.
He shall be cast down by one yet living, and shall not long survive his fall.
SOUTHWELL.
And Suffolk?
SPIRIT.
He shall perish upon the water.
BOLINGBROKE.
And Somerset?
SPIRIT.
He shall find safety in no stronghold; open ground shall serve him better than walls.
SOUTHWELL.
Enough. The answers grow dark.
DUCHESS.
No, I will hear more.
SPIRIT.
No more is permitted.
[Thunder. The spirit vanishes.]
HUME.
We are observed. This place is no longer secure.
BOLINGBROKE.
Break off the rite. Away.

SCENE III. The same garden. Enter YORK and BUCKINGHAM with guards.
YORK.
Seize them all without exception.
BUCKINGHAM.
So this was the practice—hidden arts within the court itself.
YORK.
Madam, you are taken in the very act.
DUCHESS.
You call it act. I call it inquiry.
BUCKINGHAM.
Enough. Remove them.
YORK.
Search the ground thoroughly. Leave nothing concealed.
[They search and gather writings.]
BUCKINGHAM.
Here are papers.
YORK.
Read them.
BUCKINGHAM.
Prophecies concerning the crown… and the downfall of the King.
YORK.
So the design is plain enough.
DUCHESS.
You read what you wish to find.
YORK.
We read what is written.
BUCKINGHAM.
Take them hence.
[Exeunt guards with prisoners.]
YORK.
This night has uncovered more than was expected.
BUCKINGHAM.
And yet less than is certain. These writings speak, but not clearly.
YORK.
Even so, they are dangerous in any hand.
BUCKINGHAM.
Especially when men begin to believe them.
YORK.
Come. We must report this to the King.
[Exeunt.]

GENERAL. Had you but seen him in the breach—
VALERIUS. I pray you, speak no further.
My father, when he praises me,
Makes me ashamed of what little I have done.
I acted as necessity required,
No more than any loyal man should do.
He whose intentions served the state as well
Deserves as much remembrance as my deeds.
GENERAL. You wrong yourself.
The kingdom must be told who won this day.
To bury merit is a kind of theft,
And silence may dishonour noble actions
As surely as false witness.
Therefore permit me,
Not as reward but recognition,
To speak before the army.
VALERIUS. My wounds ache enough already.
They need no herald to remind them why.
GENERAL. Then let them ache with gratitude instead.
Of all the spoils recovered in this campaign,
The horses, gold, and treasures taken here,
The tenth part shall be yours to choose before
The common distribution.
VALERIUS. My thanks, my lord,
But I cannot persuade myself to profit
From service freely given.
My sword was never hired.
I claim no larger share than any soldier
Who stood beside me in the fight.
A flourish. The soldiers cheer and cry, “Valerius! Valerius!”
VALERIUS. Peace, I beg you!
If trumpets praise where duty only acted,
Then honour loses value.
Because I bled,
Because I happened to stand nearest danger,
You raise me higher than I deserve.
Many brave men have done the same today
Without these loud acclamations.
GENERAL. Your modesty does greater injury
To your reputation than our praise can mend.
Yet whether you approve or not,
The army shall know what it owes you.
Before all gathered here,
I grant you this renowned warhorse,
Whose service is known throughout the camp.
And from this day forward,
For your defence of Blackstone Gate,
You shall be called Valerius Blackstone.
Trumpets and drums.
ALL. Valerius Blackstone!
BLACKSTONE. I thank you.
When next I ride that noble horse,
I shall endeavour to deserve the name
You place upon me.
GENERAL. Come, let us retire.
Before we rest, dispatches must be sent
To tell the capital of our success.
CAPTAIN. They shall be written at once.
BLACKSTONE. The gods mock me still.
I refused rich gifts,
Yet now I find myself compelled to ask one favour.
GENERAL. Speak freely.
BLACKSTONE. During the siege,
I lodged within a poor citizen's house.
The man showed kindness when I had little comfort.
Today I saw him taken prisoner,
Yet battle drew my eye elsewhere.
I ask his freedom.
GENERAL. Gladly granted.
Were he my enemy's closest ally,
Your request would win him liberty.
CAPTAIN. What is the prisoner's name?
BLACKSTONE. Strange.
The exhaustion of war has robbed me of it.
My memory fails me.
GENERAL. Then rest first.
Your face is caked with blood and dust.
Come.
Exeunt.
SCENE X. The Enemy Camp
Flourish. Enter ARDEN, wounded, with soldiers.
ARDEN. The fortress is lost.
SOLDIER. Terms are being discussed. It may yet be surrendered back under agreement.
ARDEN. Agreement?
Would that I belonged to the victors.
For being what I am,
I cannot be content with what remains.

“Come inside, sir, do,” said Mrs. Barton, holding the door wider. “You’ve come a fair distance this evening, I’ll wager.”
“From Ashcombe Hall,” I replied. “And since they are preparing a room for me there, I thought I would settle one small matter before retiring. I may not pass this way again for many months.”
“With whom?” she asked, leading me into the parlour.
“With Mr. Grey,” said I.
She paused and looked at me curiously.
“Then nobody has told you?”
“Told me what?”
“Mr. Grey has been dead these four months.”
I stared at her.
“Dead? Impossible! I saw him less than a year ago.”
“Aye, and a different man he was then. Sit down. You shall hear the whole story. Have you supped?”
“No.”
“Then let me bring something. You look tired enough.”
Before I could object, she disappeared. From the kitchen I heard old Matthew grumbling that “folk nowadays had no respect for decent hours, nor for decent company either.” Mrs. Barton ignored him entirely and returned with a tray carrying bread, cheese, and a steaming cup.
“You must eat while I talk,” she said.
I complied, and she began her account.
Not long after you departed, she said, I was sent for at Willow House. The summons surprised me, but I went willingly, for Miss Clara’s sake. I scarcely knew her when I arrived. She had grown pale and thin, and though she greeted me warmly, there was a sadness in her face that had not been there before.
Mr. Grey explained little. He merely informed me that Clara was to spend most of her time under my care. I was given the morning room, and she was encouraged to remain there with me. At first she appeared content. We arranged books, embroidery, and various little amusements, and for a while I believed we might pass the season pleasantly.
Yet her spirits soon changed. She disliked confinement and grew restless as summer approached. The grounds around the house were extensive, but she was forbidden to leave them, and restrictions of any sort were intolerable to her nature. Whenever I was occupied elsewhere, she complained bitterly of solitude.
There was another resident of the house, young Edward Hale. In the beginning she scarcely acknowledged him. If he entered a room, she would leave it; if he spoke, she answered only when politeness required. Edward, for his part, seemed perfectly willing to maintain the distance. He spent his days reading, working, or staring into the fire, speaking little to anyone.
Over time, however, Clara altered her approach.
One evening she watched him sitting silently beside the hearth.
“Do you suppose he ever grows tired of thinking?” she asked me. “Or perhaps he never thinks at all.”
Edward made no reply.
“Look at him, Mrs. Barton. He sits there hour after hour. If I were condemned to such a life, I should lose my senses. Tell me, Mr. Hale, what occupies your mind? Do you dream of adventures? Of distant countries? Or nothing at all?”
Still he said nothing.
“He heard me,” she continued. “I know he did. He frowned. That proves he possesses some spark of life.”
“Miss Clara,” I interrupted, “if you continue provoking him, you’ll soon regret it.”
At that Edward glanced up from his book, though whether in annoyance or amusement I could not tell.

27. That the rise and fall of nations are governed by divine providence rather than by chance or the favor of many gods.
If, therefore, kingdoms flourish or decline, not through the power of fortune but by the will of the supreme God, it follows that no earthly dominion exists apart from His judgment. Men frequently attribute prosperity to luck, to destiny, or to the favor of various deities; yet these names merely conceal their ignorance of the true cause. For God alone distributes power, establishes rulers, and determines the seasons in which nations grow strong or weak. These judgments are often hidden from human understanding, because our sight is confined to the present moment, whereas His wisdom comprehends the whole order of ages.
Nor should anyone suppose that worldly success is itself the highest good. Wealth, authority, and military strength are bestowed upon both the righteous and the wicked. Were they reserved only for the virtuous, the weak-minded might serve God merely in hope of temporal reward. By granting such things indiscriminately, He teaches that they are not the proper object of devotion. True blessedness belongs only to those who possess wisdom and justice, and this blessedness cannot be taken away by the loss of earthly possessions or political power.
28. Concerning a certain people who prospered under the worship of the one God and declined when they abandoned Him.
To make this truth more evident, let us consider a nation that was once small and insignificant, yet rose to great strength under the guidance of the one God. It was not through the aid of many divine patrons that they increased, nor by ceremonies directed toward separate gods of fertility, harvest, war, or navigation. Their fields yielded crops, their families multiplied, and their enemies were overcome without the multitude of rites that other nations considered necessary.
They crossed rivers without invoking river gods, harvested grain without offerings to guardians of the fields, and obtained victory without believing victory itself to be a goddess. The blessings they received came not from a divided host of celestial powers, but from the single source from which all good things proceed. What others sought through countless ceremonies, they obtained through obedience.
Yet prosperity did not preserve them from error. As often happens among men, abundance produced pride, and pride gave birth to curiosity concerning foreign customs and strange forms of worship. Gradually they turned away from the laws that had sustained them and trusted in inventions of their own making. Their decline followed, not because the power that had raised them failed, but because they abandoned the wisdom by which they had been governed.
Even their later misfortunes served a purpose. Scattered among many lands, they became witnesses to ancient prophecies and preserved records that testified to events long foretold. Thus what appeared to be punishment also became an instrument of providence, demonstrating that divine purposes often extend beyond the understanding of those through whom they are accomplished.
Having treated this matter sufficiently for the present discussion, we shall reserve the remaining questions for a later examination, lest the length of this discourse exceed proper measure.

AI-

LADY MARGARET. Indeed, for less than half the kingdom some women would hazard much. But look, who approaches?
Enter Lord Steward.
STEWARD. Good morrow, ladies. Have I interrupted matters of great secrecy?
ELIZABETH. Nothing, my lord, that would reward your curiosity. We were speaking of our mistress and lamenting her troubles.
STEWARD. A charitable occupation, and fitting for gentlewomen. Yet take comfort. Affairs may still turn toward a happier conclusion.
ELIZABETH. Heaven grant it.
STEWARD. Your compassion does you credit, lady. And since I find you in such virtuous disposition, I am charged to deliver a message. His Majesty has spoken favourably of you. In recognition of your merit, he intends to raise you to the title of Countess of Wexford, with revenues suitable to the dignity.
ELIZABETH. My lord, you leave me uncertain how to answer. Such honour exceeds anything I have deserved. I possess no gift equal to so great a kindness, except my gratitude and humble prayers for the King's prosperity.
STEWARD. I shall faithfully convey those sentiments.
Aside.
She carries herself with admirable grace. It is no wonder the King regards her so highly. Fortune has curious habits; today's attendant may become tomorrow's ornament of the realm.
Aloud.
Farewell, lady.
ELIZABETH. My thanks, my lord.
Exit Steward.
LADY MARGARET. There! Do you see how the world proceeds? I have spent twenty years pursuing favour at court and have worn out more shoes than I care to count. Yet fortune barely nods at me. Meanwhile you arrive, and honours are placed in your lap before you have learned how to refuse them.
ELIZABETH. It all seems unreal.
LADY MARGARET. Unreal? Does it taste unpleasant? A title, an income, the King's notice? I have never heard anyone choke on such things.
ELIZABETH. You make sport of me.
LADY MARGARET. Because the matter invites it. A countess today. Who shall say what comes tomorrow? If ambition were a ladder, I suspect you have already set foot upon the first rung.
ELIZABETH. I wish you would leave me out of these predictions. They bring me no comfort.
LADY MARGARET. Then they bring you more modesty than most courtiers possess.
ELIZABETH. The Queen suffers enough already. I would not add to her grief by becoming the subject of idle speculation. I beg you, speak of this to no one.
LADY MARGARET. Have no fear. My tongue can keep a secret when it chooses.
Exeunt.

SCENE IV. A Great Chamber of State
Trumpets sound.
Enter Officers, Clerks, Bishops, Lords, and Attendants in solemn procession. The KING sits beneath a canopy. Two CHANCELLORS sit below him as judges. The QUEEN is seated apart.
FIRST CHANCELLOR. Since our authority has been confirmed by the council, let the proceedings begin in proper order.
KING. Must every formality be repeated? The commission has already been read before all concerned. Let us proceed to the matter itself.
FIRST CHANCELLOR. As Your Majesty wishes.
CLERK. Call the parties before the court.
CRIER. Let all who are summoned attend and answer.
SECOND CHANCELLOR. Let silence be observed throughout the hall.
The assembly grows quiet.
KING. We are present. What remains?
FIRST CHANCELLOR. Only that justice may be seen as well as done. The record shall show that all parties stand before this tribunal by lawful authority.
QUEEN. I trust, then, that fairness shall accompany authority.
FIRST CHANCELLOR. Your Grace shall have every opportunity to speak.
KING. Let no man imagine the outcome settled before the hearing. We sit here to discover truth, not to pronounce it beforehand.
A murmur passes among the lords.
SECOND CHANCELLOR. Then let the examination begin.
The court prepares to proceed.

“It is a relief to move about after sitting still for so long,” said Miss Harrington.
Charlotte agreed readily, though she suspected there was more civility than necessity in the proposal. The true purpose, however, was not entirely unsuccessful. Mr. Ashford, who had been absorbed in a volume near the fire, raised his eyes from the page. The movement in his direction had not escaped him. After a moment he closed the book and listened.
“You must join us,” said Miss Harrington.
“I fear I cannot,” he replied. “Your walk appears to be founded on one of two intentions, and my presence would spoil either.”
The remark instantly awakened curiosity.
“What intentions?” cried Miss Harrington. “You must explain yourself.”
Charlotte smiled.
“Do not encourage him. He wishes to provoke us, and the surest punishment is to let him keep his secret.”
But Miss Harrington was not so easily discouraged.
“No, indeed. I insist upon an explanation.”
“With pleasure,” said Mr. Ashford. “Either you have chosen to pace the room because you have confidential matters to discuss, in which case I should be an intrusion; or you imagine that walking displays you to greater advantage than sitting, in which case I can admire the exhibition much better from where I am.”
“How intolerable!” exclaimed Miss Harrington. “What shall be done with him?”
“There are many forms of revenge,” said Charlotte. “Ridicule is among the easiest. Surely you know enough of his character to laugh at him effectively.”
“I assure you I do not,” replied Miss Harrington. “One cannot easily laugh at a person who refuses to provide material. Mr. Ashford is entirely too composed.”
“Then he enjoys a rare privilege,” said Charlotte. “Few people are safe from ridicule. I confess I value a laugh too highly not to employ it whenever I can.”
“You give ridicule too honourable a place,” said Mr. Ashford. “The wisest actions may be made absurd by someone determined to see nothing else.”
“True,” said Charlotte, “but I hope I am not one of those people. I do not laugh at wisdom. Folly, however, is another matter. Contradictions, vanities, little absurdities—those are fair game. And I imagine you possess none of them.”
“No person is entirely free from weakness.”
“Vanity, perhaps?”
“Certainly vanity.”
“And pride?”
“Pride, if governed by reason, is less dangerous than vanity.”
Charlotte turned aside to conceal a smile.
“Your investigation seems complete,” said Miss Harrington. “What judgment have you reached?”
“That Mr. Ashford considers himself remarkably free from defects.”
“Not at all,” said he. “I know several of mine. My temper, for example, is not especially forgiving. I do not easily forget injuries, nor do I readily alter an opinion once formed. Some would call me stubborn.”
“That is indeed a fault,” Charlotte answered. “An unwillingness to forgive is never attractive. Still, it is not a fault I find amusing, so you remain secure.”
“I suspect everyone has some natural weakness.”
“And yours is a tendency to think poorly of people.”
“And yours,” he replied with a faint smile, “is a determination to misinterpret them.”
Before Charlotte could answer, Miss Harrington rose abruptly.
“Someone must play the pianoforte,” she declared. “I am exhausted by conversations in which I contribute so little.”

CAPTAIN. Speak boldly to their marshal. Tell him this:
We are no soldiers dressed for festival,
But men acquainted with the dust of roads.
Our cloaks are stained with weather, mud, and toil;
The bright devices that adorned our ranks
Have long since faded under wind and rain.
Yet let him mark this well: though garments fail,
Our spirits keep their edge. Before this sun
Has left the field, my men have sworn to me
They shall wear finer colours than they do now,
Or strip them from the backs of those opposed.
If fortune grants us victory, as I trust,
Then ransom will be gathered easily.
Return no more with offers or demands.
I pledge no payment save these weary limbs,
And if they fall into their enemies' hands,
The gain will hardly justify the trouble.
HERALD. I shall report your answer faithfully.
Farewell, good captain.
Exit Herald.
CAPTAIN. Yet I suspect we have not seen the last of him.
Enter LORD PEMBROKE.
PEMBROKE. My lord, I ask the honour of leading the first assault.
CAPTAIN. The honour is yours.
Now forward, gentlemen.
The day belongs to Heaven.
Exeunt.
SCENE IV. A Battlefield
Alarum. Enter RALPH, a captured Enemy Officer, and THOMAS, a young attendant.
RALPH. Surrender, rogue!
OFFICER. Good sir, have mercy.
RALPH. Mercy? That depends upon your purse.
What are you called?
OFFICER. I am Captain Duval.
RALPH. A captain! Then your value rises.
Young Thomas, ask him whether he can pay.
THOMAS. Sir, my master wishes to know what ransom you offer.
OFFICER. Whatever is reasonable. Only spare my life.
RALPH. Hear him! He bargains already.
Tell him I am a fierce and dreadful warrior,
And not a man to be trifled with.
THOMAS. I shall tell him no such thing.
RALPH. Insolent boy! Do as you are bid.
THOMAS. Very well.
To Officer.
He says you should be frightened.
OFFICER. I am frightened enough.
RALPH. Good. Then mention a number.
OFFICER. Two hundred crowns.
RALPH. Two hundred?
The offer improves.
Ask whether he possesses more.
THOMAS. Do you?
OFFICER. Yes, if my family contributes.
RALPH. Excellent.
Inform him his life is spared.
THOMAS. He says he will release you.
OFFICER. Then I thank him sincerely.
I shall remember his generosity.
RALPH. What does he say?
THOMAS. He calls you generous.
RALPH. A just description.
Come, prisoner, follow me.
Exeunt Ralph and Officer.
THOMAS remains.
THOMAS. I have never witnessed so much noise proceeding from so little courage.
He boasts as though he had conquered kingdoms,
Yet trembles whenever real danger appears.
There are humbler men in the army
Who possess ten times his bravery
And not a fraction of his words.
Exit.

“Enough of that,” he muttered. “We shall speak again.”
He left abruptly. A strange agitation possessed him—part triumph, part dread. His pulse raced; every nerve seemed strained. For a few moments he felt almost invigorated, as though some hidden spring of energy had been released. Yet the sensation faded almost immediately, leaving behind exhaustion so profound that each step became an effort.
The man he had left behind remained seated long after the conversation had ended. He stared at the table, absorbed in thought. Something in their exchange had unsettled him. A conclusion, vague before, now appeared obvious.
“No,” he said at last to himself. “That cannot be right. Everyone has judged him too hastily.”
Meanwhile, as Victor descended the stairs, he nearly collided with his friend Leon, who was rushing up them.
For several seconds they simply stared at one another.
“You!” Leon burst out. “So this is where you've been!”
His astonishment quickly gave way to anger.
“I've searched half the city. Do you hear me? Half the city! I went back to your room twice. I questioned your landlady. I nearly dragged your servant into the street demanding answers. And here you are strolling about as if nothing has happened.”
Victor looked at him wearily.
“I wanted to be left alone.”
“Alone?” Leon repeated. “You can barely stand. Look at yourself! You're pale as death.”
“Let me pass.”
“No.”
He seized Victor by the arm.
“You disappear without a word, you worry everyone who knows you, and then you tell me to let you pass?”
Victor's expression hardened.
“Why is it so difficult for you to understand? I do not want your concern. I did not ask for it. Every kindness feels like a chain. Every conversation exhausts me. You call it friendship. To me it feels like persecution.”
Leon released his arm, more from surprise than willingness.
Victor continued, speaking faster now.
“You all insist on helping. None of you stop to ask whether your help is wanted. Perhaps I would rather be miserable. Perhaps I would rather fail. Perhaps I simply wish to be left in peace. Why can no one accept that?”
His voice rose despite himself.
“You suffocate me. Every visit, every question, every expression of sympathy. Let me alone. For once, let me alone.”
The last words came out almost as a cry.
Leon stood silent.
Then, unexpectedly, his anger subsided.
“Well,” he said at length, “go where you please.”
Victor turned away.
“Wait.”
Leon pointed a finger at him.
“You people are all alike. Every disappointment becomes a tragedy. Every inconvenience becomes a philosophy. You lock yourselves inside your own heads and stare at your troubles until they grow enormous.”
Victor did not answer.
“No, listen. I mean it. If something hurts you, you polish the wound until it shines. Then you admire it. You call that depth. I call it vanity.”
His face reddened with the force of his irritation.
“And another thing. You're all determined to be original, yet you imitate one another perfectly. The same complaints. The same despair. The same speeches about being misunderstood.”
A reluctant smile almost crossed Victor's lips.
Leon noticed it and grew even louder.
“Don't laugh! I'm serious. This evening I'm hosting a gathering. My uncle is there already receiving guests because I ran out looking for you. If you had a grain of sense, you'd come. Sit among living people for once. Talk nonsense. Eat badly. Stay too late. Anything would be better than wandering the streets arguing with your own thoughts.”

A rocky shore. SERAPHEL, exiled Queen of Valdris, stands apart. Her spirit LUMEN attends her.
SERAPHEL. They pass me by unknowing — eyes made blind By guilt and sea-salt both. Go, Lumen, fetch My crown and robes from out the cave. [Exit Lumen.] I'll shed this beggar's guise and stand once more As once I stood in Valdris. Swift, good spirit; Thy service nears its end.
Lumen re-enters, singing, and bears the robes.
LUMEN. Where the wind turns, there turn I, Through the hollow reeds I sigh; Where the river meets the sea, There shall I at last run free. Merrily, merrily shall I fly Past the sun and past the sky.
SERAPHEL. My faithful Lumen! Thou shalt have thy freedom; But first, one final office: go unseen Unto the vessel anchored in the bay, And wake the captain from his enchant'd sleep. Bid him attend us here.
LUMEN. I move like thought, and am returned Before the tide can turn a stone. [Exit.]
DUKE ALDRIC. What strangeness holds this coast? Some power bends The air about us. Heaven, be our guide!
SERAPHEL. Look well upon me, brother. I am she Whom you displaced — Seraphel, rightful queen. No phantom this: reach out and take my hand, And feel the warmth of living blood beneath.
ALDRIC. Thou canst not be — thou wert three years at sea, Or drowned — thy pulse — it beats — what sorcery Hath kept thee? Or do I, with fever'd mind, Conjure the face I wronged?
SERAPHEL. No conjuring. The isle hath sheltered me, and taught me patience. That crown which you usurped — I do require it, Though not in malice ask I its return; For I have found, in exile's bitter school, That power without pardon is a prison.
MONTARVO. [Aside.] My conscience wrings me. She was ever just.
SERAPHEL. [Aside to Montarvo.] Thou too wast party to it. I have known These three years past. Yet look, I hold no blade Above thee: thou shalt have thy peace from me If peace is what thou seek'st.
MONTARVO. [Aside.] The devil's mercy — no, it is a saint's.
ALDRIC. If thou art truly she, then tell me how Thou cam'st to live? We watched the tempest break Thy ship to timber. I — I grieve that sight More than thou know'st. My crown sat heavy on me Each morning after.
SERAPHEL. I believe thee, Aldric; And grief is a beginning. I was cast Upon this shore, and found it strange and kind. I shall not now rehearse these years entire — The tale would swallow daylight whole. Know this: I lived, I learned, I lost things dear to me As thou hast too, I warrant. We are both Made lesser by these years, and yet more whole. Take back my hand. This rocky court hath been My throne-room long enough. Let us go home.

Now having the better part of two days to pass in Gloucester before the coastal packet would take me further north, the immediate question pressing upon me was where I should find a bed and a meal at a price my circumstances could honestly sustain.
