The night was raw and wet, the kind that doesn't announce itself with any honest downpour but simply saturates everything by slow degrees, until a man finds his collar soaked without ever having felt a drop fall. I knew nobody in Gloucester. My funds, such as they were, I had already reckoned twice over in my coat pocket as I walked, and the sum did not improve with repetition. Well then, said I to myself, stopping beneath the overhang of a chandler's shop and watching the rain bead on the cobblestones, the matter is simple enough: you want a roof, Nathaniel, and four walls, and something warm in a bowl, and you want them cheap. Pride has no lodgings to offer you tonight.
I made my way along the main street first, as a man will do who knows he cannot afford the main street. The Anchor and Crown had lamplight spilling handsomely from every window and the sound of fiddle-playing within—too handsome entirely, and I moved along. Further down stood the Mariner's Rest, whose sign depicted a sailor in a hammock of improbable comfort, and from whose open door proceeded a smell of roasting meat so persuasive I had to address myself sternly and keep walking. These establishments were for men with warmer pockets than mine. The cobblestones turned uneven underfoot where the frost had buckled them, and my left boot, whose sole had been making silent but unmistakable complaints for a fortnight, now began to admit the wet.
I turned instinctively toward the water, reasoning as I had always reasoned in such ports, that cheapness follows the smell of brine. The streets narrowed. The lamplight thinned to almost nothing. Here were buildings that leaned toward one another overhead as if sharing confidences, and underfoot the ground was uncertain—rope-ends, a broken oar, the dark slick of fish-scales on stone. On one corner I found what I took for an inn, pushed open the door, and discovered instead a room full of men engaged in some sort of dispute over a net, who looked up at me without welcome and then looked away again without curiosity. I withdrew.
At last, near the waterfront proper, I came upon a narrow building wedged between a rope-walk and a warehouse, with a board hanging above its door on a rusted chain. The board bore, or had once borne, some sort of painted device—a fish, perhaps, or an anchor—but the weather had long since arbitrated the question in favour of blankness. Beneath it, in letters still just legible: Harmon's—Beds and Supper. No flourishes. No promises of comfort. The building itself listed slightly to one side, as though decades of Atlantic wind had been pushing at it from the northwest and it had simply accommodated itself to the pressure. The single window showed a light so faint it might have been a candle guttering in a back room, or a man's pipe-glow, or merely the memory of light. It had the look, altogether, of a place that expected nothing from you and therefore offered honest value—which is to say, it had the look of the very place I wanted. The door, when I tried it, opened without ceremony.

Drums and alarums. KING MALDREN enters with soldiers. His mother, LADY EDWYNN, intercepts him. LADY CORNYS, widow of Lord Hest, stands apart.
LADY EDWYNN. Stay, son, and hear me.
KING MALDREN. Mother, step aside. The field will not be kept by women's tongues. Beat drum!
LADY EDWYNN. Beat drum and thou wilt drown my words, But not my grief, which is too deep for drowning.
KING MALDREN. I am in haste.
LADY EDWYNN. Ay, ever hast thou been. In haste to enter on this wretched world And fill it with thy purposes and ruin. Thou wert not born, but launched, as men launch stone From engines built for breaking—
KING MALDREN. Peace, good madam.
LADY EDWYNN. Good? What goodness hast thou left in thee That I should scruple to withhold my curse? Thy boyhood was a catalogue of harms, Thy youth a sharper chapter, and thine age Is the whole book bound up in blood and burning. What gentle hour, what moment of thy life Hath earned thee one soft prayer from out my lips?
KING MALDREN. You nursed me once. Claim what is owed on that.
LADY EDWYNN. The breast that fed thee pays its debt in sorrow. Go then, thou, and carry hence my curse As thou wouldst carry armour to the field— It shall weigh heavier than steel, and slower Drag thee toward thy end. The prayers I offer Fly to the other side, and there take arms Against thee with the men thou goest to meet. Those souls that loved thee once and found thee false Cry out before the throne for thy destruction. Go, Maldren. Thou art built of harm, and harm Shall be the house wherein thy life is ended. [Exit.]
LADY CORNYS. She speaks for all the dead and half the living. I have not her years nor yet her fury, But every word she spoke, I seal with mine.
KING MALDREN. Lady, stay. There is a matter between us.
LADY CORNYS. There is no matter left. My lord is gone. My sons are gone. What matter couldst thou raise That grief hath not already swallowed whole?
KING MALDREN. You have a daughter, fair and worthy, Cornys.
LADY CORNYS. And wouldst thou make her instrument of peace? Then take her not — I'll mar what thou wouldst use; I'll say she was not Hest's but gotten basely, I'll clothe her beauty in such slander's rags No crown could ever find occasion for her.
KING MALDREN. Wrong not the girl; her blood is good and known.
LADY CORNYS. That very blood turned poison in my sons, And they were of that selfsame lineage. Their birth was noble — see what good it brought them.
KING MALDREN. Their stars were crossed at birth; 'twas destiny.
LADY CORNYS. 'Twas thou. Dress murder how thou wilt in stars, The hand that loosed the bolt was thine in theirs. No fate decreed it but the will of one Who wore the outward show of kinsman's love And inward harboured nothing but his own. Thou talkest destiny to stop my mouth As thou didst stop theirs — quietly, and early, Before they grew enough to speak against thee. But I am grown, and grief hath made me bold, And I will speak though every drum thou hast Beat on until they split.
KING MALDREN. Madam, as I hope To prosper in this war and come home crowned, My intentions toward your daughter and yourself Are kinder far than all you have yet known!

"Civil words!" she cried, turning on her husband with a look that would have felled a less composed man than myself to witness. "If you had not the spirit to stand your ground, you should have remained in your study and saved us all the performance. It is precisely what I expected—precisely! You will persist in reading weakness as virtue and call it patience, and then wonder that the world treats you as it does. No, I shall not open it—not for you, not for anyone! I have spent the better part of a year smoothing over your quarrels and flattering your vanity, and this is my reward—two fools united in blaming the only person in the house who possesses an ounce of sense!"
Frederick grew very white. He was not a man who managed rage with any dignity; it drained out of him and left something worse in its place, a kind of cold, shaking helplessness that I always found more alarming than an outburst. He set his hand on the doorframe and said nothing for a moment, and Marian watched him with her chin raised and her eyes bright with something between contempt and a wild satisfaction.
"Your husband," said Ravenswood, with a short laugh, moving from his position by the window, "appears to be rehearsing his death-scene. Shall I applaud, or wait for the interval?" He looked at her as he said it rather than at Frederick, which told me everything I needed to know about the state of matters between the three of them.
What happened next I could not have predicted, though perhaps I should have. Frederick, who had seemed on the verge of some silent collapse, crossed the room in three steps and struck Ravenswood across the jaw with a force that surprised us all, Ravenswood included. He staggered back against the sideboard; the decanter went over, and glass spread across the floorboards in a bright fan. For a moment nobody moved.
"Get out," said Marian—not to Ravenswood, but to her husband. Her voice had gone flat and strange.
Frederick walked out. We heard his feet on the stairs, steady and deliberate.
Ravenswood straightened, touched his jaw, and said nothing. His eyes had gone very dark. I knew that look, and I wanted no part of what it promised.
"He'll have gone for Dawson and the groom," I said quickly, which was not entirely a falsehood, for it seemed probable enough. "There are three of them at least, and you would do well to be gone before they find you here with a smashed decanter and a reason to use it against you."
Whether he believed me or not, he went—through the garden door, without ceremony or farewell.
Marian sat down heavily in the armchair and pressed both hands to her temples. "Lock that door, Joanna," she said, without looking at me. "And if you find Leonard before I do, tell him I am unwell and not to be disturbed tonight." She paused. "I am not unwell, you understand. But I intend to be, if it comes to it. He will come up here with his grievances all in order and his feelings freshly sharpened, and I cannot answer for myself if he does. Tell him I am sleeping. Tell him I was taken faint. Tell him whatever will keep him at the other end of the corridor until I have had an hour to think." She looked up at me then, and the brightness had gone out of her face, leaving something raw and exhausted underneath. "You don't think it my fault, Joanna? You saw how it began—you know I had no intention of any of this."

CAVENDISH. For this at least: that we who draw our swords Do so in a cause our hearts can answer for. Come what may after.
Enter a Soldier, breathless.
SOLDIER. My lord, make ready. Lord Pemberton advances with his van.
CAVENDISH. Good. He spares me the concluding of a speech I had no art to finish. Mark me, friends: Words are the trade of peacetime. Here is mine.
He draws.
Let every man acquit himself as well As his worst enemy would fear he might. Strike hard. Strike clean. And if we do not meet Again in such a fellowship as this— Why, we have met today, and that must serve.
Trumpets. They embrace and exeunt.

The field between the armies. Alarums. Enter LORD FENWICK and Sir THOMAS CRAY.
CRAY. Who are you, sir, that dog me through this field As though my death were all your business here?
FENWICK. My name is Fenwick. I pursue thee, Cray, Because they point at thee and say: the King.
CRAY. They point at truth.
FENWICK. Then truth shall serve my sword As well today as falsehood ever did. The Earl of Mortham fell not half an hour past, Thinking he struck at thee. I'll not mistake. Yield, or the field shall have thee.
CRAY. Not from me. I have not lived a king to die a prisoner.
They fight. Cray is slain. Enter Cavendish.
CAVENDISH. Well struck, my lord. Is't done?
FENWICK. Aye, here he lies. The King himself, at last.
CAVENDISH. The King? Look well. I know that face, and it is not the King's. This is Sir Thomas Cray — a valiant man, Accoutred so like royalty that God Himself might pause before He told the difference.
FENWICK. They dressed him like a king and sent him out To draw our swords and spend our time upon him?
CAVENDISH. The King keeps many images in the field. Strike one and two more rise.
FENWICK. Then I shall strike Each one in turn until the true face shows. I'll work my way through every coat he owns.
CAVENDISH. Work quickly then. Our line still holds, but barely.
Exeunt. Alarums. Enter GREYWICK alone.
GREYWICK. I'll confess it freely: I am not well. I have been in three places on this field today where sensible men were not. My company— God rest what's left of it—are scattered to the hedgerows and the ditches hereabouts, finding religion, no doubt, in the mud. I myself have done extraordinary things. Extraordinary. I shall remember them when I am far from here and safe.
He sees a body.
Ah. Sir Thomas Cray. There's a lesson in thee, friend. Thou put the King's own colours on thy back, and see what honour bought thee — a quiet lie in a field that nobody will name correctly in any ballad sung a year from now. Honour! I have thought the matter over at some length today, and mostly from positions of considerable personal discomfort, and I find it a commodity best viewed from the outside, at a prudent distance, like a fire that warms the room but need not be embraced directly.
Enter Lord Pemberton's son, young PEMBERTON.
YOUNG PEMBERTON. Greywick! Why stand'st thou here? Give me thy sword — I've snapped mine at the hilt. Three of Lord Fenwick's captains yet are loose and doing damage we shall all regret. Thy sword, man — quickly!
GREYWICK. My sword is — I am — the blade has a particular stiffness today that I cannot account for.
YOUNG PEMBERTON. Give it me!
GREYWICK. Here then — though I warn thee it has grown unaccustomed to the work.
YOUNG PEMBERTON. What's this? This is a — is this a flask?
GREYWICK. It is medicinal. The case looked similar. An easy error. Any man might—
YOUNG PEMBERTON. Is there a battle in which thou art not ridiculous?
He hurls it at Greywick and exits.
GREYWICK. If Fenwick still breathes, I shall find him — possibly. If he finds me first, I shall endure it nobly. There is a kind of honour even in surviving, though I grant you no one writes songs about it. Fortune, thou art a woman, and as such I have always found thee best approached with patience, flattery, and modest expectations.
Exit.

Another part of the field. Alarums. Enter KING EDWARD, young PEMBERTON, and LORD MARSHAL.
KING. Pemberton, thou'rt hurt — fall back, I prithee. Lord Marshal, see him clear of this press.
LORD MARSHAL. Not while I stand, my liege, unless I bleed as freely as he does.

Their wits thus clouded, and their terrors keen, They stumbled through the thickets wild and green; Each shadow seemed a beast, each branch a hand, And nothing answered as the frightened planned. I drove them deeper, laughing as I flew, And left sweet Cobweb tangled in the dew. So when the sleeper stirred beneath the oak, 'Twas on the strangest face that she first woke.
SILVANUS. Better than craft could compass or contrive! But hast thou touched the other pair with love, As my instruction bid thee from above?
SPRITE. The man I found in sleep beneath the thorn, And marked his eyes before the break of morn; The girl beside him — she, when he shall rise, Must be the very first to meet his eyes.
Enter Celia and Florian.
SILVANUS. Draw close. This is the man we mean to mend.
SPRITE. The girl is right. The man is the wrong friend.
FLORIAN. Why dost thou punish me with such cold scorn, Whose love for thee hath ever faithful born? Reserve such sharpness for an enemy, And turn, I beg thee, softer eyes on me.
CELIA. I chide thee mildly now; I could do worse, For thou hast given me cause enough to curse. If thou hast stolen Silvio from my side Or done him harm while I unknowing lied In sleep beside him — speak! The sun doth keep Less faithful to the day than he to me. That he would leave me, I no sooner trust Than that the sea might turn to summer dust. Thy face confesses it — that hollow look, Like one who knows too well the deed he took.
FLORIAN. That hollow look is grief of thy unkindness, And thou the murderer of my heart's blindness. Yet thou look'st bright as any morning star, As one whose conscience carries not a scar.
CELIA. What do I care for that? Where is he gone? Wilt thou restore him, or shall I mourn alone?
FLORIAN. I'd sooner give his shadow to the wind.
CELIA. Thou hound! Thou faithless, hollow-hearted hind! Hast thou then slain him — tell me, once, speak plain! From this day forth be numbered not with men again! A coward's hand might do the thing awake; Thou did'st it sleeping, for a coward's sake. No serpent struck so basely in the dark, And none had quite so forked a tongue to mark.
FLORIAN. Thou pour'st this anger on a groundless fear. I have not harmed him — know I know not where. Nor can I swear if he be dead or whole.
CELIA. Then swear to me he lives. That much I'll take.
FLORIAN. And what is that worth, given for thy sake?
CELIA. That thou shalt never look upon me more. And so I'll take my leave and shut that door — Alive or dead, he is no care of thine.
Exit.
FLORIAN. Her fury makes all following unwise. I'll rest then here beneath these clouded skies. For grief compounded upon grief doth grow Too heavy for a waking man to know; Sleep is the one poor coin that sorrow spends To lighten debt before the dark night ends.
He lies down.
SILVANUS. What hast thou done? Thou hast undone my plan, And set the love-drops on the wrong-loved man. Of thy misreading must it follow then That true love wanders false and back again.
SPRITE. What wonder? Fate decrees that for each heart Held true, a thousand others fall apart.
SILVANUS. Go sweep the wood more swiftly than the gale, And seek out Rosalind, whose cheeks grow pale With love's own fever — sighing out her days, Her colour spent in unrequited ways. Bring her by seeming here before my art Can plant a new dream in this sleeping heart.
SPRITE. I fly, I fly — no arrow parts the air With half such speed as I go hunting there.
Exit.
SILVANUS. Blossom dipped in sorrow's dye, Struck through by a careless sky, Settle softly in his eye. When his true love passes by, Let her seem to glorify All the stars that light the night — Wake to her and make her right.
Enter Sprite.
SPRITE. Master of our moonlit ring, Rosalind is near — I bring Also him who should not be, Pledging love on bended knee. Shall we stay and watch this scene? Lord, what fools these hearts have been!
SILVANUS. Peace — the noise will rouse the sleeper here.
SPRITE. Then shall two suits clash upon one ear, And a doubled wooing make a single sport. These tangled mortals are the finest sort: Give me a muddle well and truly made, And I am happiest in its motley shade.

BARDOLPH. Ha! What madness grips thee, thou hollow-cheeked rogue? Dost thou dare stand before me and speak of honour, thou that hast none? Away! The very stench of thy pretence turns my stomach.
EVANS. I do beseech you most earnestly, thou scabrous and pestilent fellow, at my entreaties and my urgings and my solemn requests, to take up and consume, look you, this onion. Because, look you, you do profess to have no love of it, and your humours and your constitution and your digestion do not welcome it, therefore I do most particularly desire you to eat it.
BARDOLPH. Not for Merlin and all his prophecies.
EVANS. There is a prophecy for you. [Strikes him.] Will you be so gracious, thou mangy wretch, as to eat it?
BARDOLPH. Thou wretched cur, I shall have satisfaction.
EVANS. You speak with great confidence, thou festering knave, and so you shall, when Providence ordains it. Until such hour I would have you remain among the living and consume your vittles. Come, here is somewhat to season it. [Strikes him.] You called me yesterday a hill-dwelling pedant; but I will make you today a scholar of the lowest form. I urge you, fall to; if you can deride an onion, you can devour an onion.
FORD. Hold, hold; you have humbled him sufficiently, sir.
EVANS. I say I will see him eat the better portion of this onion, or I will beat his skull for a fortnight together. Chew, I pray you; it is sovereign remedy for your fresh wound and your bloody foolish head.
BARDOLPH. Must I chew?
EVANS. Yes, certainly and without doubt or disputation or any manner of ambiguity whatsoever.
BARDOLPH. By this onion, I will have a terrible and prolonged revenge. I eat, I eat, I do swear it—
EVANS. Eat, I entreat you. Will you have somewhat more to accompany your onion? There is not onion enough here to swear upon credibly.
BARDOLPH. Stay thy stick; thou seest plainly that I eat.
EVANS. Much good may it do you, scurvy knave, and heartily so. Nay, waste nothing; the outer skin is wholesome for your cracked pate. When hereafter you encounter onions in your travels, I pray you show them all reverence. That is the sum of my instruction.
BARDOLPH. Agreed.
EVANS. Ay, onions are good. Here, take this coin toward the mending of your head.
BARDOLPH. A coin! To me!
EVANS. Yes, truly and in honest faith you will accept it; else I have a second onion about my person which you shall likewise eat.
BARDOLPH. I take this coin as earnest pledge of my revenge.
EVANS. If I stand in your debt, I will discharge it in blows. You shall deal in timber, and purchase nothing from me but cudgels. God be with you, and preserve you, and restore your broken skull. [Exit.]
BARDOLPH. The very stars shall hear of this outrage.
FORD. Go, go; you are a dissembling and cowardly rascal. Will you make sport of an ancient and honourable custom, observed out of reverence and carried as a worthy emblem of valour long past, and yet not venture to make good in your actions one syllable of your boasting? I have witnessed you sneering and jeering at this gentleman on no fewer than three occasions. You supposed that because he could not command the English tongue with native ease, he was therefore unable to command an English stick. You have found your error; and let a Welsh lesson henceforth teach you what an English gentleman ought to be. Fare you well. [Exit.]
BARDOLPH. Does Fortune make a fool of me at last? Word comes to me that Meg is dead in the hospital, carried off by the pox; and my last refuge is utterly destroyed. I grow old, and from these weary bones all dignity has been beaten out. Well, I shall turn to roguery, and lend my hand to picking pockets on the road. To England will I creep, and there commit my petty thefts; and I shall patch these beaten scars and swear before all men that I earned them fighting on the fields of France. [Exit.]

He remembered someone had suggested they hand him over to the authorities at once. He could not bring back the face of whoever had said it, and even now he did not know who it was, but he remembered turning and making some reply.... So that was the explanation of last night's terror. The most appalling thought was that he had very nearly destroyed himself — had come within a breath of ruin — over something so utterly insignificant. So this man could report nothing beyond his asking about the apartment and the traces of blood. So Ilya Petrovitch likewise had nothing but that delirium, no real evidence, only this psychology which proves nothing either way, nothing definite. And if no further facts emerged (and they must not, they absolutely must not!) then... then what could they do to him? How could they convict him, even supposing they arrested him?
And Ilya Petrovitch had only just now learned about the apartment and had not known of it before.
"Was it you who told him... that I had been there?" he asked suddenly, struck by a thought that came on him all at once.
"Told who?"
"The examining officer. The one in charge of the inquiry."
"Yes. The yard-keepers would not go themselves, so I went alone."
"Today?"
"I arrived two minutes before you did. And I was there — I heard everything, how he pressed you."
"Where? How? When?"
"In the adjoining room. I sat there the whole time."
"What? Then it was you — the surprise he had prepared? But how is such a thing possible? Good God!"
"I could see the yard-keepers had no intention of doing as I asked," the man began; "too late now, they said, and he may take it badly that we did not come sooner. I was troubled by it and could not sleep, and so I began to make my own inquiries. Yesterday I found out where I needed to go, and this morning I went. The first time he was not there. An hour later he could not receive me. I came a third time and they let me through. I told him everything, exactly as it had happened, and he began pacing the room and striking himself on the chest. 'What do you mean by this negligence? Had I been told sooner, I would have taken him then and there!' Then he ran to the door, called someone, spoke with him in a low voice in the corner, and then turned back to me — questioning me, reproaching me. He reproached me at considerable length. I told him everything, and told him too that you had not ventured a single word in answer to me the day before, and that you had not known me. He began pacing again, striking himself on the chest, working himself into a fury, back and forth across the room — and when they announced your arrival he sent me into the next room. 'Wait there,' he said. 'Sit still and do not stir, whatever you may hear.' He placed a chair for me himself and locked the door. 'I may have need of you,' he said. And after Nikolay had been brought in, he let me out as soon as you had gone. 'I will call for you again,' he told me."
"And did he speak with Nikolay while you were still there?"
"He sent me out beforehand, as he had sent you out, before he turned to Nikolay."
The man stood without moving, then bent down suddenly once more, touching the floor with his fingertips.
"Forgive me for the evil I thought of you, and for the wrong I did you with my words."
"God will forgive it," said Mikolayev. And as he spoke, the man bowed once more — not so deeply this time — then turned without haste and left the room.
"It proves nothing either way, nothing either way," Mikolayev repeated to himself, and he stepped out into the street with more certainty than he had felt in days. "Now we shall see who prevails," he said under his breath, a cold and bitter smile crossing his face as he descended the stairs. The bitterness was directed entirely at himself; he recalled his moment of near-collapse with something close to contempt.

PART V
CHAPTER I
The morning after the disastrous scene with Varya and her mother worked a certain sobering effect on Andrei Filippovich. Disagreeable as it was to face, he was obliged, gradually and against every inclination, to accept as a settled and unalterable fact what had seemed to him only the evening before an absurd impossibility. The wounded serpent of his vanity had been moving through him all night, slow and persistent. Rising from his bed, Andrei Filippovich went immediately to the mirror. He was half afraid of what he might find there — that his face might have yellowed in the night, that the night's damage might show in his skin.

"One can't be expected to copy documents without a decent pen. And the ink they give us is barely fit for pigs."
"Don't quarrel with your bread and butter."
"What bread and butter? They pay next to nothing for copying. What good is it?" he answered, reluctantly, as though the words were meant more for himself than for her.
"And I suppose you want to be rich all at once?"
He looked at her in an odd way. "Yes," he said, after a short pause, with unexpected firmness. "I do."
"Well, don't look at me like that, you'll frighten me to death. Do you want me to bring up the soup or not?"
"As you like."
"Oh — I nearly forgot. Something came for you this morning while you were sleeping. A letter."
"A letter? For me? From whom?"
"How should I know. I paid two copecks to the boy who brought it, out of my own pocket. You'll give it back?"
"Bring it here, for heaven's sake — bring it at once!" cried Volkov, suddenly agitated. "Bring it now!"
A moment later she placed it in his hands. He saw at once what it was: a letter from his sister, from the town of K——. He felt the colour leave his face. It had been a very long time. But beyond the simple shock of it, something else moved in him too, something he could not immediately name.
"Fedosya, go — please — here are your two copecks, but go, go quickly, please."
The letter trembled in his fingers. He could not bring himself to open it while she was still in the room; he needed to be entirely alone with it. When at last her shuffling steps had faded down the corridor, he raised the letter to his lips and held it there for a moment. Then he turned it over and studied the address — the careful, upright hand of his sister, each letter formed with that particular deliberateness he had known since childhood. He hesitated. Some obscure dread seemed to hold him back. Then he broke the seal.
It was a substantial letter, three sheets covered on both sides in a close and careful hand.
"My dear Aleksei," his sister had written, "it is nearly three months since I last wrote to you, and I know that must have caused you worry, though I hope you will not think too harshly of me for my silence once you have read everything I have to tell you. You know I think of you constantly. You and mother are everything to me — my one certainty in all this uncertainty.
"I was very sorry to learn, through mother's last letter to me, that you have had to abandon your position at the office and that the private tutoring has also come to nothing. I cannot tell you how it grieved me. My own situation here has not permitted me to send you anything, though God knows I have wished to. I earn thirty roubles a year in this household, of which I was advanced twenty when I first arrived, and as you will understand, I have been paying that back a little each month and am only now nearly clear of it. The Starovoitovs are not unkind people, on the whole, but they are careful with money as only provincial merchants know how to be, and there is little room to ask for more than what was agreed.
"But now, dear Aleksei, I have something to tell you which I hope will be, at least in part, good news. I am not to remain here much longer. The arrangement is coming to an end at Michaelmas, and I find I am not sorry for it. There are things I have not told you — things I did not want to put into a letter, not knowing whose hands it might pass through before reaching you — but I will tell you everything now, plainly and in order, so that you may understand what the past months have truly been like and why I kept silent about them for so long.
"When you wrote in the spring asking whether all was well, and saying you had heard certain things from Petrov — I scarcely knew how to answer you. Had I told you the truth, I am quite sure you would have done something rash. I know your nature, Alyosha. You would not have stayed where you were. And what would have become of your own situation then? I could not be the cause of that. So I told you only that things were tolerable, which was true enough, up to a point.
"But now I can tell you everything."

LEONTES. I gave her that ring with my own hand — it was the first token I ever placed on her finger.
CAMILLO. I cannot speak to that; but such a ring — and I am certain it was hers — I saw upon Polixenes this very morning, worn openly on his hand.
LEONTES. If it be that—
CAMILLO. If it be that, or any jewel once belonging to her, it joins with all the rest against her.
LEONTES. O, that the traitor had a thousand lives to spend! One death is nothing — nothing to appease what burns in me! Now is the veil torn from it. Mark me, Camillo — all my tender love I fling from me, up to the cold air. It is gone. Rise up, black hatred, from thy sunless pit! Love, yield thy place, thy throne within my breast, to iron revenge! Fill, heart, fill — for the poison is in thee now, and it is of the deadliest sort.
CAMILLO. Yet hold yourself in patience.
LEONTES. No patience — there is blood in this, Camillo, blood!
CAMILLO. I urge you — wait. Your mind may alter when the heat has passed.
LEONTES. It shall not alter. As the great northern river in the depths of winter drives its frozen current without pause or turning, heedless of the rocks and narrow straits, pressing forward to the open sea — so do my thoughts run now with that same cold compulsion, forward, always forward, and shall not retreat to the soft ground of love until a full and sweeping vengeance has consumed them utterly. Now, by this silent sky above me, I bind myself with sacred words. [Kneels.]
CAMILLO. Do not yet rise. [Kneels.] Hear it, you watching stars, you vast and encompassing heavens — hear that here Camillo pledges all his cunning, all his hands, all the loyalty of his heart, to the service of a wronged king. Command me, and to obey shall be my only purpose, be the work never so dark or terrible. [They rise.]
LEONTES. I receive thy pledge not with empty gratitude but with full and earnest acceptance, and will set thee to work this very hour. Before three days are out, let me hear that Polixenes breathes no more.
CAMILLO. The man is as good as dead — say only the word. But the woman — let her live.
LEONTES. Curse her — false, corrupted creature! Curse her! Come, walk with me, and I will find the means to deal swiftly with the fair deceiver. From this hour thou art my right hand.
CAMILLO. And thine I am, now and beyond all ending. [Exeunt.]

SCENE III. Sicily. The outer courtyard of the palace. Enter Hermione, Paulina, and a Servant.
HERMIONE. Sirrah, do you know where Lord Archidamus lodges at present?
SERVANT. I dare not say he lodges anywhere comfortable, my lady.
HERMIONE. Why not?
SERVANT. A man in his position, my lady — to say precisely where he lies at any hour would be to lay oneself open to contradiction, and contradiction is a quarrelsome bedfellow.
HERMIONE. Enough of that. Where does he keep his rooms?
SERVANT. To tell you where he keeps his rooms, I must needs know where he keeps himself, and that, my lady, is a thing that shifts with the wind.
HERMIONE. Can you find nothing useful to say?
SERVANT. I can say this with confidence: he is somewhere within the walls of Sicily, and that narrows it considerably.
HERMIONE. Go find him. Tell him I have spoken in his favour and that he should come to me directly.
SERVANT. That much lies within the compass of my legs, if not my wit, and I will set about it. [Exit.]
HERMIONE. Paulina, I cannot think where I set down that brooch.
PAULINA. I could not say, madam.
HERMIONE. I would sooner have lost a purse of gold. And yet — were my lord other than he is, were there any shadow of suspicion in his nature, so small a thing might be enough to kindle it. But he is not made of that stuff.
PAULINA. Is he entirely free of jealousy, then?
HERMIONE. Him? The very climate that formed him burned all such weakness out. He is not capable of it.
PAULINA. See — he comes this way.
Enter Leontes.
HERMIONE. I shall not stir from here until I have secured him on Archidamus's behalf. My lord, how do you do?
LEONTES. Well enough, my lady. [Aside.] God, what torment it is to wear a pleasant face. — And you, Hermione?
HERMIONE. Well, my lord, I thank you.
LEONTES. Give me your hand. [Takes it.] It is warm.
HERMIONE. It has known neither age nor grief enough to cool it.
LEONTES. Warm hands speak of a warm and open nature. Very warm. This hand of yours, Hermione, stands in some need of discipline — of fasting, of cool reflection, of the curb of devotion. There is a spirit in it that does not easily submit.
HERMIONE. You may say so if you will. It was that same hand, after all, that gave you my heart.
LEONTES. A generous hand indeed. Once, a heart given was a heart kept. Now it seems the hand moves faster than the heart can follow.

O Fabian, If thou keep faith, I shall resolve this doubt, Forsake my father's house and be thy spouse. [Exit.]

SCENE IV. The same. A street. Enter Valentine, Fabian, Curio, and Sebastian.
FABIAN. Nay, we must slip away while they sit at table, change our dress at my lodging, and be back again before the second course is cleared.
VALENTINE. We have scarce thought the business through.
CURIO. We have said nothing yet of who shall carry the lights.
SEBASTIAN. It is a shabby thing unless it be neatly contrived, and in my judgement better left alone than done in this disorder.
FABIAN. It lacks but five o'clock — we have time enough, if we use it.
Enter Gobbo with a letter.
Friend Gobbo, what have you there?
GOBBO. If it shall please you to break the seal, it will make itself known well enough.
FABIAN. I know this writing — in good faith, it is a fine hand, and fairer than the paper is the hand that penned it.
VALENTINE. Love's business, by the look of it.
GOBBO. By your leave, sir—
FABIAN. Where do you go?
GOBBO. Marry, sir, to bid my old master the moneylender to dine tonight with my new master, who is a gentleman of some Christian virtue and considerable appetite.
FABIAN. Here, take this. Tell sweet Isabetta I will not fail her — speak it to her alone and to no one else. Go, gentlemen—
[Exit Gobbo.]
Will you make ready for this evening's entertainment? I have settled the matter of the torch-bearer.
CURIO. Ay, I'll be about it presently.
SEBASTIAN. And I the same.
FABIAN. Come to Valentine's lodging within the hour, the both of you.
CURIO. That we shall. [Exeunt Curio and Sebastian.]
VALENTINE. Was that letter from Isabetta?
FABIAN. I must tell you everything. She has set out exactly how I am to bring her from her father's keeping, what money and jewels she has made ready, and what disguise she has prepared. If ever her father in his closeness and his cold suspicion finds any mercy shown to him, it will be on her account entirely, and for no desert of his own. Misfortune would want a sharp excuse to light on one so gentle. Come, read as we walk — Isabetta herself shall bear our light tonight. [Exeunt.]

SCENE V. The same. Before the house of Mosca, a usurer. Enter Mosca and Gobbo.
MOSCA. Well, thou shalt see it with thine own eyes — see the difference between a house that is kept with order and one that is squandered away in foolishness. What, Lisetta! Thou shalt not eat me out of house and home as thou hast lately taken to doing — What, Lisetta! — sleeping till midday and wearing out good linen and leaving candles to burn to nothing.
GOBBO. What, Lisetta!
MOSCA. Who asked thee to call? I did not bid thee call.
GOBBO. Your worship has always told me I am fit for nothing unless I am told what to do.
Enter Lisetta.
LISETTA. You called? What is it you want?
MOSCA. I am bidden to supper, Lisetta. There are the keys. And yet why should I go? They do not want me — they flatter me to my face and mock me behind my back. But I'll go all the same, if only to see what that spendthrift sets on his table. Look to the house while I am gone. I go against my better judgement. Something sits ill with me tonight — I dreamed last night of my strongbox, and not pleasantly.
GOBBO. I beseech you, sir, go. My young master does expect your arrival, and has spoken of it.
MOSCA. As I have spoken of his.
GOBBO. There is entertainment planned as well. I will not say outright there is to be a masque, but if there is, it is no mere accident that my left eye began twitching on St. Crispin's eve at the very stroke of midnight, which fell that year upon a Wednesday that had been a fast day for three years running.
MOSCA. What — a masque, do you say? Hark you, Lisetta — bolt the shutters, and when you hear the drum begin and the shrieking of those infernal pipes, do not go climbing up to peer out of the windows, nor push your head into the street to gape at fools with painted faces. Shut the house up, stop its ears. Let none of that shallow nonsense in. I swear I have no stomach for feasting tonight, and yet I'll go. Go ahead of me, sirrah, and tell them I am coming.
GOBBO. I go before, sir. [Aside to Lisetta.] Mistress, keep one eye on the street regardless. There will pass by tonight someone well worth a young woman's notice. [Exit Gobbo.]
MOSCA. What was that the rascal was muttering?
LISETTA. Only "farewell, mistress" — nothing more.
MOSCA. He is not a bad fellow at bottom, but he eats like three men, profits like none, and sleeps away half the working day besides. I keep no drones in my house. I have already found him a new place — with a master who, I trust, will let him eat his way through a borrowed fortune at his leisure.

The cold night air steadied me somewhat, and gathering what resolve I could, I made my way toward the low light burning in the window of the dwelling. I knocked softly upon the door.
"Who is there?" called a voice from within — an old voice, careful and unhurried.
"A stranger," I replied. "One who means no harm and asks only a moment's shelter."
"Come in, then."
I entered. The room was small and warm, lit by a single lamp. An elderly man sat by the hearth, his hands resting on his knees, his eyes turned toward me without seeing.
"Forgive me for disturbing your evening," I said. "I have been walking a long while and am very tired. If you would permit me to sit by your fire for a short time, I would ask nothing more."
"Sit, sit," said the old man — Vernet, as I later learned he was called. "I am sorry I can offer you little else. My daughter has gone to the village and will not return before dark, and as you may perceive, I am not well placed to be of great practical use to a guest."
"You have already given me more than you know simply by opening your door. I have food enough — it is rest and a little warmth that I need, nothing more."
He nodded, and for a time neither of us spoke. I felt the moments passing with terrible weight, knowing that what I had to do could not be long delayed — and yet I could not find the beginning of it. At last the old man spoke first.
"You have an unusual manner of speaking," he said, not unkindly. "I cannot place you. Are you from this country?"
"No. I was raised in circumstances that gave me your language, but I come from nowhere that has a name I could give you. I am on my way to find certain people — people who are very dear to me, and from whom I hope, though I am not certain, to receive some welcome."
"They are relations?"
"Not by blood. They are the only beings in the world toward whom I feel anything that might be called attachment. I have no others. I have never had others. And these — they do not know me as I truly am, and I fear that when they see me, all my hopes will end in a single moment."
The old man was quiet for a while. "That is a hard thing to carry," he said at last. "But you must not give way to despair before the moment itself arrives. I have lived long enough to know that the worst we imagine seldom falls out exactly as we dread it."
"You are kind to say so. But my case is not an ordinary one. These people have cause to fear what I am — or what they believe me to be. They have been told things, or have formed impressions, that stand entirely against me. And when they see me face to face, that impression will not be softened. It will, I am afraid, be confirmed in the most terrible way."
"And yet you intend to go to them."
"I must. I have no other hope in the world. And I have not been idle in my waiting — for a long time, longer than they know, I have done what small good I could for them, quietly and without any acknowledgement. I had hoped that this might count for something. I had hoped that kindness, even when its source is unknown, leaves some trace in the heart that receives it."
"I believe that it does," said Vernet slowly. "I believe that deeply. Goodness recognised or not is never entirely without effect." He turned his unseeing face toward me. "You say these people live nearby?"
"Very near."
He was silent again. Then: "If you were to tell me the whole of your situation — plainly, and withholding nothing — it is possible I might be of some small use to you. I cannot see your face, and so I must judge you by your words alone. But there is something in the way you speak that makes me trust you. I am not a man of any influence or resource — I have known exile and poverty — but if I could do anything to ease your way, I would do it gladly."
Something swelled in me that I had not felt before — or had felt so rarely that I no longer recognised it. I could not at once speak.
"You cannot know," I said at last, "what it means to hear those words. I have lived so long without them. I will tell you everything — I will hold nothing back — and I will trust in your goodness as I have not yet been able to trust in anyone's."
"Then tell me. But first — these friends of yours. Will you tell me their names?"
I stopped. Here it was — the moment I had been moving toward for so long, through cold and darkness and loneliness beyond any telling. I tried to answer. I formed the words in my mind and brought them forward, but they dissolved before they reached my tongue. The effort of it undid me entirely. I felt myself sink back into the chair, and a sound came out of me that I could not stop — something between a cry and a sob, raw and without dignity.
And at that moment I heard footsteps on the path outside.

"You were watching me... from the corner of the street?" Svidrigailov said at last, in a voice that was almost too composed. The other man offered no reply, nor did he glance in his direction. The silence between them stretched long and heavy. "Why follow me here... and then stand there saying nothing... what is it you want?"
Svidrigailov's voice faltered slightly at the edges. The other man turned then and fixed upon him a look of such cold and steady contempt that Svidrigailov felt it like a hand pressed flat against his chest.
"Thief," the man said quietly. The word was unhurried, almost gentle in its delivery, but it landed with extraordinary precision.
Svidrigailov continued walking. His feet carried him forward by some automatic function wholly separate from his will. A strange numbness spread through his hands and up into his jaw. They walked on together for perhaps two hundred paces, neither speaking, the man keeping always at the same distance from him, like a shadow with a separate intention.
"I don't understand you," Svidrigailov said at last. "You use that word as though you witnessed something."
"I did," the man replied with calm and unhurried certainty. Then he stopped, turned down a narrow side street, and was gone.
Svidrigailov stood at the entrance to the lane for a long moment. A cab rolled past. A woman with a basket crossed at the far end of the road. The city continued around him with total indifference.
He returned to his rooms in a state he could not properly name. It was not fear exactly—he had always prided himself on his relationship with fear, on having looked at it steadily enough to rob it of authority. No, it was something more unsettling than fear. It was the sensation of having been accurately read, as one reads a letter that was not meant to be found.
He sat at his desk without lighting the lamp. The room grew slowly darker around him. He thought of nothing with any continuity—there came instead a disordered procession of images, faces without names, a garden in summer, a corridor in some house he had visited years ago and never thought of since, the sound of water somewhere below, a child's shoe lying in mud. The images did not distress him. They moved past with a kind of dreary indolence.
After some time he lit a cigarette and sat smoking in the dark.
"A man who saw," he said aloud to no one. "But what exactly did he see?"
He turned the question over with methodical patience, the way one might dismantle a mechanism to understand why it had stopped. The man's face had been composed. Not the face of someone acting on impulse or private grievance—a face that had already concluded something, had long since passed through whatever agitation the knowledge had initially produced and arrived now at some settled and implacable resolution.
That was the detail that troubled him. Not the accusation itself, which might be managed, which might be traced back to its source and handled. It was the composure that troubled him. It suggested something finished, something already set irrevocably in motion.
He pressed the end of the cigarette against the ashtray and watched the ember die. "I should have been more careful," he said, in a tone neither of regret nor self-reproach, but simply of accurate retrospective assessment. "Though of course I was never the sort to be careful." He almost smiled. "That was always the difficulty."

GRUMIO. I told the steward that your uncle was in Florence, And that you looked for his arrival here by Thursday.
FABIAN. Thou art a clever rogue; take that for thy trouble. But here comes old Orsino. Look lively, man.
Enter Orsino and Valentio.
Good Signior Orsino, well encountered, sir. (To the Merchant) This is the gentleman of whom I spoke at length; I prithee, play the father to me here, And grant me fair Sophia for my wife.
MERCHANT. Gently, son. Sir, by your leave: I came to Verona On business of some weight concerning debts, When my son Valentio brought to my attention The tender and most earnest cause of love Between your niece and his most willing heart. And for the good report that travels with you, And for the love my son doth bear the lady, I am resolved, as any careful father, To see him settled well and honestly. If you shall like the terms as well as I, You'll find me neither difficult nor cold; I come prepared to hear and to agree, And give my blessing freely to the match.
ORSINO. Sir, hear me out, for I must speak my mind. Your honest manner and plain speech please me well. It is most true my niece Sophia here Doth love your son, or both do counterfeit With more than ordinary art and patience. If then you mean to deal as fathers deal— With substance, settlement, and honest care— And pass Sophia a sufficient dower, I am content, the match is sealed and done: She shall be his, and with my full consent.
FABIAN. I thank you, sir. But where shall we confirm These articles and set our seals upon them, That both sides may be satisfied and bound?
ORSINO. Not in this house; you'll understand me, sir. Walls are not always walls, and servants hear Far more than one would wish. Besides, old Curio Is forever loitering near where he's not wanted.
FABIAN. Then at my lodgings, if it please you well: My father rests there; we shall do the business Quietly tonight, without interruption. Send for Sophia; let my boy go fetch A scrivener who can set the matter down. The worst is only this: at such short notice Our supper may be somewhat plain and spare.
ORSINO. That suits me well enough. You, Feste, go— Ride home at once and bid Sophia prepare, And if you will, acquaint her with the news: Valentio's father is arrived in Verona, And she is like to be Valentio's wife.
VALENTIO. With all my heart I pray the heavens grant it!
FABIAN. Leave off your prayers, man, and get thee gone. Signior Orsino, shall I show the way? You're welcome, though our table may be modest— We'll make it right when next we meet in Padua.
ORSINO. I follow you.
Exeunt Fabian, Merchant, and Orsino.
GRUMIO. Valentio!
VALENTIO. What is it, Grumio?
GRUMIO. Did you mark how Fabian winked and smiled upon you just now?
VALENTIO. I marked it well. But what of it?
GRUMIO. Nothing of great consequence, only I have been left standing here to untangle the knot of his signs and gestures, if I can.
VALENTIO. Then untangle it, by all means.
GRUMIO. Thus then: Orsino is at this moment contentedly occupied in conversation with a false father on behalf of a son who is not what he claims.
VALENTIO. And what follows?
GRUMIO. His niece is to be brought by you this evening to supper.
VALENTIO. Go on.
GRUMIO. The chaplain at Saint Ambrose's chapel is entirely at your disposal and keeps no particular hours.
VALENTIO. And what is the sum of all this?
GRUMIO. I cannot say with any certainty, except that the business upstairs is a counterfeit settlement designed to occupy all interested parties. You, meanwhile, should secure your own settlement—take the lady to the chapel, take the priest, take a witness or two of good reputation. If that is not the thing you've been wanting, I have nothing further to offer, and you may bid Sophia a long and final farewell.
(Moving to go.)
VALENTIO. Wait, Grumio—
GRUMIO. I cannot linger. A woman I once knew was married on a Tuesday afternoon while she was sent to the kitchen garden to pick mint for a sauce; and there is no reason you should fare any worse. My master has sent me ahead to Saint Ambrose's to have the priest standing ready by the time you arrive with your company.
Exit.
VALENTIO. I will, and gladly, if she is willing. She will be willing; why should I doubt it? Come what may, I'll go to her directly— It shall go hard if I come home without her.
Exit.

Before the house came into view, the last of the evening had already withdrawn behind the treeline, leaving only a pale residue of light along the horizon; but the lane was bright enough beneath the full moon, and I could make out the ruts in the mud and the Queen Anne's lace trembling at the verge. The gate stood unlatched—someone had been through it recently, or perhaps it was simply never fastened anymore—and I passed through without difficulty. The garden had been attended to since my last visit: I noticed the smell first, something sweet and almost embarrassingly abundant, roses perhaps, or honeysuckle climbing the near wall, and the grass on either side of the path had been cut low. The windows were lit and standing open to the warm air, their curtains lifting at intervals, and though the evening was mild there was a fire somewhere within—I could see its red suggestion at the far end of the hall—and the smell of woodsmoke mingled pleasantly with the garden.
I did not go in directly. Something in the arrangement of the light and the sound of voices checked my step, and I remained on the path a moment, not quite willing to announce myself.
There were two of them in the room nearest the window. A young woman sat in the larger chair with her feet drawn up and a book open but neglected in her lap, and a man—older than her by perhaps a decade, with a dark, careful face—sat across from her on a low stool, his elbows on his knees, watching her with an attention he may not have known was visible. They were arguing, lightly, in the way people argue when they are more interested in the other person than in the argument.
"You said yesterday you did not mind it," the woman said.
"I said I did not mind it then. That is a different thing entirely."
"You are always making these distinctions."
"One of us ought to."
She laughed, and the sound came clearly through the open window to where I stood, and I felt that particular variety of loneliness that is not quite sadness—something closer to the sensation of arriving late to a place one had expected to belong to.
I went around to the back, as I had always done, and found the kitchen door open and old Mrs. Alderton at the table with her mending, spectacles down on her nose, a tallow candle burning steadily beside her. She looked up at the sound of my step and her expression passed through surprise, pleasure, and reproof in quick succession.
"Lord above, Mr. Ashford—walking in like a ghost with no word sent ahead! I might have put you up at the Lamb and Flag without any trouble if only I'd known. As it is I can't say what state the room is in."
"I shan't need much," I told her. "I mean to leave again by Thursday. I only came to see how matters stood. How long have you been keeping the house?"
She set down her mending and folded her hands in the manner of someone who has a considerable amount to say and is deciding where to begin.
"Since Michaelmas," she said. "After the other woman went—you'll have heard about that, I expect—and the new arrangement was settled. It's a different sort of place now than when you were last here, Mr. Ashford. Very different."
I drew out the chair opposite and sat down, and she rose to put the kettle on without my asking, which I thought was a good sign.
"Tell me everything," I said.

Nay, upon my honour, purely for the occasion. Sir, there is newly arrived to this city one Silvano; believe me, a most accomplished gentleman, replete with every fine distinction, of very gracious address and remarkable presence. To speak of him with any justice, he is the very map and measure of courtly virtue; in him you will discover the full sum of whatsoever quality a gentleman might covet.
PRINCE. Sir, your account of him loses nothing in the telling, though to anatomise him point by point would exhaust the patience of a scholar and still fall short of his full extent. But in the earnest spirit of praise, I hold him a person of extraordinary composition, and his qualities of so rare and peculiar a stamp that his only true likeness is his own reflection, and any who would follow his example must be content to walk in shadow.
CORVINO. Your highness does him perfect justice.
PRINCE. But what is the substance of it, sir? Why do we dress the gentleman in such laboured ceremony?
CORVINO. My lord?
BENEDETTO. Is it not possible to arrive at a meaning by a shorter road? You will manage it, sir, I am certain.
PRINCE. What is the occasion for naming this gentleman at all?
CORVINO. Of Silvano?
BENEDETTO. His vocabulary is quite spent. Every fine word he had is gone.
PRINCE. Of him, sir.
CORVINO. I trust your highness is not unacquainted with the excellence of Silvano—
PRINCE. I would not dare claim it, lest I be thought to rival him; and besides, to truly know another man requires one to know oneself, which is a longer undertaking.
CORVINO. I speak, my lord, of his skill with the blade specifically; in that particular, by the estimation of those best qualified to judge, he stands without an equal.
PRINCE. What arms does he favour?
CORVINO. Sword and main-gauche.
PRINCE. Those are two arms. Proceed.
CORVINO. The Duke, my lord, has laid against him a wager of four Neapolitan chargers, to which Silvano has opposed four Venetian blades with their full appointments—belts, chapes, and all manner of furnishings, three of which are, I confess, extraordinarily fine, very sympathetic to the hand, and of most ingenious and liberal workmanship.
PRINCE. What do you call the furnishings?
BENEDETTO. I suspected we should need a gloss before it was over.
CORVINO. The furnishings, my lord, are the belt-fittings and mountings.
PRINCE. The word might suit the matter better if we were equipping a siege. Let us say fittings and have done. But continue—four Neapolitan horses against four Venetian blades and their appointments: that is the southern stake against the northern. What is the precise condition of the wager?
CORVINO. The Duke, my lord, has proposed that in a course of ten passes, Silvano shall not surpass your highness by more than two hits. He has set the measure at ten against eight, and it would come to trial directly should your highness consent to take the field.
PRINCE. And if I decline?
CORVINO. My lord, I speak only of your appearance in the contest itself.
PRINCE. Sir, I shall remain here in this gallery. If it please the Duke, this is my accustomed hour for exercise. Let the weapons be brought, let Silvano be agreeable, and let the Duke maintain his intention, and I will do my best on his behalf. If I fall short, I lose nothing but my credit and the margin of hits.
CORVINO. Shall I carry this answer back to the Duke?
PRINCE. To that effect, sir; with whatever ornament your custom requires.
CORVINO. I offer my most devoted service to your highness.
PRINCE. Yours, entirely.
Exit Corvino.
He does well to advertise his own devotion; there is no one else who would do it for him.
BENEDETTO. The man hatched before he was ready and has been running about with the shell on his brow ever since.
PRINCE. He was making courtly speeches before he could properly walk. And there are a dozen more of the same flock whom this age admires beyond reason—fellows who have caught nothing but the pitch and fashion of the time, a frothy assemblage of manners with no substance beneath. Set them to any genuine trial and they dissolve like foam.
Enter a Servant.
SERVANT. My lord, the Duke commends himself to your highness by way of Corvino, who has reported that you await him in the gallery. He desires to know whether your intention to meet Silvano remains fixed, or whether you would have more time appointed.
PRINCE. My purpose does not change. I am at the Duke's disposal.

Methinks your belly, like a clock well-wound, Should toll the hour and draw you home to meat Without a boy to bear you notice of it.
LEONATO OF MILAN. Come, Balthazar, these quips are poorly timed; Keep them for some occasion fit for mirth. Where is the letter that I gave to thee?
BALTHAZAR OF NAPLES. What letter, sir? You gave no letter me.
LEONATO OF MILAN. Come, come, have done with playing at confusion, And tell me faithfully where you have put it.
BALTHAZAR OF NAPLES. My charge was only this: to bring you straight From the exchange to supper at the Dolphin. Your cousin and his wife do wait within.
LEONATO OF MILAN. Now, by my faith, I'll have a plain account Of where you've stowed the paper in your keeping, Or I shall find a way to jog that memory That grins and plays the fool when I am vexed. Where is the letter that I trusted you?
BALTHAZAR OF NAPLES. I have some marks of trust upon my back From errand-running and from being cuffed, But no such letter as you speak of, sir. If I had kept it, I should be kept poorly, For letters bring me nothing but more labour.
LEONATO OF MILAN. What cousin speaks of waiting? Whose relation?
BALTHAZAR OF NAPLES. Your worship's own, sir, lodging at the Dolphin, Who fasts from supper till your worship come And sends me thrice an hour to fetch you in.
LEONATO OF MILAN. What, dost thou mock me to my face again? Take that for thy impertinence, thou rogue.
BALTHAZAR OF NAPLES. What would you, sir? For pity, hold your hand! Nay, if you will not, then I'll use my legs.
Exit Balthazar.
LEONATO OF MILAN. Upon my soul, there is some villainy here, Some trick by which the rascal has been gulled Or practised this confusion on himself. They say this city swarms with subtle knaves, With forgers, liars, and dissembling rogues, With cutpurses that work in open day, And whispering cheats that take you by the sleeve. If it be so, I'll not linger long. I'll to the Dolphin, seek this fellow out, And satisfy myself my letter's safe.
Exit.

ACT II, SCENE I. A room in the Dolphin.
Enter Beatrice, wife to Leonato of Naples, and her younger sister Marta.
BEATRICE. Neither my husband nor the boy come back— That boy I sent in haste to find his master? The bells have struck, Marta, past two o'clock.
MARTA. Some merchant of the city may have kept him. The exchange draws men aside at every corner. Good sister, let us eat and think no more of it. A man goes where occasion calls him out, And time itself is all the rein they know; When it suits time, they'll come. Be easy, sister.
BEATRICE. Why should their ease be greater far than ours?
MARTA. Their business ever leads them out of doors.
BEATRICE. When I stay out, he takes it very ill.
MARTA. He is the governor of your own will.
BEATRICE. None but a fool submits to such a bit.
MARTA. But headstrong will must suffer for it yet. There's nothing under heaven's broad canopy But owns some bound in air, on land, or sea. The hawk, the hound, the horse that bears his load, Are subject to the hand that holds the goad. And man, that prides himself the lord of these, The master of the land and of the seas, With reason clothed and with dominion crowned, Is still by nature's ordinance duty-bound To hold his place, and woman hers beside; Then let thy will lie down and cease to chide.
BEATRICE. Such doctrine keeps you from the wedding-pew.
MARTA. Not doctrine, sister—what I've seen men do.
BEATRICE. Were you once married, you'd assert your part.
MARTA. First I would learn submission by my art.
BEATRICE. And if your husband wandered wide abroad?
MARTA. I'd hold my tongue until he crossed the threshold.
BEATRICE. How serene! And easy to be still When you have never suffered against your will. A soul that groans beneath a heavy yoke Is told to hush by those who bear no load; But set the same weight on those patient shoulders And patience thins to nothing, I will warrant. You, with no faithless mate to wound your pride, Bid me be calm while sorrow knocks inside; But live to know such usage as I've known, And this pet virtue will be overthrown.
MARTA. I may be wed one day, and put it to the test. But look—your servant comes, and with him, I think, news.

Like men possessed they hauled and heaved, every muscle cracking with the effort, until the cry rang out—"Now, Queequeg!—drive it home!" The lance was thrown. "Back water!" The oars bit hard in reverse; and in that same instant something scorched and singing swept the length of every man's forearms. It was the line, alive and running. A breath before, Flask had whipped three fast turns of it round the loggerhead, and from those turns, by the violence of its speed, a thin grey smoke now twisted up and dissolved into the salt air. Where the line burned round the post it burned also through both of Flask's palms, the wrapped cloth he had bound there having worked loose in the confusion and fallen away. It was the sensation of gripping a wire stretched to breaking—not pain exactly, not at first, but a heat so immediate and absolute it registered as something beyond ordinary feeling.
"Water on the line! Water!" Flask shouted, and the nearest man flung his bailing can across the thwart and sloshed a gout of sea over the smoking hemp.
More wraps were taken. The line steadied. The boat leapt forward with a violence that seemed to lift her clean from the surface, throwing spray in continuous white sheets from her bows while the stern sucked and churned in her wake. Flask and Queequeg shifted positions—a desperate, lunging exchange in that pitching, heaving shell of a boat, each man passing the other in a space scarcely wide enough to stand. From stem to stern the line ran rigid as iron, and the whole craft shuddered along its length at every surge; a man might have believed she had no keel at all but was simply a thing of tension, held to the water's surface by the angle of her strain.
Every man gripped his thwart with both hands and said nothing. The spray came constantly, the bow dipping and throwing and dipping again, and ahead of them the dark shape of the whale moved through the swells like a travelling hill, immense and purposeful and apparently inexhaustible.
Then, gradually, the pull began to slacken.
"Haul in—haul in!" Flask called forward, and the bowsman began working hand over hand, and the others bent to their oars, and foot by foot the boat closed with the whale until they came up along his side, close enough to see the texture of the scarred hide, the barnacles, the slow heave of his breathing. Flask braced his knee against the forward thwart and drove his lance again and again into the rolling flank, the boat backing and ranging and backing again at each command, working always at the angle that kept them clear of that tremendous, lethal tail.
The blood came freely now. It ran in dark streams down the whale's sides and spread behind them on the water in a great stain that the afternoon light turned a deep, terrible copper. The men's faces caught that reflection from the surface; they looked at one another across the thwarts and each face was red, as though they laboured not by daylight but by the light of something burning. The whale's breath came in great, convulsive jets—white vapour shot upward at long intervals, each eruption followed by a silence that felt like suspension—and Flask breathed with it, almost, his own exhalations coming hard and fast as he worked.
At last the great animal began to slow in earnest, to wallow rather than run, and Flask leaned out over the bow and set the long lance with both hands and drove it deep, working it with slow, deliberate turns as a man might work a probe into a wound—searching, careful, unhurried now that the moment had finally come. He sought the life that lay deepest in the animal, the centre of it, the place from which nothing could be recalled.
He found it.
The whale convulsed with a violence that was almost beyond dimension—a rolling, churning detonation of blood and water that obliterated everything within thirty yards of it—and the boat drove back hard through the churned white chaos, every man holding on with closed eyes, until they broke through into the clear air beyond and the sea behind them was a spreading stain, quiet now, the long business finished.

Margaret had scarcely recovered from her surprise before another occasion for reflection presented itself. Mr. Hartwell, whose reserve had once appeared almost insurmountable, now conversed with her aunt and uncle with an ease that seemed entirely free of embarrassment. She had expected politeness, perhaps; but this degree of attention exceeded anything she had anticipated.
As her aunt introduced Mr. Benson as her brother, Margaret watched Mr. Hartwell closely. She could not resist observing whether the disclosure altered his manner. For an instant he appeared startled; yet the expression vanished almost as soon as it came. Instead of retreating into silence, he addressed Mr. Benson with renewed civility and soon engaged him in a discussion concerning the surrounding countryside.
Margaret listened with increasing satisfaction. Her uncle acquitted himself admirably. His observations were sensible, his remarks well chosen, and his good humour never descended into familiarity. Every answer he gave seemed to strengthen the favourable impression she hoped he might produce.
The gentlemen soon fell into conversation about improvements to the estate. Mr. Hartwell spoke at length of several recent alterations to the grounds and invited Mr. Benson to inspect them at his leisure. He even offered the use of certain maps and plans prepared by his surveyor, should they be of any interest.
Mrs. Benson exchanged a glance with her niece, expressing surprise without speaking. Margaret returned only a faint smile. She herself could scarcely account for what she witnessed.
Why had he changed so much? His former reserve had softened into something almost approaching openness. She could not persuade herself that such a transformation had occurred on her account. Their previous disagreements hardly seemed sufficient to produce so marked an alteration.
As they continued their walk, a slight rearrangement of the party occurred. Mrs. Benson, finding herself somewhat fatigued, accepted her husband's arm, leaving Margaret beside Mr. Hartwell. For a few moments neither spoke.
At length Margaret remarked that his presence had been wholly unexpected. She had understood, from several sources, that he was not expected at the estate until the following day.
Mr. Hartwell explained that business with his steward had required an earlier arrival than originally intended.
“The remainder of our party will arrive tomorrow,” he said. “Among them are several persons already known to you.”
Margaret inclined her head but offered no immediate reply.
After a brief pause he continued.
“There is one among them, however, whose acquaintance I particularly hope you will make. My sister has heard much of you and would be pleased, I believe, to meet you during your stay here.”
The request took her entirely by surprise. She scarcely knew how to answer. Whatever desire his sister possessed to know her must surely have originated with him, and the thought was not without its comfort.
They walked on for some distance without speaking further. Margaret felt neither ease nor confidence, yet she could not deny a sense of gratification. Such an invitation implied a degree of regard she had not expected.
By the time they reached the carriage, the others had fallen considerably behind. Mr. Hartwell suggested that she might rest for a few minutes within the house, but she declined. The evening was pleasant, and they remained together upon the lawn, each occupied with thoughts not easily expressed.

His composure deserted him at last. I saw his hands tremble where they rested upon the arm of the chair, and though he strove to conceal it, grief betrayed itself in every movement. His eyes remained fixed upon the dying fire, yet it seemed to me he saw nothing before him. The room was silent save for the uneven sound of his breathing.
I watched him for some moments and, finding his misery a temptation too strong to resist, spoke with deliberate cruelty.
“You mourn her now,” I said, “yet you were never content while she lived.”
He lifted his head slowly. There was something in his expression that might once have warned me to silence, but suffering had dulled its force.
“Leave this house,” he answered.
The words were scarcely audible.
“I shall leave when I choose,” I replied. “Her memory gives me as much right to speak as it does you. She had kindness enough for all around her. Had she lived longer, she might have judged more clearly the worth of those who claimed to love her.”
He rose so abruptly that I stepped backward.
“Take care,” he said.
“Or what?” I returned. “Will you frighten me into agreement? No. I knew her better than you suppose. She would not have endured this bitterness forever. Whatever attachment bound her to you, it could not have survived unchanged.”
At that he seized a heavy book from the table and hurled it across the room. It struck the wall beside me and fell open at my feet.
The violence accomplished little beyond convincing me to depart.
I reached the doorway and turned once more.
“You are feared by many,” I said, “but fear is not devotion. Remember that.”
Then I fled.
Behind me I heard a crash of overturned furniture and a cry that sounded more wounded than angry. I did not pause to learn the cause. Down the passage, through the yard, and across the fields I ran, urged onward by equal measures of alarm and relief.
When at last the house disappeared behind the rising ground, I slowed my pace. The evening wind was cold, but never had open country seemed more welcoming. I would sooner have wandered the moors until morning than passed another hour beneath that roof.
Here the narrator paused. After a few moments she resumed in a calmer tone.
She left the district shortly afterward and settled many miles away among distant relations. Letters arrived from her occasionally, though they became less frequent as the years passed. News reached us that she had married again and borne a daughter, a delicate child who was said to resemble her mother closely.
As for the man she had fled, he remained unchanged in reputation, if not in spirit. From time to time he questioned those who had known her, asking where she lived and whether she prospered. Few answered willingly.
“Let her remain where she is,” he remarked once when I refused him information. “Distance serves us both.”
Yet he never spoke those words without a bitterness that suggested the opposite.
Years later, when her name was mentioned in his hearing, he would fall silent. Whatever else time had altered, it had not entirely subdued that old wound.

Enter ARISTON alone.
ARISTON.
Now let them banquet where they praised me most,
And count their cups among their reckonings.
Not one remains whose hand would lift my burden,
Though all were eager when my board was spread.
The gold is gone, and with it goes their friendship.
I know them now.
Let every hall that echoed with their laughter
Stand empty as their promises. Let music
Forget the doors it once made glad. The guest
Who smiled upon my fortune and my table
Has vanished with the season of my wealth.
Yet I was blind.
I called these hollow men companions.
I measured hearts by bows and courteous phrases,
And purchased thanks that lasted but an hour.
Enter STEWARD and two SERVANTS.
STEWARD.
My lord, we sought you through the city streets.
ARISTON.
Why seek me?
STEWARD.
Because we fear for you and for ourselves.
The house is left deserted. Creditors
Have sealed the gates. The servants wander idle,
Not knowing where to turn.
FIRST SERVANT.
Good master, speak.
The poorest among us grieves to see this change.
ARISTON.
Grieve not for me.
The world has shown its face at last.
I thank necessity for that instruction.
The flatterer has fled; the borrower forgets;
The friend who swore eternal faith remembers
No syllable of all his former vows.
SECOND SERVANT.
There are some honest men.
ARISTON.
If so, they hide.
I searched for one and found a hundred masks.
The city breeds fair words and barren deeds.
Its marketplaces traffic not in goods alone,
But in affection, honour, gratitude.
STEWARD.
Will you not stay and make your peace with fortune?
ARISTON.
No. Fortune and I are strangers now.
Let those who worship her attend her court.
I shall go where the woods ask nothing of me,
Where no man praises what he means to steal.
FIRST SERVANT.
Then we will follow.
ARISTON.
No.
Keep what remains of your poor livelihoods.
I go alone.
STEWARD.
The gods protect you, sir.
ARISTON.
If they have watched in silence until now,
They may continue so. Farewell to all.
Exit ARISTON.
FIRST SERVANT.
A noble heart brought low.
SECOND SERVANT.
And lower still by those who fed upon it.
STEWARD.
Come. We must gather what the ruin leaves.
When wealth departs, companions vanish first;
Yet honest sorrow lingers after both.
Exeunt.

For if these things be defended under the name of religion, they furnish the strongest evidence against those who defend them. For men are not wholly deprived of judgment, however much habit and corruption may cloud their minds. Even among those most given to vice there remains some faint admiration for virtue. Therefore the authors of deception, if they wish to ensnare the many, must borrow the appearance of what is good. They praise temperance in words and reward excess in practice; they commend humility with their lips while exalting pride before the eyes of the multitude.
Thus the crowd is taught by spectacle rather than by doctrine. Few listen to sober instruction, but many delight in public display. The result is that honorable precepts are spoken quietly to small assemblies, while disgraceful examples are exhibited openly and celebrated. The lesson most eagerly learned is not the one that is taught, but the one that is applauded.
Consider the festivals that are instituted in honor of divine powers. The people gather in great numbers. Songs are sung, ceremonies are performed, and sacred names are invoked. Yet amid these observances are introduced entertainments whose purpose is not the cultivation of virtue but the encouragement of passion. Those who attend are told that such things are acceptable to heaven. What wonder, then, if they return home with weakened discipline and corrupted desires?
For men are more readily persuaded by what they see than by what they hear. If modesty is praised while shamelessness is displayed, the display triumphs over the praise. If restraint is commended while indulgence is honored before the public eye, indulgence becomes the true object of imitation. The multitude does not inquire what is best; it follows what is celebrated.
What spirit, then, delights in such contradictions? What power urges men toward vice while pretending concern for their improvement? Surely no friend of righteousness would seek to heal conduct by means that inflame corruption. Such practices resemble a physician who commends health while secretly administering poison.
Hence it is not surprising that commonwealths decline when they mistake moral decay for religious devotion. Citizens labor to appease supernatural powers through ceremonies, yet neglect the virtues by which societies are preserved. They fear offending the gods by omitting festivals, but do not fear offending justice through greed, luxury, and dishonesty.
In this way the foundations of public order are gradually weakened. The walls of a city may stand firm, its armies may remain numerous, and its treasuries full; yet if virtue is abandoned, ruin has already entered within the gates. No enemy is more dangerous than the corruption that grows under the protection of sacred approval.
