She hesitated at first, for the two extraordinary creatures had drawn so near that their noses almost touched her sleeves. One blinked solemnly; the other smiled without appearing to know why. Still, finding neither intended to bite, she gathered confidence and continued her account.
Both listeners remained silent until she reached the part concerning the upside-down clock that had insisted upon striking tomorrow before today. Then the larger creature gave a long whistle.
“That is remarkably peculiar,” he observed.
“Peculiar?” cried the smaller one. “It is peculiarity itself!”
“The clock struck tomorrow?” repeated the larger creature thoughtfully. “I should like to hear something else. Let her recite.”
He nodded toward his companion, who immediately assumed an air of authority.
“Stand up and repeat ‘The Busy Bee,’” he commanded.
Alice was not at all pleased.
“They are forever ordering people about,” she thought. “One would imagine lessons were the principal amusement of the world.”
Nevertheless she rose and began. Unfortunately her thoughts were still occupied with the clock, and the verse emerged in a very altered form indeed:
“The curious Bee sat under the sea,
And polished his wings with jam;
He borrowed a hat from a fishing cat,
And dined upon lavender ham.”
The larger creature shook his head.
“That is not how it goes.”
“No,” agreed the smaller one, “though I cannot say how it ought to go either.”
Alice sat down quickly, feeling rather embarrassed.
“What does it mean?” asked the larger creature.
“I do not know,” said Alice.
“Then why say it?”
“I was told to.”
This answer appeared reasonable to her, though neither of the creatures seemed satisfied.
“Try another verse,” said the smaller one.
Alice sighed.
“Beyond the hill where the teacups grow,
An Owl and a Minnow played cards;
The Owl lost twice, but the Minnow paid
In buttons and peppermint shards.”
The larger creature looked alarmed.
“Can minnows carry buttons?”
“Only on formal occasions,” said the smaller one.
Before Alice could protest, both creatures began arguing over the matter. Their disagreement grew louder and more complicated until neither appeared to remember what they were discussing.
At length the larger creature stopped.
“Enough of verses,” he declared. “Let us have a song.”
“A song!” cried the other. “A song at once!”
Without waiting for permission, he began:
“O marvelous Pie, so round and high,
Revolving slowly through the sky!
Who would not trade a crown or two
For one small slice of splendid Pie?
Pie in the morning,
Pie in the rain,
Pie by the windowpane!”
He paused dramatically, then continued in an even louder voice:
“O glorious Pie, of berry and breeze,
Balanced with effortless expertise!
Whether it whistles or whether it sings,
Pie is the finest of edible things!”
“Again!” cried the larger creature.
The singer drew breath to begin once more, but at that moment a distant voice rang across the field:
“The inspection is starting!”
Immediately both creatures sprang to their feet.
“The inspection!” they shouted together, and dashed away, leaving Alice more puzzled than ever.

The vessel pressed onward through the long heaving swells, cutting the ocean with a steady violence that sent spray flying from her bows. Every spar groaned with the strain, and yet the ship seemed eager for the pursuit, as though some hidden desire possessed her timbers. The men moved about the decks with restless expectation. Each glance cast toward the horizon carried the same question, and each moment of delay sharpened it.
“There again! There again! Off the larboard bow!” came the cry from above.
The words passed through the crew like a spark through dry grass. Heads turned at once. Hands tightened upon ropes and rails. Even those who had spoken little during the morning now stared ahead with shining eyes.
The captain stood unmoving for a moment. Then he stepped forward and fixed his gaze upon the distant sea.
“Keep her course,” he said. “Let no man lose sight of that place.”
The ocean before them appeared empty save for a patch of white disturbance far ahead. Yet all knew the sign. A whale had broken the surface and vanished again beneath the dark water.
Excitement spread among them. Old fears, doubts, and warnings were forgotten. The long chase, the weary watches, the uncertain days behind them—all seemed justified by that fleeting glimpse. Their purpose narrowed to a single point upon the sea.
The ship rushed onward. Wind filled the sails until they swelled like great clouds overhead. The masts bent slightly under the pressure, and the deck trembled beneath the continual drive forward.
Again the lookout shouted.
This time there could be no mistake.
The whale erupted from the depths in a vast arching leap. Water cascaded from its shining body. For an instant it hung against the sky, immense and magnificent, before crashing back into the sea with a report like distant artillery. Foam burst outward in every direction.
A great cry rose from the ship.
The crew surged toward the rails. Some pointed; others laughed aloud. The sight overwhelmed caution. The creature seemed less an animal than a force of nature, appearing suddenly from the hidden world below and declaring its presence to all who watched.
The captain’s eyes never left it.
“There is our quarry,” he said quietly.
The men heard him and answered with renewed energy. Whatever fate awaited them beyond that horizon, they were committed now. Ship, sea, wind, and crew had become joined in a single pursuit, driving after the mighty shape that moved before them through the endless blue.

“I assure you, there is no mistake,” said Marianne. “I have never spoken with greater sincerity. He has declared himself, and I have accepted him.”
Her sister stared in astonishment. “You cannot expect me to believe it immediately. Only a short time ago, you could scarcely mention his name with patience.”
“That belongs entirely to the past,” replied Marianne. “If I judged him harshly before, I was mistaken. I see him differently now. Indeed, I think it would be unjust to dwell upon former opinions when everything has so completely changed.”
Still her sister seemed uncertain.
“You are serious?” she asked at last. “Perfectly serious?”
“Perfectly. I would not trifle with you on such a subject.”
“My dear Marianne!” she cried, taking her hand. “Then I wish you every happiness. Yet tell me honestly—are you certain of your own feelings? Do you esteem him enough? Do you love him as you ought?”
“I do. More than I imagined possible.”
“Then I am content,” said her sister. “Nothing else is necessary. But you have concealed this wonderfully well. Looking back, I can now understand many things that puzzled me, though at the time I suspected nothing.”
“There was little to tell at first,” said Marianne. “Even to myself, the change was gradual. I did not know my own heart until quite recently.”
They continued talking for some time, reviewing conversations and incidents that now appeared in a different light. What had once seemed insignificant acquired fresh meaning. By the end of their discussion, every doubt had disappeared.
The following morning, their mother observed from the drawing-room window that two gentlemen were approaching the house.
“There they are again!” she exclaimed. “I declare, they seem determined to spend every day here. Well, I cannot complain, for one of them is always welcome. But the other surprises me by his persistence.”
Marianne coloured slightly and turned away.
A few moments later the visitors entered. One addressed her sister with unusual warmth, while the other approached Marianne with a composure that concealed, though only imperfectly, his satisfaction.
Their mother, entirely unaware of the true state of affairs, soon proposed an excursion.
“The weather is delightful,” she said. “You should all walk to the hill beyond the meadow. The view is particularly fine this time of year.”
Her younger daughter immediately declined.
“I would rather remain at home,” she said.
“Then the rest of you must go without her,” replied their mother. “There is no reason everyone should lose the pleasure.”
The suggestion was received with more approval than she realized, and several exchanged glances that were not wholly accidental.

“You must not remain idle,” said the doctor. “A settled routine, some occupation, a purpose that carries you forward—those things would do you more good than any medicine I could prescribe.”
“Yes, perhaps you are right,” replied Nikolai after a pause. “I shall return to my studies soon. Once I am occupied again, everything may become simpler.”
The doctor nodded with satisfaction, though he was secretly disappointed. He had expected gratitude, perhaps even admiration for his advice. Instead, when he looked closely at his patient, he thought he detected a trace of irony in his expression. The impression vanished almost immediately.
His mother hastened to fill the silence.
“We owe you so much already,” she said warmly. “Especially after your kindness in visiting us last night.”
“You saw them last night?” Nikolai asked, turning toward the doctor. “Then you sacrificed your rest as well.”
“It was nothing of consequence,” said the doctor. “I was awake regardless.”
Nikolai lowered his eyes.
“To tell the truth, I do not understand why everyone troubles themselves on my account. You have all shown me more concern than I deserve. I ought to be grateful, yet I find myself embarrassed by it. It is difficult to accept what I cannot explain.”
“You are overthinking the matter,” said the doctor with a laugh. “Young physicians become attached to their early patients. We imagine ourselves responsible for them. Besides, I have not yet the luxury of being overwhelmed with work.”
“And he,” Nikolai added, indicating his friend, “has received nothing in return for his efforts except inconvenience.”
“What absurdity!” his friend exclaimed. “You speak as though we are strangers.”
His sister, however, watched him carefully. There was something in his manner that unsettled her. His words were kind, but they seemed rehearsed, as if he had prepared them beforehand.
He turned toward his mother.
“As for you, I only realized today how much anxiety I caused. You must have suffered greatly yesterday.”
Then, unexpectedly, he extended his hand toward his sister.
The gesture was simple, but the feeling behind it appeared genuine. She grasped his hand immediately. Relief brightened her face. Their disagreement of the previous day seemed suddenly less important.
The mother's eyes filled with tears.
“There,” she thought, “that is the son I know. Whatever troubles him, his heart remains good.”
Her gaze lingered on him. He looked exhausted. His clothes were worn, his appearance neglected. Yet to her he remained dearer than anyone in the world.
Suddenly she began speaking again.
“You cannot imagine how frightened we were yesterday. We arrived full of hope and were told at once that you had left in a feverish state. They were searching the streets for you. I thought of every terrible possibility. I scarcely knew what to do.”
The memory still shook her.
“For hours I could think of nothing else. Every story of illness or misfortune returned to me. I feared we might lose you before we had even seen you properly again.”
The room fell silent. No one doubted the sincerity of her distress.

The decision to travel had been mine alone, though Clara consented to it with visible reluctance. She feared less for the hardships of the road than for the solitude into which I willingly cast myself. Her concern had procured for me the companionship of Ashton, whose cheerful temper she believed might draw me from my habitual gloom. Yet no kindness could wholly overcome the burden that oppressed my thoughts.
When at last I departed, she stood silent beside the gate, her composure maintained only by effort. I attempted words of reassurance, but they died before reaching my lips. The carriage bore me away through valleys bright with summer light, across hills clothed in forests, and beside rivers that reflected the clear sky. These scenes passed before me almost unnoticed. My mind remained fixed upon the object of my journey and the unfinished labor that awaited me.
Several days later I arrived at a small city upon the frontier and there remained until Ashton joined me. The contrast between us was striking. Every novelty delighted him. He spoke with enthusiasm of distant towers, crowded marketplaces, and the varied character of the people we encountered. Where I saw only passing forms, he discovered endless occasions for admiration.
“You observe nothing,” he said one evening as we walked beside the river. “The world spreads its treasures before you, yet you move among them as though in a dream.”
I could not deny the justice of his remark. The beauty that animated him seemed unable to penetrate the cloud that surrounded me. Nevertheless, I listened while he described the changing colors of the sunset and the reflections trembling upon the water. His pleasure was sincere and infectious, and at moments I almost believed myself capable of sharing it.
We continued our route by boat, allowing the current to carry us through a succession of fertile districts. Vineyards descended the hillsides in ordered rows. Ancient fortresses crowned rocky heights and overlooked the winding river below. In some places the landscape appeared severe and lonely; in others it opened suddenly into broad fields dotted with villages and church spires.
The season of harvest had begun. Along the banks we heard laborers singing as they worked, their voices carried faintly across the water. Children ran among the orchards, and carts laden with fruit moved slowly along the roads. Such signs of ordinary happiness awakened in me a feeling I had not experienced for many months. Though my anxieties remained, they seemed less absolute than before.
One afternoon, as our vessel drifted beneath a range of wooded cliffs, I reclined against the railing and watched clouds gathering in the western sky. The air grew cooler, and a gentle wind stirred the surface of the river. For a brief interval my thoughts loosened their hold upon me. I ceased to dwell upon the future and surrendered myself to the quiet motion of the voyage.
Ashton, meanwhile, appeared enchanted by everything around him. He spoke of distant countries he hoped to visit and of the many wonders still hidden beyond the horizon. His imagination transformed every scene into the beginning of some greater adventure. Observing his delight, I could not help envying the ease with which he embraced the world.
“Perhaps,” he said as evening descended, “the greatest privilege is not to discover new lands, but to retain the ability to wonder at them.”
The remark lingered with me long after the light had faded and the river had vanished into darkness.

Over the past decade, Xi Jinping's increasingly assertive China has been a constant source of anxiety for many in the West.
These concerns are deeply rooted in reality. It is undeniable that Xi and the Chinese Communist Party's inner circle have grown significantly in confidence during this era. Whether it entails militarizing artificial islands in the South China Sea, suppressing democratic institutions in Hong Kong, or employing economic coercion against sovereign nations, Beijing seemingly no longer seeks approval from a Western world that has allowed China's expansionism to proceed with minimal consequences to its behavior.
The global community has been starkly reminded of China's confidence in recent months. As technology sectors scramble due to a restricted supply of critical minerals and Beijing intensifies its military posturing around Taiwan, it is crucial to examine how significantly Western leaders have misunderstood Xi and ignored his readiness to utilize the geopolitical leverage he possesses.
The global supply chain vulnerability reveals a profoundly powerful tool that provides China with leverage in its relationship with the West: its dominance over rare earth elements.
It is no secret that many Western nations, including the United States, are heavily reliant on Chinese exports of the minerals necessary to manufacture everything from smartphones to electric vehicles. The recent export restrictions have emphasized not just the economic, but the geopolitical risks of this profound dependency.
While China continues to participate in global trade, analysts note that it could easily ease export quotas to stabilize global manufacturing ahead of a major technological transition, thereby reducing costs and calming international markets.
The question from the Chinese perspective is, why should they? Beijing is currently leveraging its economic power to discourage foreign interference in its domestic affairs. "If Washington removes its tariffs and technology sanctions tomorrow, normalized trade flows of these crucial components will resume the day after," state media outlets recently implied, blaming the current supply chain crisis and inflation on American protectionist policies.
These trade dynamics are controversial because many view them as a geopolitical influence mechanism for Beijing, a concern that was not alleviated when Chinese officials recently indicated that "mutual respect" regarding Taiwan would help "stabilize the global economic situation."
Aside from the financial and geopolitical advantages derived from the world's reliance on Chinese manufacturing, it also feeds into a domestic political narrative that has evolved significantly over time in China: The West is in a state of terminal decline.
"The core of this narrative is that the United States and its allies are paralyzed by internal divisions and failing democratic systems," says a senior analyst focusing on East Asian geopolitics. "A decade ago, this argument was largely defensive, utilized by the CCP to shield itself from Western lectures on human rights. But today, Beijing can point to political polarization in the US and sluggish economic growth in Europe to argue that the Chinese authoritarian model is actually superior."
Western decline and Chinese resurgence are, of course, viewed through the specific historical lens of the CCP. Xi has repeatedly emphasized the necessity of national rejuvenation to overcome the "Century of Humiliation."
When you factor this historical grievance into so much of Xi's behavior over the last decade—building up a blue-water navy, utilizing debt-trap diplomacy in developing nations, and aggressively confronting foreign militaries in international airspace—it becomes easy to construct an image of a leader determined to restore his nation to its rightful place as a global hegemon, perfectly willing to exploit the hesitation of naïve global counterparts.
"Since the 2008 financial crisis, many in the CCP leadership have believed that the balance of global power was permanently shifting," notes a prominent scholar of Chinese politics. "This became more acute after the consolidation of Xi's power, which is why you now see a China more comfortable with coercive diplomacy, intellectual property theft, and aggressive maritime patrols. As far as Beijing is concerned, this is a strategic competition," the scholar says, adding that "for the West, however, China's rise is viewed as an economic challenge rather than a comprehensive systemic threat."
Some argue that this failure to perceive the true nature of the threat has bred a lackluster Western policy in the face of Chinese aggression. This, in turn, has meant the Chinese Premier can carry out hostile acts with very few consequences.
"The West's long-term failure has been treating each aggressive maneuver as an isolated incident, rather than recognizing the overall pattern of a rising power that has no interest in abiding by the current rules-based international order," argues a senior fellow at a major geopolitical think tank.
This is at the heart of the current geopolitical tension. China is becoming much more open and direct in its ambitions. When China leverages its critical mineral monopoly to punish political rivals, or aggressively sanctions European politicians, it is done overtly. There is no longer a pretense that Beijing is working to assimilate into the Western liberal order.
Limited consequences from the West provide little comfort to those who share a geographic proximity to China. Regional allies warn that Western disunity is Xi's greatest advantage. Every time an opportunity arises to divide the US from its allies, Beijing takes it.
Ultimately, the myth that has been fed by Western confusion and economic entanglement has gone a long way toward creating a domestic colossus who can increasingly act with impunity. For all the anxiety over China's rise, it may be that the West's reluctance to truly understand Xi Jinping has helped create the most formidable version of the leader that was ever possible.

(CNN)Germany will have to pay a €600,000 ($640,000) fine for each day it continues clearing land for the Garzweiler open-pit coal mine expansion in North Rhine-Westphalia, a European tribunal ruled Monday.
The European Commission ordered Germany to halt operations at the site following a lawsuit launched by environmental coalitions and neighboring municipalities. Germany has so far failed to comply with the order, and the regional government stated Monday it would continue the expansion -- even though the court ruling means it will incur the daily penalty.
"The state government will not halt the Garzweiler operations. From the very beginning, we maintained that suspending the extraction would threaten the immediate energy security of the industrial region," a regional government spokesperson said in a statement.
The Garzweiler mine supplies fuel to several major power plants, according to RWE, the energy company that operates the site. It is also a significant economic driver, with thousands of jobs in the region tied directly to the fossil fuel sector.
But it has long been at the center of an intense environmental dispute. The mine is located in a densely populated area of western Germany, near the Dutch border. Activists and neighboring countries have been campaigning to have the expansion halted over climate concerns -- even amid a broader European energy crunch.
According to the International Energy Agency, Germany still relied heavily on coal for power generation during recent winter months, despite ambitious long-term climate targets.
The conflict escalated when RWE made it clear it was planning to demolish several local villages, including Lützerath, to reach the coal beneath them, a plan that has caused widespread protests. Negotiations on the issue have failed, with the company refusing to change its timeline.
Environmental groups have praised the court's decision on Monday. A leading climate advocate said on Twitter they welcomed the judgment and added that the coalition is ready to monitor compliance, stressing that emission reductions must be the priority.
Residents near the site are worried about the mine's impact on air quality, structural stability, and the permanent destruction of historical land. "The villages are being swallowed. As the massive excavators inch closer to our homes, the community is torn apart. There are people who are losing their ancestral homes to a fuel we are supposed to be phasing out," one local resident told CNN last year.
RWE and the German authorities say the planned mining area is within the boundaries stipulated in decades-old permits. RWE says its studies have shown the expansion is necessary to bridge the gap until renewable sources are fully scaled. To compensate, the company is also investing in wind and solar projects.
The climate implications of the mine are also significant. Lignite, the type of brown coal extracted at Garzweiler, is the least efficient and most polluting type of coal. It has lower fuel value and higher emissions intensity compared to other fossil fuels. The European Union has said it wants to phase out coal by 2030 -- a target Germany has officially embraced, but short-term realities have sparked yet another disagreement over the transition speed.

(CNN)The Democratic Party's early 2000s dream of an emerging majority based on a diversifying electorate has run into reality. Democrats lost the 2016 presidential election, and they barely won in 2020. Part of their problem was declining support among White voters.
But the 2020 election also pointed to another problem: Asian American voters (who are growing as a portion of the electorate) moving toward the Republican Party. Recent polling -- and now this week's New York primaries -- show that these Republican gains don't seem to be going away anytime soon.
New York has a significant Asian American population relative to the country as a whole. There are five assembly districts in New York City where Asian Americans make up at least 50% of the citizen voting age population, according to the latest data available from the Census Bureau. The district with the highest percentage of Asian American adult citizens (in Flushing, Queens) backed now-President Joe Biden by 15 points in 2020, after voting for Hillary Clinton by 40 points four years earlier. (That's not a misprint. It really was a 25-point swing.)
Regularly scheduled primaries, of course, don't feature a Democratic candidate versus a Republican candidate. We can, however, look at the relative turnout between the Democratic and Republican primaries. This is instructive in New York where, despite closed primaries, enthusiasm shifts are often visible in party turnout patterns.
All told, 32% of the New Yorkers who voted Tuesday in the five most heavily Asian districts cast a ballot in the Republican primary. This may not seem like a lot but consider that in 2018, just 14% of those who voted in either the Democratic or Republican primary cast a ballot on the Republican side. 
In the Flushing-based district, 35% of primary votes were cast on the Republican side Tuesday. It was basically nothing in 2018, with a mere 8% of primary votes being cast on the Republican side. That's a 27-point shift. 
Compare this with what happened citywide in New York this year. There was slight movement toward Republicans: Of the votes cast on the Democratic or Republican side, 22% were Republican primary votes. In 2018, it was 18%. This was a 4-point shift without rounding.
Put another way, the shift in Republican primary participation in those five heavily Asian districts was nearly seven times the citywide shift.
New York, though, is only one state. Further, it's just a primary.
But even on the national level, New York does not seem to be that much of an outlier.
While it's not clear that Asian Americans have moved even more toward the Republicans relative to how Americans overall are shifting, it's clear that Republicans are holding their gains from 2020. 
Nationally, the three polls that meet CNN standards for reporting (CNN/SSRS, Fox and Quinnipiac University) have produced generic congressional ballot results since the beginning of the year and provided a crosstab on Asian American voters' midterm preferences. The generic ballot asks respondents some form of the following question: "If the elections for Congress were held today, would you vote for the Democratic or Republican Party?" 
Democrats have held a 27-point advantage among Asian Americans on the generic congressional ballot in the average of these polls so far this year.
An average of three estimates of how Americans voted in House races in 2020 (Catalist, the CNN exit poll and the Pew Research Center) found that Democrats won the House vote by about 34 points in 2020 among Asian Americans.
This 7-point shift toward Republicans among Asian Americans is slightly larger than the 5-point shift we see among voters overall -- Republicans ahead by 2 points on the generic congressional ballot, compared with losing the national House vote by 3 points in 2020.
The key thing to realize, though, is that Democrats did worse -- and Republicans did better -- among Asian American voters in 2020 House races relative to the national vote than in any House election since 2014. So while not losing additional ground is not a bad thing for Democrats, it's not a good thing either.
If anything, the picture gets better for Republicans when you examine Biden's popularity. Across the CNN, Fox and Quinnipiac polls this year, the President's net approval rating averages +8 points with Asian Americans. That's 23 points better than his net approval rating with voters overall in these polls (-15 points).
In the 2020 election, Biden's margin with Asian American voters was about 30 points better than it was overall.
So compared with 2020, Biden is doing worse with Asian Americans relative to how he is doing with voters overall. That's notable given that earlier in his presidency, Biden was doing significantly better among Asian Americans relative to how he was doing overall and compared with how he did in 2020.
This fits with an analysis from FiveThirtyEight's Geoffrey Skelley, which found that Biden's approval rating had fallen disproportionately among non-White voters, including Asian Americans, over the course of his presidency.
The fact that the political preferences of Asian Americans are jumping around may get at something larger: Their votes are up for grabs more so than the average voter's. While they may still be more Democratic-leaning, both parties have a good chance of making up ground among the Asian American electorate.
I'd expect a lot of attention to be focused on this growing bloc of voters in the midterms.

(CNN)The American presidential election that took place on Tuesday was in many ways a symbolic referendum on Donald Trump. While the final tallies are still coming in, early signs suggest the former President can chalk this one up as a definitive victory. Final polls going into the numerous battleground contests suggested that Trump's Republicans have held onto governorships in key swing areas and will increase their share of congressional seats. Most symbolically, Trump's party took a crucial Senate seat up for grabs in a traditional blue-wall state, which has been held by the Democratic party for over a decade. It is incredibly rare for a candidate facing extensive legal battles to stage such a decisive political comeback, and especially impressive that Trump managed to do this after leaving office under a cloud of deep controversy.
Much has happened since Trump lost his reelection bid in the 2020 presidential election. Back then, the US was stuck in a bitter political deadlock, with polarization in Congress frustrating a public clearly eager to move on. Much of Trump's success has been attributed to the fact he had an "America First" economic platform and just needed the legislative backing to execute his agenda -- that, and a vulnerable opponent whose administration alienated many working-class voters over inflation. Shortly after Trump launched his campaign, he was hit with a barrage of criminal indictments and civil lawsuits that critics believed would end his career.
Read More
The list of Trump's political mishaps is long. Throughout his campaign, he was criticized for his inflammatory rhetoric, questioning democratic institutions, and dropping the ball on unity by alienating moderate suburban voters. His team has been accused of nepotism and financial conflicts of interest, offering high-level access to wealthy donors. Most recently, Trump was accused of privately telling advisers he would use federal agencies to target his political enemies, a comment his campaign denied he made. Outside of domestic politics, his trade policies have been criticized for being reckless and poorly planned, leaving agricultural exporters in serious trouble. He is also being formally investigated by state prosecutors for alleged financial fraud during his time in the private sector. And his judgment has come under serious scrutiny following a massive fallout within his campaign's inner circle.
The nation is already stretched to a breaking point. Donald Trump's pile of scandals isn't helping matters
How, then, has Trump so resoundingly won this referendum on his leadership?
The first point to note is that Trump has been bailed out by public frustration over the economy. The candidate opted to focus heavily on pocketbook issues and, effectively, promised sweeping tariffs to protect domestic manufacturing. As a result, Trump is steaming ahead with his legislative plans, a success that he is able to attribute to his unique populist appeal -- an oversimplified but, for many, highly believable claim.
More importantly, Trump has managed to shift the center ground in American politics, a difficult feat in a deeply divided country. People who previously would have never considered voting Republican now see his movement as an entirely different entity to the party led by George W. Bush or John McCain. The traditional ideas of fiscal conservatism and interventionist foreign policy going hand in hand no longer really exist. The populist realignment was perhaps the clearest indicator of this, where people in traditional union areas voted for a campaign spearheaded by Trump, the billionaire real estate mogul. Through a mixture of economic nationalism, willingness to challenge corporate structures, and performative patriotism, he has found a way to appeal to a particular working-class identity that is a million miles from the globalist establishment that dominated Washington not so long ago.
Four years after leaving Washington, the US and its allies are facing massive trade friction over new tariffs
Finally, the country is very divided, which works in the President's favor, at least for now. Trump and his agenda are on the whole popular in rural and exurban America; the only parts of the country that buck this trend are sufficiently concentrated in deep-blue cities for him not to need them to win the Electoral College. The picture is different in the coastal states where Trump and the Republican platform have less power and are less attractive to many voters. This is most true in the Northeast and West Coast, where progressive coalitions look set to extend their cultural dominance.
It's not all good news, though. Sooner rather than later, absolute political chaos will be unleashed in Washington as the opposition's resistance to his administration becomes a roar, though with next to no chance of stopping his executive orders. And institutional instability between state governors and the federal government is raising fresh fears of a constitutional crisis.
Longer term, there will be a post-mortem of how the opposing party lost its grip on the working class. Tariffs will inevitably have a more visible impact on consumer prices. The spats with foreign allies over trade relationships might reach a tipping point that irritates the public. And while Trump appears to have gamed American politics perfectly for now, he's done so by driving a wedge between different demographic groups and by reaping the benefits of grievance politics. It could keep Trump and his movement in power for years, but it could also drive the citizens of the United States further apart, which could have political, economic, and social consequences well into the future. One day, it's entirely possible Trump may come to regret unleashing these demons for the sake of victory.

(CNN)Nearly three-quarters of women in the Canadian Armed Forces have experienced toxic workplace culture, sexual harassment and discrimination during their service, a groundbreaking parliamentary report revealed on Tuesday. The Canadian defense committee, which described its inquiry into the treatment of women in the armed forces as "long overdue," said that 71% of respondents stated they had faced some form of bullying, harassment or discrimination while deployed or on base. More than 3,500 women testified for the comprehensive review, including active personnel and retired veterans. The testimonies, provided in closed-door sessions and public hearings, include allegations of sexual assault, targeted intimidation, exploitation of junior recruits and "locker room" environments that openly degraded female service members. Some women also reported they were punished for rejecting inappropriate advances and witnessed colleagues being marginalized but were "too terrified of retaliation to speak up."
"The armed forces have made superficial progress recently... However, it is abundantly clear that the military remains, structurally, a boys' club," Marie-France Lalonde, chair of the committee on military workplace culture, said in a statement.
She added that "the internal grievance system, in its current form, is fundamentally broken and leaves victims feeling silenced and unsupported."
Lalonde also noted that while some leaders genuinely want to "change the culture," female personnel are being "betrayed by the chain of command," explaining that there had been numerous reports of "commanding officers burying allegations to protect the institution's image and their own promotions."
Drawing from the inquiry, Lalonde recommended that all serious sexual misconduct cases should be permanently transferred to the civilian justice system, given that military tribunals have consistently shown lower conviction rates than civilian courts. She stated that servicewomen are repeatedly being "denied basic justice."
The review is one of the most comprehensive to date, with the Department of National Defence granting a rare exemption that allowed active-duty members to speak freely to parliamentary investigators.
Following the report's release, the Department of National Defence posted on social media: "Misconduct and toxic behavior have no place in the Canadian Armed Forces, and we are dedicated to rooting out all forms of harassment."
Despite the harrowing accounts, a vast majority of the female respondents indicated they were still deeply proud of their service and would encourage others to join, Lalonde highlighted.
"Now that the systemic failures have been laid bare, it is time we started defending those who defend our country and enact reforms that strengthen our military, for everyone who wears the uniform," she said.

(CNN)For Eddy Alvarez, being an Olympian was always the ambition.
"Growing up, I always [said], 'I'm going to be an Olympian,'" Alvarez tells CNN Sport. "That's one thing that I really want to share with everyone: You gotta go and you can do it. You can go for it. Nothing's stopping you."
American dual-sport athlete Alvarez has achieved that goal twice, competing at the Winter Olympic Games in Sochi seven years ago and then winning his second Olympic medal -- a silver in baseball -- at Tokyo 2020.
Shortly after his most recent success, Alvarez was lauded as the first Cuban American to carry the flag for the US at the Opening Ceremony of the Tokyo Games.
However, the celebrations didn't stop with him. As the Games progressed, Team USA saw much success, especially among its Hispanic and Latino American contingent.
After Alvarez, there was Richard Torrez Jr. -- whose super heavyweight boxing silver medal made national history -- and Devin Booker, who won a gold medal in men's basketball.
Then came Diana Taurasi, an athlete of Argentine descent who helped the US women's basketball team secure its seventh consecutive gold, and the first basketball player of any nationality to win five Olympic gold medals.
Finally, Anita Alvarez helped Team USA make waves in artistic swimming.
More than a dozen Hispanic and Latino American athletes left Tokyo with medals around their necks, helping the American team reach the top of the medal table after two weeks of rigorous competition amid the Covid-19 pandemic.
If Hispanic American Olympic representation is in the spotlight following Tokyo, that success has come from less than promising circumstances.
Hispanic athletes began making headlines in the 1980s and 1990s, when gymnast Tracee Talavera became one of the first Mexican Americans to medal at the 1984 Summer Olympic Games in Los Angeles. Fellow teammate Oscar De La Hoya medaled years later, making him a celebrated Mexican American man to win a boxing gold medal. 
However, both athletes endured stereotypes that often hindered their path to mainstream recognition.
Talavera trained in an era where the sport was overwhelmingly White, and representation for Latinas was nearly non-existent, according to sports historians.
She also grew up in an environment where elite gymnastics was not easily accessible for minority communities.
De La Hoya, a first-generation Mexican American, faced skepticism about his loyalty and identity, fighting to prove he belonged on the US team while honoring his family's roots, historians stated.
Despite those hardships, both athletes excelled in their performances. Talavera became a key member of the team that won silver in the team competition in 1984.
At the 1992 Games in Barcelona, De La Hoya won gold in the lightweight division, fulfilling a promise to his late mother. 
Forty-four years after early pioneers, Lisa Fernandez made history as a prominent Cuban-Puerto Rican American to win a gold medal in softball as well as becoming an icon for Latina women in any Olympic sport.
"At the time, I just didn't think much of it because I felt like any other California girl, and it was just like, 'Okay, I'm representing my country and a proud American,'" Fernandez told CNN Sport.
"But I think afterwards, I felt just such an incredible amount of support from the Latino community. And I saw their pride and I was just taken aback."
According to Fernandez, she was one of the few Latinas representing Team USA at the 1996 Atlanta Games, alongside others who competed in track and field.
Fernandez credited early trailblazers like Dara Torres -- a Cuban American swimmer who is a multiple-time medalist -- as an inspiration.
"[Dara] was a huge role model of mine because I think I definitely had that connection of her being Hispanic American and someone that I felt I could emulate and look up to," Fernandez said.
Due to her landmark wins in the 1990s, Fernandez is often attributed as a pioneer for Latina athletes -- both on and off the field.
"I saw representation in Dara Torres, and that inspired me. And, hopefully, the more we see ourselves out there, the more people will believe in themselves," Fernandez explained.
Following Fernandez's Olympic gold medal performance, Hispanic Americans began finding success across a plethora of sports, including Rio 2016's "Final Five" gold-medal winning gymnast Laurie Hernandez, Olympic figure skater Scott Gomez and gold-medal winning swimmer Ryan Lochte.
Tokyo dreams -- fulfilled
The Olympic stage has steadily changed for Team USA since the days of Fernandez for both the Winter and Summer Games.
At the London Olympics in 2012, fewer Hispanic athletes represented Team USA, with several more as backups. The Tokyo Games occurred nearly a decade after the London Olympics, yet the American team saw a massive increase in Hispanic athletes since that time.
Tokyo 2020 saw dozens of athletes identifying as Hispanic or Latino, according to Hispanic heritage organizations.
Hispanics make up about 19% of the nation's population, making them the largest racial or ethnic minority group in the US, according to data from Pew Research Center. Yet, the Hispanic representation among Team USA in Tokyo was still growing to match those demographics.
Despite being one of the few Latinos on the US Olympic boxing team in Tokyo, Torrez Jr. often sees many Latinos training alongside him in California.
"We train out here in California, and so I feel like here, just within our little boxing community, I see so many Mexican Americans," he told CNN.
Of the Hispanic Americans competing for the US, many have openly identified as multiracial or from dual-heritage backgrounds.
"Generational changes happen and we're seeing even more mixed raced athletes," Fernandez said.
For multiracial athletes like Alvarez, who is of Cuban descent, envisioning his goals wasn't difficult when seeing an increasing number of successful individuals with a similar background to his.
"I really idolized [former athletes]," Alvarez told CNN. "When you look at someone that's got kind of a similar background, you kinda see the resemblance, or you kind of relate to them."
Although Alvarez's family immigrated from Cuba, he wholeheartedly chose to compete on behalf of the American team, representing the sacrifices of his parents.
"I'm an American, and growing up, even though my roots are in Cuba, it felt right," Alvarez explained to CNN. "There's no other country I would represent."
Devin Booker, another athlete of Mexican and African American descent, also noticed the diversity among other Team USA hopefuls training on the courts.
"If you look within the whole gym and in the program, we have so many athletes that come from very different backgrounds ... It's our jobs as the current athletes right now to inspire that and push for that," Booker stated.
Historically, there has been low participation among Hispanic Americans in the Olympics outside of a few sports.
A combination of socioeconomic factors -- such as the high cost of elite sports and cultural norms -- often played a role in this case of under involvement.
However, the current state of representation is slowly changing as the new wave of Hispanic American athletes challenge the status quo.
And that's in no small part to athletes like Taurasi.
Taurasi might be considered a veteran in her sport, but she is widely considered to be one of the world's best players -- no small achievement given her longevity in basketball. 
"In my sport, age can be a disadvantage," says Taurasi matter-of-factly about her lengthy career.
"I would just say it's not too late, and you can still achieve your dreams. And whatever you want to aspire to be, go after it."

(CNN)Here's a look at the life of Queen Elizabeth II.
Personal Birth date: April 21, 1926 Death date: September 8, 2022 Birth place: London, England Birth name: Elizabeth Alexandra Mary Father: King George VI Mother: Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother Marriage: Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh (November 20, 1947 - April 9, 2021, his death) Children: Charles III, November 14, 1948; Anne, Princess Royal, August 15, 1950; Prince Andrew, Duke of York, February 19, 1960; Prince Edward, Duke of Edinburgh, March 10, 1964 Education: Educated privately at home
Timeline December 11, 1936 - Elizabeth's father becomes King George VI following the abdication of his brother, King Edward VIII. Elizabeth officially becomes the heir presumptive.
Late 1940s - During World War II, Elizabeth serves in the Auxiliary Territorial Service, training as a driver and mechanic.
November 20, 1947 - Princess Elizabeth marries Philip Mountbatten at Westminster Abbey in London.
February 6, 1952 - King George VI dies, and Elizabeth ascends to the throne while on a royal tour in Kenya.
June 2, 1953 - The coronation of Queen Elizabeth II takes place at Westminster Abbey. It is the first British coronation to be fully televised.
1977 - Celebrates her Silver Jubilee, marking 25 years on the throne with large-scale parties across the UK and the Commonwealth.
1992 - The Queen dubs this year her "annus horribilis" (horrible year), marked by the marital separations of three of her children and a destructive fire at Windsor Castle.
August 31, 1997 - Princess Diana dies in a car crash. The Queen faces unprecedented public criticism for her initial delayed response and decision to remain at Balmoral.
2002 - Celebrates her Golden Jubilee. Her mother and her sister, Princess Margaret, both pass away earlier this same year.
2012 - Celebrates her Diamond Jubilee. She also participates in a James Bond sketch for the opening ceremony of the London Olympic Games.
September 9, 2015 - Becomes the longest-reigning British monarch in history, surpassing the record previously held by her great-great-grandmother, Queen Victoria.
April 9, 2021 - Prince Philip dies at Windsor Castle at the age of 99.
February 6, 2022 - Celebrates her Platinum Jubilee, marking an unprecedented 70 years on the throne.
September 8, 2022 - Queen Elizabeth II dies at Balmoral Castle in Scotland at the age of 96.
September 19, 2022 - The Queen's state funeral is held at Westminster Abbey. An estimated 4 billion people worldwide watch the broadcast. She is buried at St. George's Chapel in Windsor.

(CNN)The Canadian government has declared a state of emergency across large swaths of British Columbia (BC) as a devastating atmospheric river batters the province and forces thousands to evacuate.
Rains have been inundating communities since Tuesday, but parts of the Pacific Northwest tipped into crisis on Thursday as critical dikes overflowed, adding to swollen rivers and causing widespread flash flooding.
The provincial and federal governments have authorized numerous emergency disaster declarations in areas spanning the lower mainland, from the Fraser Valley to Vancouver Island, said BC Minister of Public Safety Mike Farnworth in a news conference on Friday.
There have been no widespread casualties reported yet -- but, Farnworth warned, "we are moving closer and closer to an inevitable tragedy."
"We cannot emphasize this enough: do not put yourself in danger, and do not put the agencies that are out there to assist you in the event of a flood rescue in danger," he said.
Some families were forced to evacuate in the middle of the night as rivers rose to catastrophic levels, and 5,000 more people -- primarily in the Abbotsford region -- may have to evacuate on Saturday, said provincial Premier John Horgan at the news conference.
"This is nothing like we've seen in our lifetimes," Horgan said. In parts of the province that have been hit harder, this is a once-in-a-century event; in other regions like the Fraser Valley, it is a "one-in-50-years" event, he stated.
Mark and Sarah Henderson lost the dairy farm they owned Thursday on what was supposed to be the week of their 20th anniversary. They lost everything they owned, including most of their livestock, as flood waters powered through Sumas Prairie, an area near the city of Abbotsford in BC, Mark's sister Emily wrote on a GoFundMe page set up to help the family. The page has raised nearly $120,000 CAD (roughly $95,000 USD) from 2,100 people in one day.
A video circulating on social media shows a barn being carried by floods in the same area, but CNN could not independently verify whether it was the one the Hendersons had owned.
"We have just been blown away with people's generosity," said Emily Henderson in a Facebook post. "We are all so grateful and cannot thank everyone enough."
Since Tuesday, the provincial emergency management agency has responded to 8,000 calls for assistance and conducted more than 900 flood rescues. Thousands of emergency workers and volunteers are still on the ground, helping trapped residents.
Photos show backyards and homes half underwater and highways flooded to waist-high levels. In the interior town of Merritt, residents rescued stranded pets struggling to stay afloat in rough waters; nearby, an entire bridge was swept away by the raging floodwaters, according to CNN affiliate CBC News.
Horgan urged residents to follow local guidance, stay off the roads, and heed evacuation orders if needed -- even for those who live in flood-prone areas and may have experienced flooding before.
"This is different," he warned. "What we're going through is unprecedented compared to what you've been through for the last 50 years. So please take it seriously."
Authorities don't know yet how many homes or public infrastructure have been lost, but "the damage is catastrophic," he said.
The natural disaster declaration could be further extended into the interior if the damage increases, said Farnworth. The declaration allows those affected to receive financial assistance, including subsidies for affected livestock or agriculture, and low-interest loans.
Canada's federal government activated disaster relief for flood-affected BC residents, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced in a televised address on Friday.
Those who are significantly injured, lost homes or had their homes damaged will be entitled to $2,000 CAD (roughly $1,580 USD) per household. "The terrible storms and floods that have been ravaging British Columbia for the past few days are taking a heavy toll," Trudeau said in his address, mentioning that he has been in regular contact with Horgan. 
The Prime Minister praised emergency workers and military personnel in his comments, urging people in flood-hit regions to listen to their advice and warnings, saying "for now, our primary message is very clear: keep yourself safe, keep your family safe, and follow the instructions of the professional agencies." 
Heavy rains are expected to continue in the upcoming week, with another atmospheric river forecast to move across the province from the coast, bringing significant rainfall to the coastal mountains, said Armel Castellan of Environment Canada. The worst-affected areas could see rain totals more than three times the November monthly average falling in just 48 hours.
Flooding on the Fraser River, which runs through the lower mainland, is expected to be as bad as a similar event that occurred in 1894, according to a statement from Environment Canada.
More than 200 schools in British Columbia will remain closed on Monday, with some requiring "extensive maintenance and repair," according to a press release from local officials. Thursday will be the first day of some reprieve, with rains expected to lighten into scattered showers.
But the cleanup operation will take many more months, said Emergency Management BC officials, according to CNN affiliate CTV News. Teams on the ground are expecting operations to continue "well past the winter holidays," and river levels will take time to recede. "We're looking at some very long and protracted recovery efforts," they said.

(CNN)The Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics will not sell tickets to the general public as the Covid-19 situation globally and within China remains complex, according to organizers. The announcement was made on Monday, following a finalized plan by the local organizing committee and international Olympic bodies.
"In light of the current pandemic situation globally and to ensure the safety of all participants and spectators, it has been decided that tickets should not be sold anymore to spectators from outside China's mainland," the International Olympic Committee (IOC) and the Beijing 2022 Organizing Committee announced in a joint press statement. While the general public is excluded, groups of targeted domestic spectators will be invited to venues under strict Covid-19 safety measures.
The news comes as several Chinese cities announced tightened lockdown measures on Monday, after a cluster of new cases was identified over the weekend.
Foreign spectators were also absent for the entirety of the Tokyo Olympics a year prior. The government is set to enforce a strict "closed-loop" management system across Beijing and other competition zones -- which will begin well before the opening ceremony -- to prevent any cross-infection, according to state media. This means there will be an absence of regular fans for 100% of the ticketed events during the Winter Games, with only pre-approved groups allowed inside the bubble.
"We very much regret that this situation has impacted the Winter Games, and we sincerely apologize to all international sports fans who were looking forward to traveling to watch the Games at the venues. We hope that you understand that these measures are unavoidable and being implemented in order to prevent the spread of infection. Everyone is encouraged to watch the Games at home," the statement added.
The statement also said that organizers will continuously monitor the situation and have further remote meetings "in the event of any major changes in the infection situation."
The Winter Games are scheduled to begin on February 4 and run through to February 20.

(CNN)The Oregon Ducks are out of the NCAA Women's Final Four due to severe health and safety protocols, allowing the South Carolina Gamecocks to advance to the national championship.
The Ducks were scheduled to play the Gamecocks Sunday evening in the national semifinal, but the NCAA declared the game a no-contest early Sunday morning because of medical protocols.
"This decision was made based on the recommendation of the Tournament Medical Staff and the local Department of Public Health. As a result, South Carolina will advance to the Championship," the NCAA said in a statement. "The NCAA and the committee regret that Oregon's student-athletes and coaching staff will not be able to continue in the tournament in which they earned the right to participate."
The NCAA could not provide additional details due to medical privacy laws, they added.
"The last 48 hours have been incredibly heartbreaking for everyone involved and my heart goes out to the student-athletes, coaches and staff of our basketball program," Oregon Athletics Director Rob Mullens said in a statement. "This is a devastating way to have their historic season come to an end, we appreciate their dedication all year long, as well as the unwavering support from Ducks fans."
On Friday, Oregon had only seven players available in the team's 72-68 victory over Stanford.
The Gamecocks will face either the UConn Huskies or the Iowa Hawkeyes in the national title game beginning Tuesday in Dallas, Texas.
South Carolina won the 2022 National Championship.
The Ducks are not the only team whose postseason was cut short because of unexpected health protocols. Texas A&M's men's golf team, Florida State's women's soccer team, and Ohio State's wrestling team were also forced to withdraw from recent NCAA championship tournaments due to severe illness outbreaks.

Part 1: Generated Text
(CNN)Osteosarcoma did not dictate Liam Thorne’s life. He did.
No matter the obstacles placed in front of him, of which there were many, he refused to be pitied and was determined to live a life that mattered. He inspired countless people, invited -- no, demanded -- honest talk about chronic illness and mortality, and brightened the worlds of those he touched with his humor, resilience, and spirit.
On Sunday morning, after battling a sudden onset of sepsis following an experimental immunotherapy treatment he started just two weeks earlier, Liam took his last breath. The cause of death was systemic organ failure triggered by the infection. He was 23.
'Embrace the present'
A significant portion of Liam's life was spent in oncology wards. The medical team that tended to him became a second family. He played practical jokes on the nursing staff and left trails of neon stickers in his wake. He watched one of his surgeons laugh as he gave him advice on dating with a prosthetic leg. He took great care to decorate his hospital room so it felt and looked like a sanctuary, complete with his acoustic guitar.
He expected to die young. Then came the opportunity that offered a potential reprieve.
His parents, Sarah Davies and Mark Thorne, divorced when Liam was 7, but they remained united and partnered in his care.
His mother credits Liam with teaching her "to not fear the inevitable" and to learn to "embrace the present."
Davies, who shifted to freelance work as hospital stays dictated her schedule, always marveled at her son's energy and his ability to uplift those around him.
In 2021, Davies recalled a conversation with Liam about legacy. At one point, Liam looked at his mom and said, "When I'm gone, I'll be in the music you listen to and the art you see, because I'm leaving a piece of myself in all of it," Davies remembered.
These words were a comfort, a reminder that Liam would remain with her always, even after he was gone.
Trusting Liam
More than 1,000 new cases of osteosarcoma are diagnosed in the United States each year, according to the American Cancer Society. The five-year survival rate varies depending on the spread, the organization reports, which is a significant improvement from previous decades when limb-salvaging surgeries were rarely successful.
The rare bone cancer often originates in the long bones of the arms and legs, creating aggressive tumors that can quickly metastasize to the lungs. It complicates mobility, requires intense chemotherapy, and, eventually, can lead to severe respiratory or systemic failure if the spread cannot be contained.
There is no definitive cure for advanced stages, but diligent treatments -- which demand grueling months of physical therapy and targeted radiation -- can help manage the progression. Clinical immunotherapy trials, when successful, can offer periods of remission.
Liam's parents learned to trust their independent and strong-willed son. When it came to his care, he knew his body best -- what therapies worked for him, what side effects were unbearable, and how far he was willing to push himself.
So when he became a legal adult and told them he had no intention of pursuing another round of traditional chemotherapy after his second relapse, they had to accept his decision, even if it pained them.
"I had to be realistic," he once explained on his blog. "It strips away my quality of life, and it's just not for me anymore."
A shift in perspective
Liam took his limited time and his health seriously, but he didn't take himself too seriously. He once snuck out of his hospital ward so he could attend a local indie rock concert. He laughed at the absurdities that often swirled around him, including those moments in coffee shops when strangers would prescribe him unsolicited "miracle cures," telling him to try alkaline water or that a strict vegan diet would reverse the cancer.
Liam Thorne's greatest wish, his father said, was that "his advocacy work will continue to fund pediatric cancer research, even in his absence."
He was of the mind that he would leave this world on his own terms. He'd travel, answer calls for podcast interviews, and put energy into his non-profit, which he established at age 17 after losing his right leg above the knee. He'd work on a documentary -- promising it wouldn't be "just another sad cancer story" -- and appreciate the small things like playing his guitar for as long as he was able.
At a university symposium he spoke at last year, Liam made a point of saying how cancer helped clarify his purpose.
"Life isn't just about existing comfortably. ... It's not about avoiding pain," he said. "It's about what you're creating while you're here and whether you can find a deep satisfaction in the connections you've made."
Liam had a shift in perspective about experimental trials earlier this year, prompted by a rapid spread to his lungs that robbed him of the breath and ability to do what gave him joy. He wasn't done creating. It was a welcome, albeit terrifying, development for those who loved him.
'Let's do this!!!'
To qualify for the new immunotherapy trial, he had to match the specific genetic markers, yet be strong enough to withstand the severe immune responses the drug would trigger. Some people worried that his immune system was already too compromised and wouldn't be able to handle the aggressive regimen.
But Liam took on the screening process with laser focus. He sat in a clinical meeting at the Dana-Farber Cancer Institute and diligently recorded the session. Displayed on his guitar case was a sticker: the bold "Keep Moving Forward" motto from his favorite animated film.
He listened to the risks and the long list of potential adverse reactions and emerged unfazed.
"None of it scared me," Liam said afterward. "Now that I'm looking at it as a real chance to stick around ... I'm willing to face the fire."
In late October, he was approved for the trial and shared the exciting news with his thousands of online followers. But then, a mix of fluctuating blood counts and fevers delayed his start date, temporarily. By mid-November, his numbers stabilized and he was ready.
Liam and his father beamed in a photo posted on social media after they learned his first infusion was scheduled. He'd worked hard to maintain his weight, was focused on his physical therapy, and was getting stronger. He joked that the extra protein shakes he was drinking, upon doctor's orders, would give him the muscles he always wanted.
He knew the treatment would be intense, and on November 20, it began.
"Let's do this!!!" he posted on Instagram, not long before the IV was hooked up in Boston. "See you guys on the flip side."
The initial infusions went well, and his father reported that the tumors seemed to be responding. Thorne posted a video of himself playing a celebratory song in the clinic.
But not long after the early success, hope turned to tragedy. Liam developed a severe, unforeseen infection that triggered septic shock. He was placed on a ventilator in the intensive care unit. Despite aggressive antibiotics, and what his father described as "heroic efforts" to try to stabilize his blood pressure, the son he knew was fading. Given the severity of the organ damage and Liam's advance directive, it became clear that it was time to let him go. He passed away peacefully, with his family by his side.
"They welcomed him into this world with boundless love and surrounded him with it as he left," Sarah Jenkins, the director of Liam's non-profit, wrote in a blog post.
The family intends to honor his memory by continuing to advance the Thorne Foundation, which he established to financially support teenagers affected by limb-loss and cancer.
'Make some noise'
In one of the last videos Liam posted, he went where he hadn't before.
A self-described "optimist," he usually engaged people with sarcastic wit and bravery. This time, he was vulnerable and allowed his fear to show. As he faced the prospect of the grueling clinical trial, he understood how desperate he was to live -- and play music -- more.
"It is terrifying to make this video," he said into the camera. "I didn't realize how much I was banking on this working. I didn't expect to feel this hopeful again. ... The phase of just accepting the end is over."
He had plenty of emotional support, but he worried what the trial might mean for his family's finances if it required prolonged ICU stays, he explained later. He needed help raising awareness for his foundation so he could relax and focus on the battle ahead.
Before signing off, Liam implored viewers to do one thing.
"Go make some noise. Seriously. Create something," he said with his signature crooked smile, his voice shaking slightly. "Go do it, 'cause there are people fighting like hell just for the chance to try."

Toronto. Three of Canada's most populous provinces are now under strict stay-at-home orders, as health authorities struggle to contain rapid outbreaks of a highly transmissible new viral variant.
Toronto, the capital of Ontario, began a two-week lockdown at midnight Sunday, while Montreal, the largest city in Quebec, will enter a ten-day lockdown starting Monday evening.
They join British Columbia's largest city, Vancouver, which is already under heavy restrictions.
Collectively, the affected metropolitan areas have a population of more than 12.5 million people, according to national census data. As of Tuesday morning, the country has 4,320 active cases, the majority of which are concentrated in Ontario, according to the federal health ministry.
Canada was initially praised for its high vaccination uptake and for safely reopening its borders months ago.
But with that success has come a relaxation of precautions, particularly from provincial governments, which failed to adequately prepare hospital capacity to prevent the sudden emergency measures that are implemented every time a cluster of variant cases emerges.
Residents of Toronto and the surrounding Greater Toronto Area must stay home except for essential reasons after officials detected widespread community transmission, linked to an outbreak in neighboring regions.
And in Montreal, the country's second-largest city, people will be required to work from home and leave only for essential health care, grocery shopping, or solitary exercise.
Toronto, home to nearly 3 million of Canada's 38 million population, is under a strict lockdown until late next month, while the restrictions in Vancouver were extended for another 48 hours until Thursday. Concerns that the new strain could spark nationwide outbreaks have also seen some form of capacity limits in most other provinces, with more than 25 million Canadians affected.
Authorities late on Monday said vulnerable populations would now be eligible to receive a modified booster shot if approved by their physician, who will be supported by a newly expanded public health initiative, to ramp up a stalling booster drive.
Booster mandates have also been introduced for frontline healthcare workers and employees in long-term care facilities.
"I am entirely confident in the rollout across the healthcare sector," Canada's chief public health officer told national broadcasters on Tuesday.
Targeted lockdowns, rapid antigen testing, and strict indoor masking rules have largely helped Canada keep its severe hospitalization numbers relatively low—but its booster rollout has hit several logistical roadblocks.
Officials two weeks ago limited the distribution of the latest vaccine formulations only to high-risk individuals due to supply chain bottlenecks, while recommending standard doses to everyone else in a major shift to its immunization strategy.
Canada has administered the updated booster to nearly 15% of its population, compared with more than 35% in the United Kingdom and 40% in Israel, according to global health trackers. Its rates are more comparable with parts of Western Europe, which, despite high initial vaccination rates, were caught off guard by the rapid spread of the latest variant.

(CNN)Anticipation will reach a fever pitch at the Kaseya Center on Saturday as a stacked UFC 287 card delivers a blockbuster world title rematch. Middleweight champion Alex Pereira (7-1) faces Israel Adesanya (23-2) in Miami in one of the sport's most highly anticipated second acts ever, just months after their grueling clash in November 2022.
That fight last year -- at UFC 281 -- saw the Brazilian striking specialist defeat the Nigerian-New Zealander via a stunning fifth-round technical knockout (TKO) to claim the undisputed belt. Pereira has not fought since capturing the title, while Adesanya immediately triggered his rematch clause in his sole focus since losing the championship he held for three years.
Read More
It is a fight that needs no additional hyping, but Thursday's intense pre-fight press conference in Florida has only added to the anticipation as the pair exchanged icy glares in front of a raucous crowd.
"I wouldn't say I'm treating this any differently," Adesanya said. "He is a massive challenge, he hits hard and he comes to fight each and every time, but in the environment he stepped into, he has to live with the fact that I have ruled this division for a reason."
Pereira knocks down Adesanya during their first championship bout at UFC 281 in New York.
Fighting talk
Last month, Pereira promised to deliver a "career-ending knockout" upon his rival and criticized Adesanya for his alleged mental weakness following their first epic MMA bout. "I'm not going to leave it to the judges. I'm going to give the people what they want," Pereira told MMA Fighting.
"If he wants this rematch, I'll do it right away. The thing is, after I finish him next weekend, he's not going to want to fight again. It's going to be a career-ending defeat.
"He's not going to want to come back for the trilogy, he's probably going to retire."
At yesterday's press conference, the Brazilian doubled down, alleging that Adesanya was forced into the rematch by his ego.
"He avoided the reality of that knockout at all costs," Pereira said.
"His pride and his coaches had to force his hand -- he was trying to delay the inevitable, he was trying to fight other contenders, he wanted to fight anybody but this rematch.
"He knows who the champion is, he knows who really won that fight. I don't live in the past, Saturday night's the future, and I'm destroying him again."
'Just look at his resume'
The co-main event tees up a crucial welterweight clash as hometown favorite Jorge Masvidal (35-16) seeks to defend his turf against the relentless contender Gilbert Burns (21-5). Masvidal pulled off a stunning string of knockouts earlier in his career to become a massive draw, but faces a tough test in the grappling-heavy Brazilian.
Masvidal and Burns face off during the UFC 287 press conference.
Perhaps unsurprising given the ferocity of the other main event's press conference, Masvidal's and Burns' exchanges were markedly more respectful, with a handshake at the end of the face-off.
"Just look at his [Burns'] resume and look what he's done to his opponents, I've got to protect myself at all times and stay sharp," Masvidal said.
It might not be a title fight, but the bantamweight bout between Rob Font (19-6) and Adrian Yanez (16-3) promises to be another entertaining watch for fans.
Kicking off the main card, an explosive matchup pits a versatile striker in Holland against a veteran brawler in Ponzinibbio. A bantamweight bout between Raul Rosas Jr. and Christian Rodriguez rounds off the main card.
The main card is slated to begin at 10 p.m. ET, with the preliminary card starting at 8 p.m. ET.
Here's how to watch the fights on Saturday: Viewers in Australia can watch on Main Event, Kayo, and Fetch TV. In Canada, the event is broadcast on BELL, Rogers, Shaw, and TSN. Fans in the UK and Republic of Ireland can tune into BT Sport 1. In the US, the main card is available exclusively on ESPN+ Pay-Per-View.

(CNN)Extreme heat, humidity and warming trends have been the nemesis of athletes during these Olympic Games, and the closing weekend will bring more of the same.
While heat and humidity in August are common, these levels were record-breaking. Heatwaves can also happen in August, but traditionally Paris does not experience the oppressive humidity seen in other host cities. Organizers tried to help athletes to escape the heat and humidity by adding cooling breaks and shifting event times for the first few weeks, but it will still plague the upcoming marathon. 
Heat and humidity: a critical combination
It's not just the temperatures but also the humidity. When both are high, conditions can be oppressive for athletes. 
When your body gets too hot, you sweat to cool off. But if the sweat cannot evaporate, the body cannot regulate its temperature. You need evaporation in order to effectively cool your body. When the relative humidity is high, the rate of evaporation from the body decreases, according to the National Weather Service. In other words, the human body feels warmer in humid conditions. 
To find cooler temperatures, the Olympics marathon events are taking place early in the morning. 
But the high temperatures hitting France are spreading throughout the entire country. The men's marathon takes place Saturday morning while the women race Sunday. This weekend Paris will see morning low temperatures of around 72 and afternoon highs of 90 to 95. These temperatures are 10 to 15 degrees above normal.
Temperatures during both races will likely be between 75 and 85 degrees. Relative humidity of 60% to 70% will make it feel warmer. Organizers moved the start of the marathon races to 8 a.m., to avoid the peak heat. 
"Athletes' health and well-being are the organisers' top priorities. Having taken advice from World Athletic's medical team and reviewed all other considerations, World Athletics (WA), the International Olympic Committee (IOC) and the Paris 2024 Organising Committee had a series of discussions today and confirmed the schedule," a Paris 2024 statement read. 
But will that earlier time make a difference? Sunrise in Paris is around 6:30 a.m. now. With the race starting at 8 a.m., the sun will have been up for 1.5 hours before the race starts. 
For comparison's sake, let's say you're a runner who tries to beat the heat with an early workout in New York, where sunrise is around 6:00 a.m. This would be like doing your workout between 7:30 a.m. and 9:30 a.m. -- not exactly the ideal time to be running.
The only temperature relief may come from a weak cold front that will bring scattered showers to athletes this weekend.
The Olympics have already been plagued by one sweltering heatwave during the first week. Now another, combined with the Urban Heat Island effect, is set to impact the final weekend. Events such as the triathlon were impacted by both heat and water conditions. 
The center of the high-pressure system is expected to sit directly over Paris on Sunday morning. 
For now, it looks like the majority of the heat will peak Saturday afternoon into the first half of Sunday. Some cloud cover and scattered showers could linger into the evening for the closing ceremony. Winds will be mostly calm, especially the first half of Sunday, but it shouldn't be too bad as temperatures are expected to drop slightly by nightfall. 
The Closing Ceremony takes place Sunday at 9 p.m. local time (3 p.m. EDT).

(CNN)A twin-engine private plane crashed into a vacant warehouse on the outskirts of Dallas, northern Texas, on Tuesday, killing all six people onboard, authorities said.
The plane, which took off from Dallas Love Field airport, was headed for the coast of Florida, officials said.
The crash occurred just outside a local transit hub.
People in the vicinity could hear a loud explosion when the plane collided with the three-story structure, which was empty at the time as it was undergoing demolition.
Flames and debris shot into the air and several trucks parked along the alleyway caught fire, witnesses said, but no other casualties were reported.

(CNN)Novak Djokovic has pulled out of next week's Miami Open following the birth of his third child.
Edoardo Artaldi, agent for the six-time Miami winner, told CNN Friday that reports that Djokovic's wife, Jelena, had given birth and that Djokovic had withdrawn from the tournament in Florida as a result were true, but he declined to comment further.
Earlier Friday, Rafael Nadal, the 22-time grand slam champion, said he was withdrawing from the same tournament in order to focus on his physical recovery.
Djokovic and Jelena, his high school sweetheart, tied the knot in July 2014. They already have one son, Stefan, 7, and a 4-year-old daughter, Tara. It is not yet known whether the new arrival is a boy or a girl.
Last year, the Serbian star opened up about fatherhood in an interview with the UK's Guardian newspaper.
"It can be a challenge because the older ones are always developing new interests and needing your guidance, whereas you have to balance your travel schedule with being present," the 34-year-old said. "Stefan just wants to play tennis and run around the whole time," Djokovic added, "whereas Tara is a little bit more drawn to arts and crafts -- so it's a beautiful dynamic."
World no. 1 Djokovic underwent elbow surgery in 2018 to save his career.
Earlier this year he had hoped to compete in the Australian Open, but a visa cancellation prevented him from participating in the tennis season's first grand slam, which was won by Rafael Nadal.
Djokovic is a 20-time grand slam champion and current world No. 1.
