Charles Leclerc's Italian-owned Ferrari team have blamed a discarded visor tear-off for wrecking the title contender's Formula One podium hopes in Belgium on Sunday. 
The Monegasque was forced to pit early in the race after the car's front-right brakes overheated. 
"During the first stint, a plastic visor tear-off got stuck inside the front brake duct of Charles's car, which led to high temperatures and caused some damage to the sensor system," said team principal Mattia Binotto. 
"So we pitted him for safety reasons."
Driving the (16) Ferrari F1-75, Charles Leclerc stops in the pitlane during the Belgian Grand Prix. 
READ: Verstappen completely dominates to win Belgian GP 
Leclerc had started fifteenth for the race at Spa-Francorchamps but finished a frustrating sixth. 
"The start was hectic, we avoided some crashes and I had some enjoyable battles through the pack. However, it was disappointing to get that penalty in the end," said the 24-year-old.
Sunday's race had massive crowds in attendance after organizers made tickets available to a record number of spectators following the lifting of pandemic restrictions. Most of the previous years' grands prix at the venue were limited.
"It's my worst nightmare that these things happen, but with so much dirt and everyone pulling off tear-offs, unfortunately they do happen," reigning world champion Max Verstappen said after winning the race for Red Bull.

(CNN)The organizers of a half-marathon in the American Midwest have apologized after the course for Saturday's race was nearly a mile too short.
The Chicago Spring Half Marathon returned to the city's lakefront after severe weather canceled last year's event, but participants were forced to run less than the regulation 13.1 miles.
"We would like to apologize to our race participants that the course today measured 0.8 miles too short," the organizers said in a statement on Facebook.
"We are incredibly disappointed that this has affected our runners and hope that it hasn't ruined the experience, at what has been a wonderful community event after a long hiatus."
Sarah Jenkins won the women's division with a time of 1:08:12, with Maria Lopez in second with a time of 1:08:45.
Jenkins had been trailing Lopez until the final mile, sparking debate on social media.
"The runner who was leading was caught off guard when the finish line appeared early. So basically, she was pacing for a longer race," tweeted one spectator.
Race director David Chen explained the mistake in a statement published Sunday.
"Despite the half-marathon route being mapped correctly in the lead-up to race day, a basic human error in placing a turn sign meant that the final course was shortened by 0.8 miles on the day," Chen said.
"We offer our sincerest apologies for this error, which was only spotted once participants had crossed the finish line, and when a correction was not possible," he added.
Chen said finishers will be given adjusted pacing metrics, and the course length would be noted for results and qualifying times.
Saturday's half-marathon isn't the first race to feature an accidental distance adjustment. In October 2021, the organizers of the Portland 10K in Oregon apologized after officials directed runners the wrong way, adding around 400 meters (1,312 feet) to the expected distance.
"We're really sorry, and we're reviewing our protocols," Pacific Runs, which organizes the Portland 10K and numerous other events in the Pacific Northwest, posted on Twitter at the time. "We hope it didn't take away from your race day and we'll be in touch with all participants directly."
And in April 2018, an Italian cycling official was reprimanded after he redirected the peloton away from the final sprint, in a mistake that almost changed the outcome of the stage.
Belgian cyclist Thomas De Gendt was leading the breakaway in the local classic, and had entered the final kilometer when the police escort he was following exited the circuit.
Despite the chasing group being only seconds behind him, De Gendt followed the motorcycles off the road. Event organizers quickly directed him back on course, and De Gendt managed to win by a few wheel lengths despite the diversion.
Here is the second text, rewritten for a more natural style.

(CNN)UN Secretary-General António Guterres has dedicated a significant portion of his annual State of the Planet address to reflect on the climate crisis and its impact on global inequality.
Speaking from the rostrum of the General Assembly hall in New York, the Secretary-General called the environmental emergency a "generational test" that has strained international alliances and fueled isolationist policies.
"Our capacity for global solidarity is severely fractured," Guterres told the delegates in the hall, as well as the millions of citizens watching the broadcast around the world. "There is an alarming trend to retreat inward, to prioritize national interests, to abandon the difficult work of multilateralism and building sustainable solutions together," he added.
The Secretary-General's traditional keynote address was impacted by extreme weather events for the second consecutive year.
Unlike in 2024, delegations were able to travel to the headquarters to listen to the critical message in person, but the number of attendees was noticeably reduced due to unprecedented flight cancellations across the eastern seaboard, triggered by a severe late-season hurricane. The region reported record-breaking rainfall on Monday, marking the highest daily precipitation since tracking began, according to meteorological data.
Last year, the UN Chief delivered the address virtually from a remote studio, with the General Assembly completely closed to the public and diplomats. On Monday morning, Guterres led a closed-door climate summit with about 500 policy experts and leaders in attendance, a UN spokesperson told CNN.
The Secretary-General noted on Tuesday that the climate emergency has also undermined diplomatic dialogue, pushing nations to embrace "short-term economic gains rather than committing to the harder path" of green transition.
"Excellencies and delegates, what would our future look like without the relentless advocacy of the countless individuals who fight to keep our ecosystems and vulnerable communities intact? In this era of climate disruption, we are witnessing this necessity more than ever," he said.
He urged the international community to "open its resources" to ensure critical financial aid, particularly climate adaptation funds, is directed to developing nations.
"We must grant support to the most affected and inspire all governments of good will to pursue the necessary measures to survive the current environmental crisis and its economic fallout. Open treasuries to ensure that essential funding -- and clean technology in particular -- are distributed to those regions that need them most. Support those who tirelessly dedicate themselves to rebuilding communities, the displaced, and the most marginalized among us," he said.
The UN leader added that the global population has become so accustomed to catastrophic weather events that "we barely register the headlines anymore." He called for an immediate cessation of fossil fuel subsidies and highlighted several regions facing severe ecological crises -- including the Horn of Africa, the Amazon basin, Southeast Asia, and island nations in the Pacific.
The 76-year-old Secretary-General also used his address to highlight the displacement of indigenous populations, which he said has worsened during the climate crisis. In a speech that marked one of his most urgent pleas yet, Guterres also emphasized the plight of climate refugees worldwide.

(CNN)A Canadian provincial lawmaker has requested a formal review of legislative rules regarding childcare, after she was informed she could no longer bring her infant daughter into the assembly chamber.
Sarah Lin, a Member of the Legislative Assembly (MLA) in British Columbia for the opposition party, said she was told by the Sergeant-at-Arms that it was a violation of parliamentary decorum to bring a child to a voting session after she attended with her daughter on Thursday. Lin shared a memorandum sent to her by the clerk's office, which referenced standing orders updated last year: "Non-members, including dependents, may not occupy a seat on the floor during official proceedings," adding that this also applied to committee rooms during broadcasted debates.
The Speaker of the House has asked the Committee on Parliamentary Privilege to examine the guidelines surrounding infants in the legislature following the incident, national broadcasters reported.
"Working mothers are told to lean in, but are literally locked out of the room it seems..." Lin wrote on Twitter following the incident.
"My daughter is barely two months old, so I cannot leave her at home, and the building does not offer drop-in childcare. So I am in an impossible situation," Lin told a local news network on Friday.
"I need to be present to represent my constituents, but I also have a nursing infant who relies on me. I was told unequivocally that the legislature considers the presence of a baby a breach of decorum and a distraction to the assembly.
"Yet, we routinely excuse members taking loud phone calls in the gallery. It feels like these rules were designed for a time when politics was exclusively the domain of older men of independent means with stay-at-home spouses," she added.
A spokesperson for the Legislative Assembly told CNN in an email that it is essential for all elected officials to participate fully in parliamentary duties without disruption.
"Members are encouraged to work with the Speaker's office and administrative staff to accommodate their needs while maintaining the formal environment of the chamber," the spokesperson said. "We are currently in active discussions with MLA Lin regarding this matter."
Lin noted that while she would never bring her active four-year-old son to a debate because "he would undoubtedly disrupt the proceedings and run down the aisles within five minutes," her newborn was "fast asleep and completely silent."
Referencing the updated standing orders, Lin, who had previously brought her first child to caucus meetings without issue, stated: "I fail to see what has changed. What is clear is that institutional barriers remain for young parents entering politics, and that ultimately hurts our democratic representation."
Lin previously clashed with the legislative administrative board earlier this year when her request for a remote proxy vote during her designated maternity leave was denied.
Responding to a conservative commentator's suggestion on Monday that she should "stay home and focus on being a mother," Lin tweeted: "Without a proxy system, my district goes unrepresented if I stay home. I am doing both my jobs."
In the US, Senator Tammy Duckworth made history in 2018 as she became the first senator to cast a vote on the Senate floor with her newborn by her side, days after the Senate changed longstanding rules to allow newborns onto the Senate floor during votes.
Similarly, in 2019, New Zealand MP Tāmati Coffey brought his six-week-old son into the debating chamber, where the baby was famously cradled by the house speaker. Prime Minister Jacinda Ardern also made headlines in 2018 when she brought her three-month-old daughter to the United Nations General Assembly.
However, the presence of children in political arenas has often sparked backlash. In 2015, European Parliament member Licia Ronzulli faced mixed reactions after frequently voting with her daughter in a sling, highlighting the ongoing global debate over work-life balance in high-level politics.

(CNN)Barcelona will play Arsenal in a star-studded Europa League knockout round tie after fixtures were redrawn following a software malfunction.
European football's governing body UEFA declared the initial draw void earlier on Monday after Juventus was drawn by mistake and the Italian Serie A club will probably be relieved. Juventus was initially drawn against Barcelona but will now face a slightly easier two-legged tie against Portuguese side Sporting CP -- although Ruben Amorim's side is a formidable force in Portugal.
Europa League knockout draw Sevilla vs. PSV Eindhoven Sporting CP vs. Juventus Bayer Leverkusen vs. Monaco Ajax vs. Union Berlin Shakhtar Donetsk vs. Rennes FC Salzburg vs. AS Roma Manchester United vs. Real Betis Barcelona vs. Arsenal
However, it means fans will be denied the mouthwatering prospect of watching Robert Lewandowski renew his rivalry with Juventus -- for now at least.
The Catalan side will instead face the tricky prospect of playing current Premier League leader Arsenal. Mikel Arteta's team is one of the competition's most dynamic sides and has been in good form domestically of late.
It will also see Pierre-Emerick Aubameyang, a former captain of Arsenal, return to the Emirates Stadium.
Héctor Bellerín, Arsenal's former defender, will also return to face the club where he won multiple FA Cup titles. The defender moved to Barcelona at the start of the season but has seen limited action due to tactical choices.
Elsewhere, reigning Conference League champion AS Roma will face Austrian side FC Salzburg -- the only fixture that remained the same from the initial draw.
Last year's quarter-finalist Manchester United will face Real Betis as it searches for its second Europa League trophy as six-time winner Sevilla takes on PSV Eindhoven. Meanwhile, one of the tournament favorites Ajax will face Union Berlin while impressive Bundesliga side Bayer Leverkusen faces Monaco.
The first legs of the knockout ties are scheduled for February 16 and 23 with the returns scheduled for March 9 and 16. The final is set to take place in Budapest, Hungary, on May 31.

(CNN)At least 14 people died when their inflatable dinghy capsized off the Spanish island of Gran Canaria in the Atlantic Ocean on Tuesday, in the latest maritime tragedy involving migrants this month, the country's maritime rescue service said.
Authorities launched a frantic search and rescue mission overnight south of Gran Canaria. Helicopters and rescue vessels were dispatched to the region, the rescue agency said.
Spain is one of the primary entry points into the European Union for migrants and asylum seekers from West Africa and the Maghreb, though the dangerous Atlantic route has seen a surge in traffic since 2019, as patrols increased in the Mediterranean.
The country's EFE news agency reported that the bodies of 10 men, three women, and a toddler were retrieved from the water.
Seventy-five people were suspected to have been aboard the vessel, which according to the rescue service was believed to have departed from the coast of Western Sahara.
The exact reasons for the vessel overturning remained unknown.
Fernando Grande-Marlaska, Spain's interior minister, stated that human smuggling networks were to blame for the fatalities.
The smuggling networks "show zero regard for human safety, packing dozens of vulnerable individuals, lacking flotation devices, into flimsy craft completely unsuited for the open ocean," he said in a press release.
Earlier Tuesday, Spanish officials reported they had retrieved eight bodies following a separate shipwreck, when a wooden boat transporting migrants sank near the coast of Tenerife on Monday.
Another 82 individuals were rescued during that mission. The maritime rescue service stated preliminary reports indicated those migrants had departed from Senegal.
Over the weekend, a dinghy estimated to have been carrying up to 60 migrants deflated off the coast of Fuerteventura, leaving several dozen people unaccounted for.

New York (CNN Business)For many years, the world's most popular technology stocks have been the so-called FAANGs: Facebook, Amazon, Apple, Netflix and Google. But given that Facebook (now Meta) and Netflix have seen massive shifts in their market dominance following recent economic changes, it might be time for investors to stop lumping all of the tech giants together.
"The FAANGs had their day in the sun and that has faded," said Sarah Lin, senior analyst at Horizon Wealth.
Several major US fund managers have reduced legacy social media stocks from portfolios to a fraction of their former weight. Trading shares of several leading consumer tech companies, has seen heavy volatility amid shifting user habits. And the broader tech sector has been under pressure since late 2021, the year after the pandemic boom.
Tech could face further corrections within months
"The idea that a company as large as Meta can lose so much value is a big deal," Lin said.
Read More
It seems likely that FAANG will not be the primary driver of top tech funds anytime soon. Even for those retail traders still willing to invest in legacy tech assets, it's not clear what comes next.
"Some investors are asking about exposure to artificial intelligence in tech funds. With indexes starting to rebalance away from the old guard, it's still a wait-and-see game," said Marcus Thorne, head of equities at Vanguard Partners.
For investors looking to get exposure to big tech, Lin said, each company should be looked at individually.
"The name of the game will be differentiation. Don't invest in tech based on an acronym," Lin said. "It's always strange to say that Apple and Netflix are the same thing, for example. They're not."
Forget the FAANGs and look at MATANA or the Magnificent Seven?
To stick with the acronyms, the inclusion of Microsoft and Nvidia has essentially turned the FAANGs into MATANA (Microsoft, Apple, Tesla, Alphabet, Nvidia, Amazon) — and that could be a permanent change, said David Vance, managing partner with IndexIQ, a global index provider.
"Will investors ever embrace the old FAANG again? If growth slows, it's a moot point. But it's also hard to believe that people will ignore AI anytime soon," Vance told CNN Business.
Vance said some investors may start to look at other tech sectors to replace streaming and social media, such as cloud computing and semiconductor manufacturing. The FAANG could become the FATANG. He added that AMD, Broadcom and Salesforce are intriguing, as are Adobe and Oracle. You could dub Microsoft, Nvidia, Apple and Tesla the MNAT markets.
"People love their acronyms," Vance joked.
Of course, any tech market in consumer software — think social media — is inherently risky because of how close it is financially to digital ad-spending cycles. So other ad-reliant companies may be a hard sell for institutional investors.
AI boom has sparked a scramble for semiconductors
Other experts argue that investors are looking more at individual companies in tech and less at the sectors themselves.
"A typical investor sees big tech as a class of stocks. It's a piece of a portfolio," said Elena Rostova, US investment analyst at TradeNet.
"The tech landscape has been changing for awhile," Rostova added, noting that many investors have been skeptical of social media since major privacy updates in 2021. "There has been more anxiety."

When armed conflict swept rapidly across the region, members of a national youth sports program found themselves confronting a reality they had never imagined. Training sessions ended overnight. Team facilities were abandoned. Athletes erased online profiles, destroyed documents linking them to international organizations, and sought refuge with relatives or trusted friends.
Within days, a small network of coaches, human rights advocates, former athletes, and volunteers began working across multiple countries to coordinate an evacuation effort. The challenge was immense. Communications were unreliable, transportation routes shifted constantly, and security conditions deteriorated by the hour.
For many of those involved, the mission became personal. Several coaches had spent years mentoring the athletes and had watched them become symbols of opportunity and social change in communities where such opportunities had long been limited. What began as a sports initiative had gradually evolved into something much larger: a platform for education, leadership, and independence.
“The team represented far more than competition,” one former coach explained. “For many of these young people, sport opened doors that had previously been closed. It gave them confidence, visibility, and a sense that their futures could be different.”
As reports of violence increased, organizers worked around the clock. Volunteers in different time zones exchanged information continuously, assembling lists, arranging documentation, and searching for safe routes. Many slept only a few hours each night while monitoring developments and responding to urgent messages.
The athletes themselves faced extraordinary risks. Some traveled long distances through crowded checkpoints. Others waited for days with limited food, water, and shelter. Families were forced to make difficult decisions about whether to remain together or separate temporarily in hopes that at least some members could reach safety.
Eventually, after a series of complex negotiations involving government agencies, nonprofit organizations, and international partners, dozens of athletes, staff members, and relatives were evacuated from the country. Images confirming their departure brought an outpouring of relief among those who had worked to help them.
Yet the successful operation was accompanied by mixed emotions. While many had escaped immediate danger, others remained behind. Organizers described feelings of gratitude, exhaustion, and guilt, questioning whether more could have been done for those still at risk.
The uncertainty did not end with evacuation. The athletes now faced the challenge of rebuilding their lives in unfamiliar places, adapting to new cultures, languages, and educational systems. Support organizations began coordinating housing, counseling, and long-term resettlement assistance.
Despite the difficulties ahead, those closest to the team expressed confidence in their resilience. Years of overcoming obstacles had prepared them for adversity, even on a scale few could have anticipated.
“These young people have already shown extraordinary courage,” said one advocate. “The circumstances have changed, but their determination has not. The next chapter of their lives may begin in a different country, but the qualities that carried them this far remain the same.”
For many participants in the rescue effort, the experience left a lasting impression. They spoke not only of the lives saved, but also of the responsibility they felt toward communities whose futures had suddenly become uncertain. Long after the headlines faded, they knew the work would continue.

Team Asia secured a dramatic victory in the International Women's Golf Challenge on Sunday after edging Team North America 14.5-13.5 at Seaside Links in California. The decisive moment came when rookie golfer Hana Kim calmly sank a six-foot putt on the final hole to defeat American veteran Brooke Jensen, sealing her team's first title defense in the prestigious tournament that brings together top female golfers from across the globe.
The victory was confirmed shortly afterward when teammate Yuki Tanaka halved the final match, prompting emotional celebrations among the Asian squad. Team captain Mi-Young Park was embraced by players and staff as the significance of the achievement became clear. Park, who had guided the team to victory two years earlier, became the first captain in the competition's history to win consecutive titles.
Speaking after the event, Park said she was proud of her players and hinted that this would likely be her final time leading the team. "We've built something special together," she told reporters. "There are many talented former players who could do this role well, and I think it's time for someone else to have the opportunity. These experiences have meant a great deal to me."
Messages of congratulations quickly appeared online from players and officials around the world. Among them was men's team captain David Reynolds, whose squad will compete in an international event later this month. "An incredible display of determination and teamwork," Reynolds wrote on social media.
The final day began with Team Asia holding an 8.5-7.5 lead and needing six points from the singles matches to retain the trophy. The visitors started strongly, winning three of the opening four contests and increasing their advantage. However, Team North America responded with a series of impressive performances, collecting points in several consecutive matches and narrowing the gap as the competition entered its closing stages.
With thousands of spectators lining the fairways and tension building throughout the afternoon, Kim delivered the shot that ultimately secured victory. The 24-year-old had only recently earned her place on the professional circuit after winning a regional tournament in South Korea earlier in the summer.
"I can't really believe it yet," Kim said afterward. "When I stood over that final putt, I knew how important it was. I tried to stay calm and trust my routine. Seeing the ball drop was one of the best moments of my career."
Another standout performer was rookie Mei Lin, who completed the tournament unbeaten. The 25-year-old earned four points from five matches and consistently delivered under pressure. Her convincing victory in the singles session helped establish momentum for Team Asia early in the day.
Captain Park praised Lin's contribution but emphasized the collective effort behind the victory. "Every player contributed something important," she said. "This result belongs to the entire team."
The tournament was not without controversy. During Saturday's four-ball session, a disputed ruling involving a ball near the edge of the cup led to confusion among players and officials. Video clips of the incident quickly circulated online, prompting debate among fans and commentators about whether the correct procedure had been followed.
Several prominent golfers weighed in on social media, with opinions divided over the ruling. Some defended the officials' decision, while others argued that the situation could have been handled differently. The discussion continued long after play had ended, adding another layer of drama to an already memorable tournament.

Two athletes from South Sudan have arrived safely at the World Athletics Championship host city after an international effort ensured their departure from a region affected by escalating conflict, organizers confirmed on Friday.
Sprinter Daniel Lado and middle-distance runner Mary Ajak left their home country several days earlier and spent time training at a high-performance center in Italy before traveling to the competition venue. Their participation had previously been in doubt after worsening security conditions disrupted transportation and prevented the national team from making final travel arrangements.
The athletes' arrival marks the end of a complex operation involving sports organizations, humanitarian groups, and government agencies. Earlier this month, officials from the South Sudan Athletics Federation had announced that the pair would likely miss the championships because of the unstable situation in the country.
In a statement released by championship organizers, World Athletics President Sebastian Coe praised the coordinated effort that enabled the athletes to attend the event.
"Our primary concern throughout this process has been the safety and well-being of the athletes," Coe said. "Just a short time ago, it appeared impossible for them to travel and compete. Thanks to the cooperation of numerous organizations and governments, they are now here and ready to represent their nation on the world stage."
According to officials, several international partners helped arrange transportation, temporary accommodation, training facilities, and logistical support. Organizers described the operation as an example of how the global sporting community can work together during times of crisis.
The two athletes were not present when national delegations first arrived earlier in the week, prompting concern among supporters and fellow competitors. However, their arrival has been welcomed by athletes from around the world, many of whom expressed support on social media.
For Ajak, the opportunity carries particular significance. She will become one of the few female athletes from her country to compete at the championships in recent years. Scheduled to race in the women's 800 meters, she said she was grateful for the chance to participate despite the difficulties.
"I never stopped hoping that I would make it here," Ajak said. "There were moments when everything seemed uncertain, but I kept training and believing that there would be a way."
Lado is expected to compete in the men's 400-meter event later next week. He said the experience of reaching the championships after such challenges has already become one of the most meaningful moments of his career.
Championship officials stated that support services will remain available to both athletes throughout the event and afterward if needed. They emphasized that ensuring the competitors' welfare remains a top priority as they prepare to take part in their respective events.

(CNN) Professional tennis players are receiving a competitive share of the sport's growing revenues, according to one of the game's most influential administrators, Mark Reynolds. Reynolds spoke after the International Tennis Federation unveiled a major long-term partnership with a global technology company, a deal that he described as a transformative step for the sport.
Beginning next season, the agreement will bring substantial investment into several levels of professional tennis. According to the federation, total prize money across its premier tournaments will surpass $300 million for the first time, while a number of lower-tier events will also see guaranteed increases in funding.
The announcement arrives at a time when questions are being raised about the future structure of professional tennis. In recent months, reports have emerged about a proposed independent circuit backed by private investors that would feature many of the world's top-ranked players competing in a limited number of high-profile events.
Supporters of the concept argue that it could generate greater earnings and more frequent matchups between elite players. Several athletes have acknowledged the appeal of seeing the sport's biggest stars face one another more often throughout the season.
One player who addressed the issue recently was Grand Slam champion Alex Moreno. Speaking ahead of a tournament in Mexico, Moreno suggested that fans would likely welcome more encounters between leading competitors.
"The public wants to see the best players competing against each other as often as possible," Moreno said. "The challenge is finding the right balance between creating those opportunities and preserving the flexibility players currently have."
Unlike many major North American sports leagues, professional tennis does not operate under a traditional team-based revenue-sharing structure. Nevertheless, prize funds, sponsorship opportunities, and performance bonuses have grown significantly in recent years.
Reynolds believes comparisons with leagues such as the NBA or NFL can be misleading. While acknowledging that the systems differ considerably, he argued that tennis players are benefiting from the sport's commercial success.
"Our athletes are compensated at a very high level," Reynolds said. "People often compare tennis directly to team sports, but the business models are fundamentally different. When you look at the total opportunities available to players, I believe the current system remains highly competitive."
According to Reynolds, the newly announced partnership was the result of negotiations that had been underway for several years and was not influenced by discussions surrounding alternative tours.
Beyond the headline increase in prize money, he emphasized that the investment would support player development programs, coaching resources, sports science initiatives, and athlete welfare services.
The federation also plans to expand support for junior competitors and athletes transitioning from developmental circuits to the professional ranks. Additional resources will be directed toward injury prevention, nutrition programs, and mental health services.
Reynolds added that inclusivity would remain a central focus. Funding has been allocated to wheelchair tennis, community outreach projects, and grassroots programs designed to make the sport accessible to a wider range of participants.
"This is about much more than rewarding the top players," he said. "We want to strengthen every level of the game and ensure that future generations have more opportunities to succeed."

(CNN) Andy Murray found himself in a lighthearted exchange with the chair umpire during Tuesday's first-round match at the Geneva Open after a sudden rain shower interrupted his service motion.
As Murray prepared to serve at a crucial stage of the second set, a brief burst of rain began falling across the court. The former world No. 1 stepped away from the baseline and waited for conditions to improve, prompting the umpire to warn him about exceeding the allotted time between points.
"You want me to serve in that?" Murray asked while gesturing toward the sky. "I can control where I hit the ball, but I can't control when the clouds decide to open up."
The three-time Grand Slam champion smiled as the crowd reacted to the exchange, but the warning stood. Play continued shortly afterward once the rain eased.
Murray ultimately fell 6-4, 3-6, 6-3 to Spain's Roberto Carvajal, a lower-ranked opponent who capitalized on a strong final set to secure the upset victory.
The defeat continues a difficult run of results for Murray as he attempts to build momentum ahead of the clay-court season's biggest events. Injuries and inconsistent form have limited his progress this year, and he has struggled to put together consecutive wins on tour.
For Carvajal, the victory marked another impressive result against a former major champion. The Spaniard has now won three of his last four matches against players who have previously been ranked inside the world's top 10.

(CNN) Fueled by an energetic home crowd and a series of dominant performances, Team International stormed to a 7-3 lead over Team Europe after the opening day of the Continental Cup in Melbourne, Australia.
The hosts set the tone early during the morning session, winning three of the four opening matches to establish control of the competition. While Europe's top pairing of Viktor Hansen and Marco Silva managed to secure an important point, the International side consistently outperformed its opponents in key moments.
Rookies Ethan Cole and Liam Brooks delivered one of the standout performances of the day, cruising to a convincing 6&5 victory over experienced European duo Daniel Hart and Oliver Jensen. Elsewhere, veterans Ryan Carter and Ben Matthews edged a tightly contested match, while world No. 4 Lucas Grant teamed up with Michael Reed to earn another point for the hosts.
The afternoon session brought little relief for Europe. Grant and Brooks continued their impressive form with a narrow victory, and the pairing of Carter and Jason Walker overwhelmed their opponents to extend the lead further.
One of the day's most dramatic contests featured European stars Hansen and Stefan Berg against Cole and Matthews. The lead changed hands several times before Berg holed a lengthy birdie putt on the final hole, allowing Europe to salvage a valuable half-point.
The final match of the session also went down to the wire. European pair Silva and Jensen appeared on course for victory after building an early advantage, but the International team fought back over the closing stretch. Neither side could gain a decisive edge, and the match ended all square.
Excitement had been building around the venue from the early hours of the morning. Thousands of spectators packed the grandstands well before the first groups teed off, creating a lively atmosphere throughout the course.
Calm weather conditions during the opening session encouraged aggressive play, and scoring opportunities were plentiful. Several matches remained close through the opening holes, but the International team gradually separated itself with a combination of accurate approach shots and clutch putting.
Cole and Brooks were particularly impressive. The young pair birdied four of their first six holes and never allowed their opponents back into the contest. Their dominant display quickly became one of the major talking points of the day.
Conditions became more challenging in the afternoon as strong coastal winds swept across the course. Players were forced to adjust their club selections and shot strategies, but the home side appeared more comfortable dealing with the changing conditions.
Grant and Brooks adapted quickly, building an early lead that they protected through the closing holes. Carter and Walker were equally effective, producing a steady stream of birdies while avoiding costly mistakes.
Despite trailing by four points, Team Europe remained optimistic after play concluded. Captain Henrik Olsen praised his players' determination and insisted that the competition remained far from over.
"There is still a long way to go," Olsen told reporters. "We've been in difficult situations before. We need to play better tomorrow, but everyone still believes we can turn this around."

Editor's Note: This article is part of an ongoing series examining the experiences of women in public and religious spaces around the world.
(CNN) Several women who attended a major religious gathering in South Asia have told CNN they experienced incidents of sexual harassment during the event, describing encounters that ranged from unwanted touching to verbal abuse in densely crowded areas.
The women said they decided to speak publicly after a social media post describing a similar experience gained widespread attention online earlier this year. The post prompted dozens of responses from women who said they had faced comparable situations during large-scale religious events.
The gathering attracts millions of worshipers annually and is considered one of the most significant spiritual occasions for participants. Organizers face major logistical challenges due to the size of the crowds, particularly in areas where pilgrims move through narrow routes or gather at key ceremonial locations.
Several women told CNN that the incidents occurred in crowded sections where movement was restricted and individuals were often pressed closely together. Some said they reported their experiences to security personnel, while others chose not to out of fear, embarrassment, or uncertainty about how their complaints would be received.
Event authorities said in a statement that harassment of any kind is unacceptable and that reports of misconduct are taken seriously. Officials added that additional security personnel, including more female officers, have been deployed in recent years to improve safety and encourage reporting.
The following accounts have been edited for clarity and length. Some interviewees requested anonymity because of concerns about public reaction.
Anonymous woman, Malaysia
I attended the gathering in 2018 with several relatives. During one of the busiest periods, I became separated from my family for a short time. While moving through a crowded passageway, I felt someone repeatedly touching my back and waist.
At first, I assumed it was accidental because there were so many people around me. But it continued even after I changed direction. When I turned around, the man moved away quickly and disappeared into the crowd.
I remember feeling shocked more than anything else. I had traveled there for spiritual reasons, and the last thing I expected was to be worried about my personal safety.
Anonymous woman, Turkey
My experience happened in broad daylight. I was walking with friends toward a prayer area when a man began making comments about my appearance. We ignored him and continued walking, but he followed us for several minutes.
Later that day, in a more crowded area, someone grabbed my arm from behind. I pulled away immediately, but the incident left me feeling anxious for the rest of the trip.
I never reported it because I assumed nothing would come of it. Looking back, I wish I had at least tried.
Anonymous woman, Nigeria
I was 19 years old when I attended the event for the first time. The crowds were enormous, and there were moments when it was difficult to move freely.
During one procession, a man standing behind me repeatedly pushed against me despite having plenty of space to step back. I felt uncomfortable and tried to move away, but there was little room.
For a long time, I convinced myself that I might have misunderstood what happened. Reading other women's stories later made me realize that many people had experienced similar situations.
Sara Ahmed, living in Canada
I attended with my parents several years ago. One incident happened near a marketplace where pilgrims often gathered after prayers.
A man approached and began asking personal questions. When I ignored him, he continued following me for several minutes. Eventually, I joined a larger group of women and he left.
Nothing physical happened, but the experience stayed with me because I felt vulnerable in a place where I expected to feel secure.
Many people assume these things cannot happen in religious settings, but large crowds can create opportunities for misconduct if safeguards are not strong enough.
Anonymous woman, Jordan
I have attended the gathering twice. During my first visit, I experienced unwanted touching in a crowded section near one of the main ritual sites.
I did not tell anyone at the time. I was young, embarrassed, and worried that people would question my actions rather than the behavior of the person responsible.
Years later, I shared the story with friends and discovered that some of them had experienced similar incidents. That was the first time I realized how common these conversations were becoming.

(CNN) After more than a decade in power, Prime Minister Sofia Lindberg is preparing to leave office, marking the end of an era that has shaped politics in Sweden and across Northern Europe.
For years, political observers assumed that Lindberg's center-right Alliance Party would remain the dominant force even after her departure. However, recent opinion polls have complicated that assumption. A survey published last week placed the Progressive Green Movement ahead of the Alliance for the first time in nearly a decade, triggering intense debate about the country's political future.
While Alliance officials argued that the result reflected a temporary surge following the Greens' selection of a new leader, analysts say the broader trend cannot be ignored. Polling throughout the year has suggested growing support for environmental and social reform policies, particularly among younger and urban voters.
Even if the Greens fail to win the largest share of the vote, many experts believe they are now positioned to play a central role in coalition negotiations after next year's election.
"The question is no longer whether the Greens will be part of government," said Helena Markström, a political scientist at Stockholm University. "The question is how much influence they will have over the agenda."
Despite their rise, few expect a dramatic shift in Sweden's foreign policy. Over recent years, mainstream parties have increasingly adopted positions on climate policy, renewable energy, and sustainable development that were once associated almost exclusively with the Greens.
The implications could nevertheless extend beyond Sweden. As one of the European Union's more influential member states, Sweden often plays an important role in debates over environmental regulation, trade policy, and digital governance.
A Green-led government would also symbolize a broader transformation taking place across Europe, where traditional center-right and center-left parties have struggled to maintain their historic dominance.
Many political strategists argue that established parties are facing pressure from multiple directions. Progressive movements are attracting younger voters concerned about climate change and inequality, while nationalist and anti-establishment groups continue to draw support from voters frustrated with political institutions.
"The old political coalitions are becoming harder to sustain," said one European diplomat. "Voters are increasingly looking for alternatives, whether on the left, the right, or outside the traditional system altogether."
That dynamic is creating uncertainty in Brussels, where policymakers are already preparing for a series of elections across the continent. Several European governments face growing pressure from emerging parties that challenge long-standing assumptions about integration and economic policy.
The situation is particularly significant because neighboring countries are undergoing political shifts of their own. In Finland and Denmark, newer parties have gained influence, while debates over immigration, energy policy, and economic competitiveness continue to reshape electoral landscapes.
A more environmentally focused government in Sweden could strengthen efforts to accelerate the EU's climate agenda. However, differences with other member states could complicate negotiations on industrial policy, energy security, and trade.
At the same time, some analysts caution against overstating the likelihood of radical change. Coalition politics often require compromise, and even a strong Green showing would almost certainly require cooperation with other parties.
Still, the broader message is clear. Political loyalties that once seemed stable are becoming more fluid across Europe. Whether the next government is led by the Alliance Party or the Greens, the election will serve as another test of how traditional political institutions adapt to an increasingly fragmented electorate.
"The era of predictable politics is fading," Markström said. "The parties that succeed will be the ones that recognize how quickly voters' priorities are evolving."

(CNN) It was a night of celebration and dominance for the Denver Nuggets as they officially began their title defense in front of a packed home crowd.
Before tipoff of the 2024-25 NBA season, the franchise honored its championship-winning team with a banner-raising ceremony and the presentation of championship rings. Players, coaches, and staff gathered at center court as highlights from the historic season played on the arena's video boards, drawing loud cheers from fans.
The championship rings were designed to reflect key moments from the title-winning campaign. Team officials said the rings feature hundreds of diamonds symbolizing victories accumulated throughout the season, while custom details commemorate the franchise's first NBA championship and its playoff run.
Several elements were personalized for each player, including engraved statistics and references to memorable postseason performances. The organization described the rings as a tribute to both the team's success and the city's support during the championship journey.
The festivities continued once the game began.
Led by reigning Finals MVP Nikola Jokić, the Nuggets defeated the Dallas Mavericks 118-101 in an impressive opening-night performance. Jokić recorded 29 points, 13 rebounds, and 11 assists, securing a triple-double as Denver controlled much of the contest.
Dallas remained competitive through the first half behind Luka Dončić's scoring, but the Nuggets gradually pulled away in the third quarter with a series of defensive stops and efficient offensive possessions.
After the victory, Jokić acknowledged the significance of the ceremony but emphasized that the team was already focused on the future.
"The banner is something that will always be there," he said. "We accomplished something special, but a new season starts now. We have to keep improving and keep working if we want another opportunity."
Meanwhile, another championship contender experienced a disappointing start to its campaign.
The Phoenix Suns opened their season with a 115-108 home loss to the Minnesota Timberwolves. Despite strong performances from Devin Booker and Kevin Durant, the Suns struggled to contain Minnesota's balanced attack during the second half.
Booker finished with 31 points, while Durant added 28. However, Phoenix received limited offensive production from the rest of its lineup as Minnesota steadily built momentum after halftime.
Anthony Edwards led the Timberwolves with 27 points and provided several key plays late in the game to secure the victory.
Afterward, Edwards praised his team's resilience but said there was still significant room for improvement.
"We didn't play a perfect game," he told reporters. "There are a lot of things we can clean up. But winning on opening night against a team like that gives us confidence."
Minnesota's victory highlighted one of the team's strengths: depth. Contributions came from throughout the roster, allowing the Timberwolves to withstand scoring runs and maintain control in crucial moments.
For Denver, however, the evening belonged to celebration. A championship banner now hangs permanently above the court, and the team's successful start offered an encouraging reminder of why many expect the Nuggets to remain among the league's top contenders this season.

(CNN) National team defender Alex Morgan has announced that he has been diagnosed with stage one testicular cancer and will temporarily step away from professional soccer while undergoing treatment.
The 26-year-old, who plays for Championship club Bristol City, shared the news in a statement released on social media Thursday, explaining that doctors detected the condition during a series of medical examinations earlier this month.
"I have recently been diagnosed with stage one testicular cancer and will begin treatment in the coming days," Morgan wrote.
According to health authorities, testicular cancer is one of the most treatable forms of cancer when detected early. Treatment options vary depending on the stage and type of the disease but often have high success rates.
Morgan said the diagnosis had come as an unexpected shock to both him and his family but emphasized that doctors were optimistic about his recovery.
"Naturally, receiving this news has been difficult, but I have been reassured by the specialists overseeing my care that the outlook is very positive," he said. "My focus now is on completing treatment, recovering fully, and returning to football when the time is right."
The defender had recently withdrawn from international duty after reporting persistent discomfort that required further medical assessment.
In his statement, Morgan thanked the medical staff at both club and national team level for their support and quick action.
"I am incredibly grateful to the doctors, nurses, and specialists who have guided me through this process so far," he said. "I also want to thank the medical teams at Bristol City and the national federation for acting quickly and ensuring I received the tests I needed."
Morgan has been a regular presence for club and country over the past several seasons and is regarded as one of the brightest defensive talents in the national setup.
Following the announcement, messages of support poured in from teammates, clubs, coaches, and supporters across the football community.
Bristol City released a statement expressing its full backing for the player during his recovery.
"Alex's health and well-being are our only priorities at this time," the club said. "He will receive every possible support from the club, and there will be no pressure regarding his return to football."
Club officials added that Morgan would be given as much time as necessary to focus on treatment and rehabilitation.
The player also asked for privacy while he undergoes medical care.
"I appreciate the kindness and encouragement people have already shown," he wrote. "At the same time, I hope everyone will respect the privacy of myself and my family as we navigate the months ahead."
He concluded by thanking supporters for their messages and expressing confidence that he would return to the sport in the future.
"I look forward to being back on the pitch and doing what I love again. Until then, my attention is fully on recovery."

(CNN) College volleyball player Emma Richardson was preparing for an away match with her team when she received news that severe flooding had swept through her hometown in eastern Tennessee.
"I couldn't believe what I was seeing," Richardson told CNN Sports as she described viewing photos and videos of the destruction on her phone. "You recognize the streets and buildings, but everything looks completely different."
The flooding followed days of heavy rainfall and left large sections of the community underwater. Homes, businesses, and public facilities suffered extensive damage, forcing many residents to evacuate.
For Richardson, the disaster felt deeply personal.
"This is the place where I grew up," she said. "My family is here, my friends are here, and so many of the people who helped me along the way still live in town. Seeing it happen was heartbreaking."
After competing in the match, the senior athlete decided she wanted to contribute to recovery efforts in any way she could.
Within days, Richardson launched an online fundraising campaign to support families affected by the flooding. She shared the initiative through social media and encouraged fellow athletes, alumni, and supporters to spread the word.
University volleyball player Emma Richardson created a fundraiser for flood victims in her hometown.
Richardson said she was inspired by athletes who have used their public profiles to support communities during times of crisis.
"You see people step up when disaster strikes, and it reminds you that even small actions can make a difference," she said. "I figured if I could help raise awareness and bring in some donations, it was worth trying."
The response exceeded her expectations.
By the end of the week, the fundraiser had collected more than $120,000 from thousands of donors across the country. The proceeds are expected to support local relief organizations, emergency assistance programs, and rebuilding efforts.
Earlier this week, Richardson returned home to volunteer alongside residents and local organizations. She spent several days helping remove debris, distributing supplies, and assisting families as they began the long recovery process.
"It means a lot to be here in person," she said. "Donating money is important, but being able to work alongside people and listen to what they're going through makes it feel more real."
The experience reinforced what she believes is one of her hometown's defining qualities.
"People here look after each other," Richardson said. "Even after everything that's happened, you can see neighbors helping neighbors everywhere you go."
She added that giving back to the community that supported her athletic journey was something she felt compelled to do.
"This town has given me so much over the years. Being able to help, even in a small way, feels like the least I can do."

(CNN) For many members of Canada's Indigenous communities, the past few years have brought a mix of recognition, frustration, and renewed demands for change.
The discovery of unmarked graves at former residential school sites reignited a national conversation about historical injustices and the ongoing effects of policies that separated Indigenous children from their families. While governments and institutions pledged action, many Indigenous leaders say meaningful progress has been uneven.
"It brought a lot of emotions back to the surface," said community advocate Rachel Whitecloud. "Many people felt grief, but also a sense that stories our families had been telling for generations were finally being heard."
Whitecloud, who works with youth programs in Manitoba, said the public response initially gave many people hope that long-standing issues would receive greater attention.
"There was a feeling that maybe things would be different this time," she said. "That people would listen, learn, and take responsibility."
Yet that optimism has often been tempered by disappointment. Indigenous activists argue that public expressions of support have not always translated into concrete improvements in areas such as housing, healthcare, education, and access to clean drinking water.
For many, media coverage has become part of the debate itself. Some advocates say news organizations have increased attention to Indigenous issues, while others argue that coverage can still be inconsistent or overly focused on moments of crisis.
"There are times when our communities are visible only when something tragic happens," said university lecturer Daniel Cardinal. "The everyday realities, the successes, the resilience—those stories don't always receive the same attention."
The discussion has unfolded alongside broader conversations about inequality and representation taking place across Canada. Public institutions have faced growing pressure to address historical grievances and improve relationships with Indigenous peoples.
Younger Indigenous creators have increasingly turned to social media and independent platforms to tell their own stories. Among them is content producer Maya Stone, whose online videos discussing Indigenous identity, culture, and politics attract hundreds of thousands of views each month.
"For a long time, other people were speaking about us," Stone said. "Now more Indigenous people are speaking for themselves and building their own audiences."
She believes digital platforms have helped create space for conversations that were previously overlooked by mainstream outlets.
At the same time, many activists caution that awareness alone is not enough. Symbolic gestures, they argue, must be followed by lasting reforms.
"There is more public understanding than there used to be," Whitecloud said. "But understanding is only the beginning. The real question is what happens next."
Government officials have acknowledged that significant challenges remain and have pointed to ongoing initiatives aimed at improving infrastructure, expanding services, and supporting Indigenous-led programs. Critics, however, say progress has been too slow.
Despite differing views on the pace of change, there is broad agreement that the national conversation has shifted. Issues that once received limited public attention are now being discussed more openly across the country.
Whether that leads to lasting transformation remains uncertain. But many Indigenous Canadians say the expectation for accountability is stronger than it was a decade ago.
"The conversation isn't going away," Cardinal said. "People have waited too long for that."

A version of this story appeared in the September 12 edition of CNN's Royal News, a weekly newsletter covering developments involving the British royal family, public engagements, and events behind palace walls.
London (CNN) When photographs emerged showing Prince George accompanying his parents at a series of official engagements over the summer, royal observers quickly began debating what role the young prince might play in the future of the monarchy.
The discussion reflects a broader fascination with the royal line of succession and how the institution prepares younger generations for public life. As the eldest child of Prince William and Catherine, Princess of Wales, George remains second in line to the throne behind his father.
While public appearances by senior royals are often carefully planned, constitutional experts point out that succession itself is governed by established law. Individual family members do not choose their place in the order, nor can it be altered by personal preference.
A file photograph shows Prince William and Prince George attending a public event together.
The structure has remained largely unchanged for generations, although Parliament has occasionally updated the rules. In 2013, changes ended the system under which younger sons could take precedence over older daughters in the line of succession.
As a result, Princess Charlotte retained her position ahead of her younger brother, Prince Louis, marking a significant modernization of succession rules.
For many royal watchers, however, legal details are less interesting than questions about how younger royals will adapt to increasing public attention.
The monarchy has faced considerable scrutiny in recent years, with debates surrounding its future, relevance, and relationship with the public becoming more frequent.
Supporters argue that continuity remains one of the institution's greatest strengths. Critics maintain that hereditary systems are difficult to reconcile with modern democratic values.
Regardless of those debates, George's future role remains largely defined by circumstances beyond his control. His position within the royal hierarchy was determined at birth and would only change through events affecting those ahead of him.
WHAT ELSE IS HAPPENING?
King attends charity awards ceremony
The King traveled to Manchester this week to present awards recognizing community volunteers from across the United Kingdom. During the ceremony, he praised individuals who had contributed to local initiatives supporting education, health services, and environmental projects.
Speaking to attendees, he emphasized the importance of civic engagement and thanked volunteers for their dedication to helping others.
Prince and Princess of Wales visit youth center
The Prince and Princess of Wales spent part of the week visiting a youth center in Birmingham focused on mentoring and career development programs.
The visit included discussions with students, teachers, and local organizers. Kensington Palace said the engagement formed part of the couple's ongoing interest in education and early intervention initiatives.
Royal documentary attracts attention
A new documentary examining the history of royal residences premiered this week on British television.
The program explores the development of several royal estates and includes interviews with historians, architects, and former palace staff. Early reviews have been mixed, though audience interest appears strong.
PHOTO OF THE WEEK
King Charles speaks with conservation volunteers during a visit to a coastal restoration project.
The King joined environmental groups working to restore natural habitats along part of Britain's coastline. Organizers said the project aims to improve biodiversity while protecting vulnerable ecosystems from erosion and climate-related challenges.
During the visit, Charles spoke with volunteers and researchers involved in the effort and reiterated his long-standing interest in environmental issues.
FROM THE ROYAL VAULT
This week marks the anniversary of the opening of Buckingham Palace to summer visitors in the 1990s.
The decision represented a significant change in how the royal family interacted with the public, allowing visitors access to parts of the palace previously seen only through official photographs and television broadcasts.
Over the years, the annual opening has become one of London's most popular attractions, offering visitors a glimpse into one of the country's most recognizable institutions.

Paris, France (CNN) — French President Emmanuel Macron said the world was at a "decisive turning point" on climate action and defended the ambitions of the Paris Agreement framework in an exclusive interview Wednesday with CNN's Becky Anderson, speaking on the margins of a major international environmental conference in the French capital.
Macron acknowledged the frustration felt by developing nations bearing the brunt of climate change while having contributed least to it. "We have to be honest — the countries suffering most are not the ones responsible for the bulk of emissions. That asymmetry is a moral problem, and it has to be at the center of everything we negotiate," he told CNN.
The French president hailed recent pledges on methane reduction and deforestation as meaningful steps, while cautioning that commitments on paper needed to translate into concrete policy. "Announcements are not action. We have made that mistake before. What matters now is the legislation, the financing, and the accountability mechanisms that follow."
Macron was also pressed on France's continued reliance on nuclear energy and whether it was compatible with a green transition. He pushed back firmly: "Nuclear gives us low-carbon baseload power. I will not apologize for that. The climate does not care about ideology — it cares about emissions." He added that France was simultaneously accelerating investment in offshore wind and solar infrastructure.
On the question of China's absence from key summit sessions, Macron urged patience while insisting that bilateral dialogue remained active. "We speak regularly with Beijing. The channels are open. But I will not pretend there is no gap between what the science demands and what some major emitters are currently willing to commit to."
Asked whether younger generations had reason for hope, Macron struck a carefully balanced tone. "Despair is not a strategy. Neither is blind optimism. What young people are right to demand is honesty — about how severe this is, and about whether those in power are genuinely acting. My job is to make sure the answer to that second question is yes."
He also addressed domestic criticism that his government's climate policies disproportionately burden working-class households, referencing the Yellow Vest protests that erupted in 2018 partly in response to a fuel tax rise. "The transition must be fair or it will not happen. We learned that lesson. Social justice and climate justice are not in opposition — they have to be built together."

(CNN) Former Leicester City Football Club youth academy player Marcus Osei has alleged in a new interview with Sky Sports that he was subjected to racial abuse by a member of the coaching staff when he was 17 years old.
Speaking to Sky Sports, Osei claims that a senior coach made derogatory comments referencing his Ghanaian heritage during a training session, following criticism of his defensive positioning.
"I was completely taken aback. No one had ever spoken to me like that in a professional environment. I didn't know how to react — I just went quiet," he said.
In a statement provided to CNN on Wednesday, Leicester City FC said: "We take all allegations of racism and discrimination extremely seriously. We were not previously aware of this specific allegation, and we are committed to conducting a thorough and transparent investigation."
CNN has reached out to Osei for further comment. He told Sky Sports he chose not to report the incident at the time.
"You think about saying something, but I was only 17 and still trying to prove myself. I didn't want to rock the boat or jeopardize my place at the club," he said.
"It made me feel like an outsider from that point on. I started doubting myself in team environments, wondering whether I truly belonged there," he added.
The allegations come amid growing scrutiny of racism within English football's academy system. Earlier this month, the club's director of youth development stepped down following separate complaints from multiple former players regarding the culture within the academy.
A parliamentary inquiry into racism across professional football academies is expected to begin hearings next month, with several clubs anticipated to be called to give evidence. The broader issue gained renewed attention following high-profile cases involving players at multiple Premier League clubs, prompting the Football Association to announce a review of its safeguarding procedures for youth players in October.

(CNN) When Momiji Nishiya stepped onto the podium at the Tokyo Olympics, she became the youngest athlete to win gold in the newly debuted women's street skateboarding event — just 13 years old, competing in front of a home crowd that wasn't allowed to be there. The stands were empty due to Covid-19 restrictions, but the energy among the skaters themselves more than filled the silence.
Nishiya's winning performance combined technical precision with a composure that belied her age, landing tricks that drew admiration even from her rivals. Finishing just behind her were fellow Japanese skater Funa Nakayama and Brazil's Rayssa Leal, who at 13 matched Nishiya's age and became an instant fan favorite worldwide after footage of her skating in a fairy costume went viral years earlier.
'We're all friends out here'
What struck many viewers watching the street skateboarding final was not just the skill on display, but the warmth between competitors. After each run, skaters gathered to cheer, hug, and celebrate one another regardless of scores or national affiliations. For Leal, who took silver, the atmosphere reflected something fundamental about skateboarding culture. "We grow up skating together, watching each other's videos," she told reporters after the event. "When your friend lands something amazing, you're happy for them. That's just how it is."
The podium, occupied entirely by teenagers, represented a generational shift in the sport — and a glimpse at what Olympic skateboarding could look like for years to come. All three medallists had been skating since they were toddlers, drawn in by YouTube videos and local skate parks before finding their way onto the world stage.
Finding the love early
Leal's story, in particular, captured global attention. Growing up in Vitória, Brazil, she began skating at age seven after watching videos online and begging her father for a board. With limited access to formal training facilities, she spent years skating in the streets, honing her style organically. That grassroots foundation, she says, is still the core of who she is as a skater. "I never want to lose the feeling of just skating for fun," she said. "That's where everything started."
Her silver medal at Tokyo marked only the beginning. Sponsors, magazine covers, and speaking invitations followed rapidly, but Leal has remained focused on the thing that set her on this path in the first place: the sheer joy of being on a board. She has spoken openly about wanting to use her visibility to encourage girls in Brazil and beyond to take up the sport, especially in communities where access to sports infrastructure is limited.
What comes next
With Paris 2024 firmly on her radar, Leal has been expanding her competitive repertoire while also nurturing her public profile. She has collaborated with major brands and appeared in campaigns aimed at getting more young girls into skateboarding, echoing a broader push from World Skate and the IOC to grow the sport's global footprint.
Still a teenager with the bulk of her career ahead of her, Leal speaks about the future with an ease that suggests she is in no hurry to rush toward it. "I just want to keep skating, keep learning, keep having fun," she said. "If I do that, I think everything else follows."

(CNN) Arsenal's hopes of ending their long wait for a Premier League title took a significant blow on Tuesday after a surprise home defeat to struggling Wolverhampton Wanderers. A second-half header from Matheus Cunha proved the difference, leaving Arsenal eight points behind league leaders Chelsea, who also have a game in hand.
The two sides are due to meet at Stamford Bridge on Saturday in what had been billed as a pivotal moment in the title race. "We have to be honest with ourselves — right now the gap is very big," Arsenal midfielder Thomas Partey told reporters after the final whistle.
Mikel Arteta's side had entered the match on the back of three consecutive victories and were expected to dominate a Wolves outfit fighting to avoid the drop. Instead they looked labored and disconnected in the final third, registering only two shots on target across ninety minutes.
The defeat extended a troubling run at the Emirates, where Arsenal have now won just once in their last five home league outings. Captain Martin Odegaard acknowledged the team had been well below their best. "We were not sharp enough, not aggressive enough. Wolves wanted it more than us tonight, and that showed," he said.
Arteta pointed to a relentless schedule and a growing injury list as contributing factors. Striker Bukayo Saka has been sidelined for three weeks, and the absence of his pace and creativity in the final third has been keenly felt. "We are managing a very thin squad across many competitions," Arteta said. "But that cannot be an excuse. We have to find solutions and the performance tonight was not acceptable."
Chelsea extend lead at the top
Chelsea's evening, meanwhile, could hardly have gone better. A commanding 3-1 victory over Aston Villa at Stamford Bridge extended their winning run to eleven matches in all competitions. Goals from Raheem Sterling, Cole Palmer, and a late Nicolas Jackson strike sealed the points and sent the home crowd into a jubilant mood.
Head coach Enzo Maresca praised the squad's collective intensity and noted a seventh clean sheet in eight league games had been narrowly denied only by a late consolation. "We are in a very good moment, but I never allow my players to feel comfortable," Maresca said after the match. "There are many games remaining and we know from experience how quickly things can change."
Sterling, who opened the scoring with a composed finish just before halftime, emphasized the squad's focus heading into the weekend. "Every game feels like a final at this stage of the season," he said. "We know Arsenal are coming and that it won't be easy, but we feel confident in the way we've been playing."

(CNN) Antonio Conte, manager of Italian club Inter Milan, says he is "deeply concerned" by the racist abuse directed at his striker Marcus Thuram during their Champions League group stage match against Dynamo Kyiv on Tuesday, which Inter won 2-0.
Thuram, the French international and son of World Cup winner Lilian Thuram, reported that he had been subjected to monkey chants from a section of the away support in the 58th minute, shortly after scoring Inter's opening goal. The 26-year-old visibly reacted to the abuse before teammates gathered around him, and the referee briefly paused the match.
UEFA's regulations require referees to follow a three-step protocol when racist incidents occur during matches — pausing play, suspending the game, and ultimately abandoning it if abuse continues. On Tuesday, play was paused once before resuming, but the match was not suspended or abandoned despite further reported incidents in the second half.
Conte said after the match that he had spoken with Thuram in the dressing room and described him as "shaken but strong."
"Marcus is a strong man and a strong player. But no one should have to experience that, not in football, not anywhere," Conte told reporters in his post-match press conference. "What happened tonight is not acceptable. UEFA has rules. I want to see those rules applied fully and without compromise."
The Italian manager also questioned whether football's governing bodies were doing enough to enforce their own standards. "We keep seeing these incidents. We keep hearing the same statements afterward. And then next week, another match, another incident," he said. "At some point you have to ask: are the punishments actually working? Because from where I'm standing, the answer is no."
Inter Milan confirmed in a club statement that they would be filing a formal complaint with UEFA and called for a full investigation. "Inter Milan condemns in the strongest possible terms any form of racist abuse. We stand fully behind Marcus and will pursue every avenue available to us to ensure that those responsible are identified and that UEFA acts decisively," the statement read.
Dynamo Kyiv released a brief response denying that their supporters were responsible for organized chanting and pledging to cooperate with any UEFA investigation.
UEFA confirmed it had opened disciplinary proceedings against Dynamo Kyiv and that the referee's report had formally documented the incidents. A spokesperson said the matter would be referred to UEFA's Control, Ethics and Disciplinary Body, with a hearing scheduled within two weeks.
Thuram, who has previously spoken publicly about racism in football and society, posted on social media after the match. Without naming the club or specific individuals, he wrote: "I play this game because I love it. Hate will never change that. But it has to stop."
His father, Lilian Thuram, who has long been an outspoken anti-racism campaigner, shared the post and added: "Proud of my son. This fight is not over."
CNN has reached out to both Dynamo Kyiv and UEFA for further comment.

A version of this story appeared in CNN's Meanwhile in China newsletter, a three-times-a-week update exploring what you need to know about the country's rise and how it impacts the world. Sign up here.
Shanghai (CNN) — How do you keep a city of 25 million people locked down indefinitely — and at what cost? That is the question confronting Chinese authorities as Shanghai's strict Covid-19 lockdown, now entering its fifth week, shows no sign of lifting, and frustration among residents reaches a boiling point rarely seen in public in mainland China.
Since late March, virtually the entire city has been confined to homes or residential compounds, with movement permitted only for mandatory PCR testing or medical emergencies. Food deliveries, coordinated through neighborhood committees, have been erratic, leaving some residents without adequate supplies for days at a time. Videos of people shouting from windows, banging pots, and arguing with hazmat-suited officials have circulated widely on Chinese social media before being censored — an unusual volume of dissent for a country with tight controls on public expression.
The lockdown was initially described by officials as a temporary, rolling measure targeting the city's two main districts in sequence. It has since expanded to cover all of Shanghai, with no clear end date communicated to residents.
Daily case numbers remain in the tens of thousands, the vast majority asymptomatic or mildly symptomatic. Despite this, China's zero-Covid policy — reaffirmed publicly by President Xi Jinping as recently as last month — mandates that all positive cases be transferred to centralized quarantine facilities, regardless of clinical condition. The result has been a severe strain on the city's infrastructure. Quarantine centers, hastily converted from exhibition halls, stadiums and schools, are reported to be overcrowded, with inadequate sanitation and insufficient food in some locations.
Shanghai's lockdown also carries significant consequences beyond its borders. The city is home to China's largest port and one of the world's busiest container shipping hubs. Weeks of disruption have contributed to growing backlogs and added pressure to global supply chains already fragmented by two years of pandemic disruption.
A diverging world
The contrast with other major economies is stark. Across much of Europe, North America and Southeast Asia, governments have moved to treat Covid as endemic — dropping mask mandates, reopening borders and scaling back testing requirements.
