In the United Kingdom, all legal Covid restrictions were lifted in February. In the United States, the Biden administration ended the public health emergency declaration in May.
Even within Asia, the divide is visible. South Korea lifted its outdoor mask mandate in April. Japan, long one of the most cautious countries in the region, has steadily reopened to international tourists. Thailand and Vietnam, having once pursued aggressive suppression strategies, have pivoted to living with the virus after vaccination rates climbed.
China remains the last major economy committed to zero-Covid, a position that has grown increasingly difficult to defend as the Omicron variant, far more transmissible than previous strains, renders elimination all but unachievable according to many epidemiologists. Experts outside China have largely concluded that the question is no longer whether to transition away from zero-Covid, but when and how.
For now, Chinese authorities show little sign of reconsidering. State media has continued to frame the lockdown as a necessary and patriotic sacrifice, while officials point to the comparatively low death toll in China relative to Western nations as evidence the policy has worked. But with economic growth forecasts being revised downward, factories idled, and residents increasingly vocal in their frustration, the pressure on Beijing is quietly mounting — even if it remains, for now, out of public view.
CNN's Beijing bureau contributed to this report.

(CNN) The 2023 college basketball season has delivered everything fans could have hoped for: packed arenas, deafening student sections, and the kind of rivalry matchups that remind you why March became its own cultural institution.
All of that will be on full display when two of the sport's historic powerhouses collide in the Big East Championship at Madison Square Garden in New York City — the self-proclaimed mecca of basketball. It's top-ranked Connecticut and third-ranked Marquette, and the stakes could hardly be higher.
The Husky Machine
UConn has long been one of college basketball's most decorated programs. The Huskies have won five national championships and have become synonymous with tournament excellence under coach Dan Hurley, who has steadily rebuilt the program into a genuine superpower since arriving in Storrs in 2018.
This season, UConn entered the year as the clear favorite after dismantling the field en route to a national title the previous spring. Their roster turned over significantly, losing four players to the NBA Draft, but Hurley simply reloaded. That has become something of a trademark.
The Huskies opened the year 7-0 before a tense road trip to Villanova nearly derailed things. Trailing by nine with under four minutes to play, freshman guard Solo Ball — who had struggled to find his footing early in the season — took over, scoring eight straight points and setting up the game-winning layup with eleven seconds left. UConn survived, 74-73.
They have not lost since. A 17-1 conference record heading into Championship weekend is as dominant a run as the Big East has seen in years, and Ball has gone from question mark to the frontrunner for Big East Player of the Year. His emergence has given UConn something even their previous title team lacked: a genuine go-to scorer in the closing minutes of tight games.
"He's grown up before our eyes," Hurley told reporters after UConn's regular season finale. "Early on, he was trying to do too much. Now he trusts the system, trusts his teammates, and when the moment gets big, he rises."
The Golden Eagles Circle
Marquette's path to this moment has been equally compelling, if a little more turbulent. Head coach Shaka Smart, now in his third season in Milwaukee, finally has the pieces to make a genuine title run — and his team knows it.
The Golden Eagles opened the year unranked but won eleven of their first thirteen games, climbing steadily into the top ten. Their calling card has been suffocating perimeter defense and a balanced offense that features five players averaging double figures — the first Marquette team to accomplish that in over two decades.
Their only real stumble came in late January, a two-game losing skid against Providence and Georgetown that briefly threatened to derail their conference title hopes. Smart responded by simplifying the offense and shifting to a smaller, quicker lineup — a gamble that paid off immediately. Marquette won eight straight to close the regular season, including an emphatic 81-64 road win over then-No. 6 Creighton.
Senior guard Kam Jones has been the engine of it all, averaging 22 points per game since the lineup change and drawing comparisons to previous Marquette greats. "We went through a tough stretch and it made us better," Jones said after the regular season ended. "We know who we are now. We stopped trying to be perfect and started playing together."
What's at stake?
For UConn, a Big East title would be the program's sixth in the Dan Hurley era and would all but cement their status as the No. 1 seed in the NCAA Tournament. More than that, a win would validate their claim to being the best team in the country — a claim a few voters still dispute despite their record.
For Marquette, the calculus is different. Smart is 0-4 against UConn since arriving at the program, and the Golden Eagles have not won the Big East Tournament since 2003. A win on Saturday would shatter both of those storylines at once, and with Selection Sunday one day away, it would send the program into the tournament on the kind of momentum that has historically translated to deep runs.
Two years ago, a different Marquette team reached the Elite Eight before bowing out to eventual champion Kansas. Smart and his players believe this group is better. Whether they're right depends on 40 minutes in the Garden.
"We've put ourselves in a position to compete for something real," Smart said this week. "Now we have to go do it. Nobody's going to hand us anything — especially not them."

(CNN) Should Saturday's NBA playoff game be broadcast on ESPN or framed and hung in the Smithsonian?
That's the question swirling around LeBron James and Kevin Durant ahead of their second-round clash this weekend — the first postseason series in NBA history to feature two opposing players each with more than 27,000 career regular-season points still actively competing at an elite level.
James, 40, posted a characteristically understated message on Instagram ahead of the series: a black-and-white photo of the two men shaking hands at midcourt, captioned simply "Legacy." Durant, 36, responded within the hour: "Let's give them something to remember."
The combined career statistics these two men have accumulated defy easy summary. Between them: eight NBA championships, six scoring titles, two Olympic gold medals apiece, and more All-NBA selections than any two players in the league's modern era. Both rank in the all-time top five in points scored. Historians of the sport have spent the better part of a decade arguing over which of them deserves to be called the most skilled offensive player the game has ever produced.
Older, not slower
They may not have the runway that younger stars like Jayson Tatum, Anthony Edwards, or Shai Gilgeous-Alexander still enjoy, but dismissing either man as a spent force would be a serious mistake.
James, completing his 22nd season after joining the Golden State Warriors on a one-year deal that raised eyebrows across the league last summer, has averaged 24 points, nine assists, and seven rebounds per game in the regular season — numbers that would be remarkable for a player a decade younger. Critics questioned whether he could adapt to a new system under head coach Steve Kerr. The answer has been emphatic.
The Warriors and Durant's Phoenix Suns met twice during the regular season. Phoenix won both, including a dominant 118-97 victory in February in which Durant scored 38 points and held James to 19 on nine-of-twenty-two shooting. James has not forgotten.
"That one stung," he admitted to reporters on Thursday. "But we're a different team now than we were in February. I'm a different player. We've had time to build something here and I think people are starting to see what that looks like."
Durant, meanwhile, is fresh off dismantling a young Oklahoma City Thunder team in the first round, averaging 31 points per game in a five-game series that was closer than the outcome suggested. Head coach Frank Vogel has had five seasons to build an offense around Durant's singular abilities, and the results have been quietly remarkable: three Western Conference Finals appearances and one championship in 2022.
"This is where you want to be," Durant told reporters after closing out the Thunder. "This matchup, this stage. I've been thinking about it since the bracket came out."
What's at stake?
For James, the calculus is straightforward. A deep playoff run — ideally a championship — with a third different franchise would cement an argument many already believe is settled: that he is the greatest to ever play the game. A title with Golden State, following rings with Cleveland and the Los Angeles Lakers, would be unprecedented.
For Durant, the stakes are no less significant. At 36, he is widely expected to announce his retirement at season's end regardless of outcome. A second championship, won against the player who has defined his era, would be the crowning note to a career that needs no further justification — but would certainly benefit from one.
The two men have faced each other in the playoffs three times previously, with Durant's teams holding a 2-1 series advantage. Each meeting has produced at least one moment that replayed for years afterward on highlight reels and in arguments between fans.
"I've known for a long time that this is what the end of my career would look like," Durant said on Thursday. "Competing at the highest level, against the best. That's all I've ever wanted. Sunday gives us that."

(CNN) It was marketed as an unbreakable fortress for sensitive corporate communications — a premium encrypted messaging platform promising total privacy from competitors, hackers, and governments alike. Instead, it became one of the most productive surveillance tools European law enforcement had ever deployed against organized crime.
Authorities in the Netherlands, France, and Belgium revealed on Monday that they had secretly infiltrated EncroChat, an encrypted communications network widely used by criminal networks across Europe, for nearly two years — harvesting tens of millions of messages and triggering one of the continent's largest coordinated law enforcement operations in recent memory.
The joint operation, codenamed "Operation Emma," was led by Europol and Eurojust in coordination with national police forces across more than a dozen countries. Officials said the infiltration of the network had produced over 115 million intercepted messages, leading to more than 1,500 arrests across Europe, the seizure of over 30 metric tons of narcotics, and the confiscation of more than 180 million euros in cash and assets.
Speaking at a press conference in The Hague on Monday, Europol Executive Director Catherine De Bolle described the operation as a landmark moment for cross-border law enforcement. "For the first time, we were able to read the real-time communications of criminal organizations at scale," she said. "These were not low-level offenders. These were the highest levels of organized crime — the planners, the financiers, the decision-makers."
EncroChat devices, which sold for several thousand euros each on the black market, were modified smartphones with the camera, microphone and GPS functions disabled. They could only communicate with other EncroChat devices and were marketed explicitly to users who required, in the words of the platform's own promotional material, "total operational security." The platform had an estimated 60,000 users worldwide at its peak, the majority of whom, authorities allege, were engaged in criminal activity.
The breakthrough came when French cyber-investigators managed to implant malware onto the EncroChat network's servers, located in France, allowing them to intercept messages before they were encrypted. The operation ran covertly for several months before EncroChat administrators detected the intrusion and shut the platform down in June 2020, sending a warning to users telling them to destroy their handsets immediately.
By then, authorities say, the damage — from the criminals' perspective — was done. The intercepted communications contained detailed discussions of drug shipments, weapons deals, contract killings, and money laundering operations. In several cases, law enforcement agencies were able to intervene before planned attacks took place, including the disruption of at least three alleged contract murders in the Netherlands alone.
Not all of the operation's legal foundations have gone uncontested. Defense lawyers in multiple countries have challenged the admissibility of EncroChat evidence in court, arguing that the mass interception of private communications violated the rights of defendants. Several cases in Germany and the United Kingdom are still working through the courts, with judges reaching differing conclusions on the legality of how the data was obtained and shared between jurisdictions.
Europol said Monday that further arrests were anticipated as investigators continued to work through the volume of material collected, and that related operations targeting successor networks were already underway.
"Criminal networks will always look for new tools," De Bolle said. "But this operation has shown that no platform is beyond reach."

(CNN) A prominent Premier League manager and members of his coaching staff have been targeted with threatening messages on social media following a contentious tactical decision that contributed to his club's defeat in a high-profile match over the weekend.
Brentford manager Thomas Frank confirmed on Monday that he and several of his backroom staff had received abusive messages, including threats of physical harm, after his decision to substitute a key forward in the second half was widely blamed by supporters for costing the team a crucial victory. Frank said he had reported the messages to the police and had been in contact with the Premier League.
"It is deeply upsetting, not just for me but for my family and for members of my staff who have nothing to do with decisions made on the pitch," Frank said in a statement released through the club. "I have been in football long enough to know that fans are passionate and that results matter. But there is a line, and threats of violence are far beyond it."
The Premier League condemned the abuse in firm terms. "Threats of violence and intimidatory behavior directed at any manager, player, official, or member of staff are completely unacceptable," said a spokesperson. "We fully support Thomas and his staff in reporting this matter to police and will cooperate fully with any investigation."
The Professional Footballers' Association and the League Managers Association issued a joint statement expressing solidarity with Frank and calling for urgent action from social media companies. "This is not an isolated incident. Managers, players and officials are routinely subjected to abuse that in any other professional context would be treated as a serious criminal matter," the statement read. "Social media platforms must do more — proactively and immediately — to identify and remove threatening content and share information with law enforcement."
The Metropolitan Police confirmed they had received a report and that enquiries were ongoing. "We take all reports of malicious communications seriously," a spokesperson said. "Threatening behavior online is not consequence-free, and those found to have committed offenses will be pursued."
Frank is expected to take charge of Brentford's next fixture on Thursday, though the club said it was in ongoing discussions with the Premier League about security arrangements. He briefly addressed reporters ahead of the match, striking a composed but clearly frustrated tone.
"I love this game. I love managing in this league," he said. "But it has to be safe for everyone involved — not just the players on the pitch, but the people on the touchline, in the offices, and at home."
Several current Premier League players took to social media to express support for Frank, with a number calling on platforms to take greater responsibility for content posted on their sites. Britain's Sports Minister also weighed in, saying the government's forthcoming Online Safety legislation would place new legal obligations on platforms to act swiftly against criminal abuse.

Brussels, Belgium
Flemish nationalist parties secured enough seats on Sunday in Belgium's regional parliament to extend their majority, though a resilient performance by Flemish social democrats suggested that coalition negotiations in Brussels could prove long and complicated.
With over 98% of ballots counted, the combined nationalist bloc took 52.3% of the vote, their strongest showing in a decade. The most probable outcome was a continuation of the existing center-right Flemish coalition, though smaller parties held significant leverage over the final shape of any government.
The result is unlikely to trigger any immediate push for formal secession from Belgium. While tensions between Dutch-speaking Flanders and French-speaking Wallonia remain a persistent feature of Belgian political life, most voters on Sunday cited economic anxieties and the rising cost of living as their primary concerns — not the constitutional question of the country's future.
Turnout was 68%, down from 74% at the previous regional election, a dip that analysts said may have modestly benefited nationalist parties whose core supporters tend to vote at higher rates in lower-participation elections.
The centre-right New Flemish Alliance, known by its Flemish initials N-VA, remained the dominant force in the region, winning an estimated 29 seats in the 124-seat Flemish Parliament. Party leader Bart De Wever declared the result a mandate for greater autonomy and renewed fiscal transfers between the regions.
"Flanders has spoken clearly," De Wever told supporters at the party's election night gathering in Ghent. "We want control over our own future — our own budget, our own social policy, our own choices. That conversation with Brussels cannot be delayed any longer."
The far-right Vlaams Belang, which has increasingly competed with N-VA for the Flemish nationalist vote in recent cycles, won an estimated 26 seats, a slight decline from its previous high-water mark. The party's leader, Tom Van Grieken, nonetheless framed the result as a continued rejection of what he called Belgium's "dysfunctional federal model."
The Flemish social democrats, Vooruit, delivered the evening's most notable surprise, winning 21 seats — a gain of four — and positioning themselves as a potentially decisive partner in any coalition talks. Party leader Melissa Depraeter said her strong showing showed that Flemish voters wanted economic reform, not constitutional upheaval.
"People are worried about their energy bills, their wages, their children's futures," Depraeter told reporters as results came in. "They did not vote tonight for an abstract debate about borders. They voted for change."
For Belgian Prime Minister Georges-Louis Bouchez, whose MR party governs at the federal level in an awkward coalition that spans the linguistic divide, the result offered mixed signals. N-VA's continued dominance deepens pressure on the federal government to accelerate talks on a new state reform, a process that has stalled repeatedly in the current legislative term.
Constitutional experts noted that the path to any formal partition of Belgium — a scenario that resurfaces in political discussion every few years — remains extraordinarily complicated, requiring approval across multiple parliaments and a level of cross-community consensus that does not currently exist.
For now, the most immediate question is a simpler one: who will govern Flanders, and on what terms.

(CNN) After years of anticipation and months of mounting uncertainty, the Paris 2024 Summer Olympics are set to open in spectacular fashion along the banks of the Seine — but the road to Friday's ceremony has been anything but smooth.
Competitive events began Tuesday with football and rugby sevens matches held across multiple cities, as is customary before the formal opening. But overshadowing the early action has been a wave of security concerns, logistical strain, and persistent questions about whether one of the world's most densely populated capitals can absorb the demands of hosting the planet's largest sporting event.
French authorities have deployed approximately 45,000 police officers and 10,000 military personnel across Paris in what officials are calling the most extensive security operation in the country's history. The unprecedented scale of Friday's opening ceremony — which will take place outdoors along a six-kilometer stretch of the Seine River rather than inside a stadium — has drawn particular scrutiny, with an estimated 300,000 ticket holders and up to 222 delegations of athletes expected to participate in the floating parade.
"The scale of what we are attempting has no precedent in Olympic history," Paris 2024 chief organizer Tony Estanguet acknowledged at a press conference on Monday. "There are risks. We are managing them seriously and with every resource available to us."
Those reassurances have not fully calmed nerves. Just days before the ceremony, French authorities arrested several individuals in connection with an alleged plot to disrupt the Games, prompting an emergency security review. The French interior ministry confirmed the arrests but declined to provide further details, citing the ongoing nature of the investigation.
Transport networks have also come under pressure. Paris's metro system, already operating near capacity during peak summer tourism season, has struggled to absorb the additional load of arriving athletes, journalists, and spectators. Long queues at Charles de Gaulle airport and widespread delays on regional train lines prompted complaints from delegations arriving over the weekend.
The athletes' village, located in Saint-Denis just north of the city, has itself become a talking point. Several national Olympic committees flagged concerns about the absence of air conditioning in residential units — an unusual omission for a summer Games — with organizers defending the decision on environmental grounds and pointing to distributed cooling units as an alternative. The United States, Britain, Canada, and several other delegations announced they had installed their own cooling systems at their own expense.
American swimmer Caeleb Dressel, who is bidding to add to his five gold medals from Tokyo, was diplomatic when asked. "It's warm," he said with a smile. "We've dealt with it."
Los Angeles looks ahead
With Paris finally underway, attention in Olympic circles has already begun to shift toward 2028, when the Games move to Los Angeles for the third time. The LA28 organizing committee is expected to present a progress report to the International Olympic Committee later this week, with officials describing the city's preparations as on schedule.
Los Angeles previously hosted the Games in 1932 and 1984. The 1984 edition, held during a Soviet-led boycott, is widely remembered as a financial and organizational success that helped rescue the Olympic movement from a period of crisis.
LA28 chairman Casey Wasserman sought to frame the 2028 Games as an opportunity to build on Paris's ambitions. "Every Games teaches the next host something new," he told reporters on the sidelines of a pre-opening reception. "We are watching, learning, and preparing."
The 2032 Summer Olympics are scheduled for Brisbane, Australia, which was confirmed as host city in 2021. It will be the third time Australia has hosted the Summer Games, following Melbourne in 1956 and Sydney in 2000.

(CNN) Los Angeles Dodgers outfielder Cody Bellinger said he was grateful to have walked away unharmed after a serious car accident on a highway outside Phoenix, Arizona, on Saturday, just days before the start of the team's spring training camp in Glendale.
Bellinger told reporters Sunday that he was driving to the Dodgers' training facility when a vehicle travelling at high speed crossed into his lane on Interstate 10 and sideswiped his SUV, sending it into the guardrail. Bellinger said his vehicle sustained significant damage to the driver's side and that he was briefly shaken before realizing he had escaped serious injury.
"It happened so fast," Bellinger said. "One second everything is normal, and then you're just trying to figure out what just happened. I'm really lucky."
The 2019 National League MVP said the incident had given him a fresh sense of perspective heading into the new season. "You get so caught up in the grind, in the preparation, that you forget to step back and appreciate that you're healthy and you're here," he said. "Yesterday reminded me of that pretty quickly."
Bellinger's agent confirmed that the outfielder had been examined by team medical staff on Sunday morning and was cleared of any injuries. He was present and took part in light workouts at the Dodgers' complex on Sunday afternoon, according to a team spokesperson.
The Arizona Department of Public Safety confirmed the incident and said the other driver had been cited for unsafe lane change. No other vehicles were involved and no injuries were reported.
Bellinger, who is entering his third season with the Dodgers after signing a multi-year contract extension last winter, is expected to be in the starting lineup when the team opens their Cactus League schedule next week.
"I called my family right after," Bellinger said. "That was the first thing I did. Just wanted them to know I was OK. Honestly, I'm just happy to be here talking to you guys."

(CNN) A sweeping seven-year investigation into corruption and financial misconduct across international athletics concluded Wednesday, as the US Department of Justice announced it would return $165 million to the World Athletics governing body, the International Association of Athletics Federations, and several national athletics federations identified as victims of a systemic doping-cover-up bribery scheme.
The DOJ determined that officials within multiple organizations had accepted payments from athletes and their coaches in exchange for suppressing positive doping test results, allowing competitors to continue competing at the highest levels of the sport — including at the Olympic Games — while concealing banned substance use from anti-doping authorities.
"This was a profound betrayal of clean athletes everywhere," said World Athletics President Sebastian Coe. "Money that was stolen from the integrity of our sport will now be returned to programs that protect it. This is a significant day, but it is also a reminder of how much damage was done and how much work remains."
The investigation, which began in 2016 following a series of whistleblower disclosures and an explosive report by an independent commission established by the World Anti-Doping Agency, resulted in charges against 38 individuals and four corporate entities across 15 countries. Among those charged were former senior officials of the Russian Athletics Federation, intermediaries operating in multiple jurisdictions, and representatives of several sports management agencies.
Charges included wire fraud, money laundering, obstruction of justice, and conspiracy to defraud anti-doping bodies. Several individuals have already pleaded guilty, while others are still awaiting trial. The DOJ said the investigation remained ongoing.
The recovered funds will be channeled into a newly established Clean Sport Development Fund, to be jointly administered by World Athletics and an independent oversight board. According to the DOJ, the fund will support anti-doping education initiatives, athlete welfare programs, and the development of athletics infrastructure in lower-income countries where governance oversight has historically been weakest.
"We are committed to making sure this money reaches the athletes and communities it was always meant to serve," Coe said.
The case drew comparisons to the 2015 FIFA corruption scandal, in which US federal prosecutors similarly used wire fraud and money laundering statutes to pursue misconduct in an international sports organization, given that much of the relevant financial activity passed through American banks.
CNN's Washington bureau contributed to this report.

Rome (CNN) Pope Francis will not travel to Lisbon next week for the closing ceremony of World Youth Day, according to a Vatican spokesperson. The announcement follows a period of reduced public engagements for the 86-year-old pontiff, who has been managing ongoing knee and hip problems that have increasingly limited his mobility in recent months.
The Vatican had confirmed earlier on Monday that the Pope would "reluctantly" withdraw from the planned in-person appearance at the Parque Eduardo VII gathering, which is expected to draw hundreds of thousands of young Catholics from around the world. He would instead deliver a blessing and address via video link from the Apostolic Palace.
"The Holy Father deeply regrets that he is unable to be present in Lisbon for this historic occasion but looks forward to being with the faithful in spirit and through his message," the statement read.
Earlier this month, Francis had used particularly blunt language about the church's responsibility to young people during a general audience in St. Peter's Square, drawing applause from pilgrims when he said institutional "timidity" in the face of social injustice was "simply not acceptable."
A senior Vatican official confirmed to CNN that the decision to withdraw from Lisbon was made purely on medical advice and was unrelated to any acute health event. The Pope had been seen using a wheelchair at several recent public engagements, a visible sign of the mobility difficulties he has experienced since undergoing colon surgery in 2021.
Despite the travel cancellation, Francis has continued to carry out a full schedule of meetings and audiences at the Vatican. CNN's review of his public calendar shows he conducted more than fourteen formal engagements in the first three weeks of July alone, including audiences with several heads of state and a lengthy address to a symposium on environmental theology.
He declined to reduce that pace last week, presiding over his regular Wednesday general audience and meeting privately with a delegation of Ukrainian Catholic bishops, despite calls from some within the Curia for a more cautious approach to his schedule.
Last month, the Pope dismissed suggestions he was considering retirement, saying he had "no intention" of following the path taken by his predecessor Benedict XVI. "When the Lord wants me to stop, he will let me know," he told a group of Jesuit journalists in a wide-ranging interview.
Still, the decision not to make the journey to Lisbon — a trip he had publicly expressed enthusiasm for as recently as last month — is the latest in a series of adjustments to his public role. Long-haul foreign travel has become increasingly rare, with shorter regional trips and domestic engagements now making up the bulk of his schedule outside of the Vatican.
CNN has launched Vatican Dispatch, a new weekly newsletter covering the Holy See, the papacy, and the global Catholic Church. Sign up here.

(CNN) "There are days when it still doesn't feel real," Dwyane Wade said in a quiet moment after a charity event in Miami last weekend, marking the one-year anniversary of the death of his closest friend in basketball.
One year after the passing of David Stern — the NBA commissioner who transformed the league into a global phenomenon — those whose careers he shaped most directly are still processing a loss that cut deeper than professional admiration. For Wade, and for many others across the league, honoring Stern's legacy has become part of how they carry him forward.
Stern died on January 1, 2020, following a sudden brain hemorrhage. He was 77. Over 30 years as commissioner, he oversaw the NBA's expansion from a domestically struggling league into one of the most recognized sports properties in the world, building platforms for the very players who now mourn him.
"He gave so many of us a stage," Wade said. "Not just to play, but to be who we are. To have opinions, to have a voice. That wasn't always the case and he changed it."
LeBron James, who came of age in the league Stern built and clashed with the commissioner on more than one occasion, spoke about him with unmistakable warmth. "We didn't always see eye to eye and I think he respected that, and I respected him for it," James said. "He was tough, he was honest, and he cared about this league more than anybody."
'He saw the whole board'
Current NBA Commissioner Adam Silver, who served as Stern's deputy for years before succeeding him in 2014, said the past year had only deepened his appreciation for what his predecessor built — and for how much of what the league takes for granted today traces back to decisions Stern made decades ago.
"David had this ability to see around corners," Silver said at a memorial event held in New York last week. "He saw the global game before anyone else did. He saw the business of basketball becoming something that could rival any entertainment property in the world. And then he went and built it."
Silver said he still finds himself reaching for the phone to call Stern when a major decision confronts the league — a reflex that hasn't faded. "I'll probably never fully stop doing that," he admitted.
Among the players who knew Stern personally, the tributes have been consistent in one theme: he treated them as partners, even when the relationship was combative. Chris Paul, president of the National Basketball Players Association, said Stern's willingness to engage directly with players — sometimes fiercely — ultimately made the league stronger.
"He pushed back. Hard, sometimes. But he also listened," Paul said. "That's rarer than people think in this business."
The NBA held a moment of silence before games on New Year's Day to mark the anniversary. Several teams wore commemorative patches on their jerseys, and a tribute video produced by the league's entertainment division aired during halftime of the nationally televised game.
For Silver, the anniversary was a moment to reflect not just on what Stern achieved, but on the standard he set. "Every decision I make, I ask myself whether it moves the game forward," Silver said. "That's what he taught me. That's what he was."

(CNN) The Las Vegas Raiders could become the first NFL team to welcome a full stadium crowd since the pandemic emptied arenas across the country more than a year ago, after Nevada officials signaled they would allow Allegiant Stadium to operate at full capacity beginning with the team's preseason home opener.
The franchise announced Thursday that Allegiant Stadium would be open to its 65,000-seat capacity for the Raiders' first preseason game against the San Francisco 49ers on August 14, pending final confirmation from the Nevada Department of Health. Raiders President Marc Badain said the organization was "thrilled" at the prospect.
"We built one of the most spectacular stadiums in professional sports in large part to give our fans an experience unlike any other," Badain said in a statement. "To finally be able to welcome all of them through the doors is something we have been working toward since the day this building opened."
The announcement coincided with Nevada Governor Steve Sisolak's decision to lift most remaining capacity restrictions on large indoor venues across the state, citing a sustained decline in Covid-19 hospitalizations and an accelerating vaccination rollout. As of Thursday, approximately 39% of Nevada's adult population had been fully vaccinated, according to state health department figures.
Some health officials expressed caution. Dr. Fermin Leguen of the Southern Nevada Health District acknowledged the improving picture but noted that full indoor stadiums presented a different challenge than outdoor venues. "We are encouraged by the trends," he said at a briefing Thursday. "We also ask that residents continue to exercise personal responsibility."
Fans attending the game will still be encouraged, though not required, to wear masks inside the stadium once Nevada's indoor mask mandate expires on June 1. The team said it would maintain enhanced cleaning protocols and increase ventilation throughout the building.
The Raiders' situation is complicated by the franchise's unusual circumstances. Allegiant Stadium, a $1.9 billion domed facility, opened in July 2020 — just as the pandemic was forcing sports leagues to play without spectators. The Raiders played their entire inaugural season in Las Vegas without a single fan in the building, a deeply frustrating experience for a franchise that had relocated from Oakland specifically to build a new identity and fanbase in the Nevada desert.
A limited number of fans were permitted to attend selected home games during the 2020 season at other NFL venues, but the Raiders were among the teams that played before empty seats for the entire year.
The NFL is expected to confirm full stadium attendance for all 32 teams when the 2021 regular season begins in September, though individual state and local regulations may affect some franchises.
Las Vegas Mayor Carolyn Goodman welcomed the announcement, calling it a significant step in the city's economic recovery. "This city runs on hospitality, on spectacle, on bringing people together," she said. "Having a full house at Allegiant Stadium is exactly the signal Las Vegas needs to send to the world right now."
Not all voices were in agreement. Several epidemiologists cautioned that the Delta variant, which had begun emerging in other parts of the world, posed a potential threat to the reopening timeline. "We are in a better place than we were six months ago," said Dr. Brian Labus of the University of Nevada Las Vegas School of Public Health. "That does not mean the pandemic is over."
As of Thursday, 42,000 tickets for the preseason opener had already been sold. Team officials said the remaining inventory would go on sale by the end of the week.

A crowdfunding platform that promotes itself as a champion of free expression has become a fundraising hub for a range of controversial political figures and activist groups, drawing criticism from watchdog organizations and renewed scrutiny from payment providers.
An examination of campaigns hosted on the platform found dozens of fundraisers connected to politically charged causes, including legal defense efforts, travel expenses for demonstrations, and investigations into disputed election claims. Several campaigns have collectively raised hundreds of thousands of dollars from supporters across the country.
Among the beneficiaries are activists facing criminal charges, organizers of large-scale political rallies, and media personalities who claim they have been unfairly targeted by mainstream institutions. Supporters argue that these individuals deserve access to financial assistance regardless of their political views, while critics contend that such fundraising can strengthen extremist networks and enable harmful activities.
The platform's leadership maintains that its role is not to judge the popularity of a cause but to determine whether it complies with the law and the company's policies. According to company executives, campaigns are generally permitted as long as they do not explicitly advocate violence, discrimination, or illegal conduct.
"We believe people should have the opportunity to support causes they care about," one executive said in a recent interview. "Our responsibility is to provide a service, not to act as an ideological gatekeeper."
That position has sparked debate among advocacy groups that monitor online extremism. Some researchers argue that fundraising infrastructure is a critical component of political movements, particularly those operating on the fringes of public discourse. Restricting access to financial resources, they say, can reduce the reach and influence of organizations associated with violence or hate.
Others view the issue through the lens of free expression. Legal scholars note that raising money for legal representation, public advocacy, or political organizing is often protected under constitutional principles, even when the underlying cause is controversial.
The company has also faced pressure from financial partners. Several payment processors have reportedly suspended services for certain campaigns or declined to handle transactions associated with higher-risk activities. In some cases, fundraising pages remain visible online even though donations can no longer be accepted.
Despite the controversy, the platform continues to grow. Originally established to support charitable and community-based projects, it now hosts campaigns ranging from medical expenses and disaster relief to political activism and legal defense funds. Company leaders say increasing traffic and donations have significantly expanded the platform's reach in recent years.
The debate surrounding the site reflects a broader question facing the technology industry: should digital platforms remain neutral hosts for lawful activity, or should they take a more active role in determining which causes deserve access to their services? As political polarization continues to shape public life, that question is unlikely to disappear anytime soon.

(CNN) Another familiar face is making the move into daytime television.
NBCUniversal announced Tuesday that Emmy-winning actress and producer Viola Davis will host a new daytime talk show set to air across several major network affiliates next year.
"I've been fortunate to experience so many different chapters in my life and career," Davis said in a statement. "Each one has taught me something valuable about resilience, purpose, and the importance of human connection. I look forward to creating a space where people can share their stories and where we can learn from one another."
Davis added that she hopes the program will highlight meaningful conversations and celebrate people from all walks of life. "I've always been fascinated by what drives people, what challenges them, and what inspires them to keep moving forward. This show is an opportunity to explore those stories and connect with audiences in a new way."
Davis first gained widespread recognition for her acclaimed stage performances before establishing herself as one of Hollywood's most respected actors. She earned an Academy Award for her role in the 2016 film "Fences" and has also received multiple Emmy and Tony Awards during her career.
Read More
Throughout her professional journey, Davis has often spoken publicly about overcoming poverty and adversity during her childhood. More recently, she appeared in the historical action film "The Woman King," which received praise from both critics and audiences.
The hour-long talk show is expected to premiere in the spring.

(CNN) The college football postseason field is officially set, with the selection committee announcing the four teams that will compete for the national championship on Sunday.
The No. 1-ranked Texas Longhorns (12-1), No. 2 Oregon Ducks (12-1), No. 3 Florida State Seminoles (13-0) and No. 4 Washington Huskies (12-1) secured spots in this year's College Football Playoff.
The semifinal matchups, scheduled for New Year's Day, will feature top-seeded Texas facing fourth-seeded Washington in the Sugar Bowl at Caesars Superdome in New Orleans, Louisiana. Meanwhile, Oregon will take on Florida State in the Rose Bowl at the Rose Bowl Stadium in Pasadena, California.
The winners of those contests will advance to the national championship game, which is set to be played later in January at NRG Stadium in Houston, Texas.
Florida State's inclusion marks a significant achievement for the program, which completed an undefeated regular season and captured its conference championship. The Seminoles relied on a balanced roster and strong defensive performances throughout the year to remain among the nation's elite teams.
Texas earned the top seed after winning its conference title game and recording several victories over ranked opponents. Under head coach Steve Sarkisian, the Longhorns have returned to national prominence following several seasons outside the championship conversation.
Oregon enters the playoff after a dominant campaign that featured one of the country's highest-scoring offenses. The Ducks secured key victories during conference play and finished the season with momentum following a convincing championship-game performance.
Washington claimed the final playoff position after compiling a strong résumé highlighted by multiple wins against ranked competition. The Huskies' passing attack played a central role in their success throughout the season.
Just outside the playoff field, the fifth-ranked Penn State Nittany Lions (11-2) narrowly missed selection despite another successful year. The team is expected to receive an invitation to a major bowl game and finish the season against a highly ranked opponent.
The sixth-ranked LSU Tigers (10-3) also remained in contention until the final rankings were released. LSU's explosive offense helped keep the Tigers in the national discussion throughout much of the season.
With the playoff bracket now finalized, attention turns to the semifinal games, where four of the country's top programs will compete for the opportunity to play for a national championship.

At the Presidents Cup on Saturday, veteran golfer Justin Thomas added another milestone to his already impressive international career, becoming one of the most successful American players in team competition history.
The 32-year-old secured his 18th match victory in the event after teaming up with Patrick Cantlay to defeat the International Team pairing of Sungjae Im and Corey Conners in the morning foursomes session. The American duo rallied after a difficult start, overturning a two-hole deficit midway through the round to earn a 2&1 victory.
It marked their second consecutive win together during the tournament, having also claimed a point in Friday's team matches. Thomas has become known for his strong performances in team events and has consistently delivered points for the United States throughout his appearances.
Since making his debut several years ago, Thomas has established himself as one of the team's most reliable competitors. Entering this year's competition, he was already among the leading active American players in total points earned.
The latest victory moved him further up the all-time standings and reinforced his reputation as a player who thrives under pressure. His partnership with Cantlay has been particularly effective, with the pair demonstrating strong chemistry throughout the week.
Despite reaching another personal milestone, Thomas focused on the overall contest rather than his individual achievement. Speaking after the round, he emphasized that the team's position in the competition remained the priority.
"It's nice, of course, but we're here to win as a team," Thomas told reporters. "Personal records are something you can appreciate later. Right now, we're trying to keep momentum on our side and continue putting points on the board."
He added that the International Team had been highly competitive and that the United States could not afford to become complacent.
"There are still a lot of matches left," he said. "We've played some good golf, but every session matters. We need to stay focused and keep fighting for every point."

(CNN) The death of a woman whose pregnancy was complicated by severe fetal abnormalities has intensified debate over abortion laws in a European country where access to the procedure has become increasingly restricted.
Thousands of demonstrators gathered in major cities over the weekend after activists linked the woman's death to legislation that sharply limits the circumstances under which pregnancies can be terminated. Protesters argued that legal uncertainty surrounding abortion has made some physicians reluctant to intervene even when a patient's health is deteriorating.
The woman, who was in the second trimester of pregnancy, was admitted to a regional hospital after experiencing serious medical complications, according to statements from her family and legal representatives. Prenatal examinations had reportedly identified significant abnormalities in the fetus.
Family members said doctors chose to continue monitoring the pregnancy rather than perform an immediate termination. As the woman's condition worsened, she developed a severe infection and later died despite medical efforts to save her.
Her death has become a focal point for abortion-rights advocates, who say restrictive laws have created confusion among healthcare providers about when intervention is legally permitted. Demonstrators carried signs and candles during rallies held in several cities, calling for reforms that would provide doctors with greater discretion in emergency situations.
Lawyers representing the family have requested a formal investigation into the medical decisions made during the woman's treatment. They argue that both individual clinical judgments and the broader legal environment should be examined.
Hospital officials expressed condolences to the family and said all actions taken by medical staff were consistent with existing regulations and professional standards. Authorities have launched a review to determine whether any procedural errors occurred.
The case quickly gained national attention after details were shared on social media. A slogan translating to "Never Again" spread widely online and became a rallying cry for demonstrators demanding changes to the country's abortion policies.
Government leaders have rejected suggestions that abortion restrictions directly caused the woman's death. Officials maintain that current laws allow physicians to act when a patient's life or health is in danger and argue that any failures were related to medical decision-making rather than legislation itself.
Supporters of the restrictions contend that the laws are designed to protect unborn children and should not be blamed before investigations are complete. Several lawmakers urged the public to wait for official findings before drawing conclusions about responsibility.
Medical and legal experts remain divided. Some argue that physicians may become overly cautious when legal penalties are unclear, while others insist that existing exceptions provide sufficient flexibility for doctors to respond to emergencies.
The case has drawn comparisons to incidents in other countries where the deaths of pregnant women sparked national conversations about reproductive rights and ultimately influenced legislative change.
For activists seeking reform, the woman's death represents what they describe as the human cost of restrictive policies. They hope the public attention generated by the case will lead lawmakers to revisit the country's abortion laws and clarify the circumstances under which medical intervention can occur.

Six years after his most recent major triumph, Rory McIlroy has put himself back in contention for another memorable victory. The four-time major champion surged into the lead on Friday during the second round of the US Open, finishing with a three-shot advantage over the rest of the field.
The Northern Irishman looked in complete control for much of the day. He collected five birdies across his first 13 holes and appeared on course for an even lower score before a pair of late bogeys reduced his advantage. Nevertheless, he ended the round at 6-under par, putting himself in a strong position heading into the weekend.
Despite the disappointing finish, McIlroy remained upbeat afterward and said he was pleased with the overall quality of his play. During his post-round interview, he was informed that several rivals had dropped shots on the closing holes, leaving him alone at the top of the leaderboard.
"That's nice to hear, but there are still two long days ahead," McIlroy said. "I've put myself in a good position, but nothing has been won yet. The course is demanding, and there are a lot of great players chasing."
If McIlroy can maintain his lead through Sunday, it would mark his first major championship victory in more than a decade. Since lifting the PGA Championship trophy in 2014, he has come close on several occasions but has been unable to add to his major tally.
After recent tournaments in which inconsistency affected his results, McIlroy credited his improved performance to better mental preparation. He explained that he has spent considerable time working on concentration and staying committed to every shot.
"I've been focusing on keeping my attention in the present moment," he said. "It's easy to think ahead in a tournament, but I've tried to stay disciplined and approach each shot with the same level of commitment."
He added that mental training has become increasingly important as his career has progressed.
"You have to keep developing every part of your game," McIlroy said. "For me, that includes focus, patience, and trusting what I see. Those things can make a huge difference over four rounds."

(CNN) A professional basketball coach has been dismissed by his club after making controversial remarks during a post-game interview that drew widespread criticism from fans, advocacy groups and league officials.
The comments were made following his team's narrow loss in a domestic league match on Saturday. Frustrated by what he believed were unfavorable officiating decisions, the coach used an analogy involving domestic violence while discussing the number of fouls awarded to his team.
"We seem to have to go through something extreme before we get a call," the coach said during a television interview. "At this point, it feels like one of our players would have to be assaulted before the referees notice anything."
The remarks quickly sparked backlash on social media, with critics accusing the coach of trivializing a serious issue. Several supporters called on the club to take disciplinary action, while advocacy organizations condemned the language as inappropriate and insensitive.
Later that evening, the club released a statement distancing itself from the comments.
"The views expressed by our head coach do not reflect the values of this organization," the statement said. "We consider the remarks unacceptable and sincerely apologize to anyone offended. The matter is being reviewed internally."
Less than 24 hours later, the team announced that the coach's contract had been terminated with immediate effect.
"Following a review of recent events, the club has decided to part ways with the head coach," the organization said in a statement. "An interim coaching arrangement will be announced in the coming days."
The coach, who enjoyed a lengthy playing career before moving into management, has worked for clubs in several countries and previously led teams to domestic titles. He joined the club before the start of the current season with the aim of rebuilding a struggling roster.
Despite expectations of improvement, results have been disappointing. The team currently sits near the bottom of the league standings after winning only a handful of games through the first half of the campaign.
League officials declined to comment on the dismissal but reiterated their commitment to maintaining professional standards among players, coaches and club personnel.
The incident has renewed discussion about accountability in sports and the consequences public figures may face for comments made during emotionally charged post-match interviews.

(CNN) This year's film awards season has underscored a transformation that has been building for years: streaming platforms are no longer simply competing with traditional movie studios—they are increasingly defining what success looks like in the industry.
Several of the most celebrated films of the year have come not from theatrical releases but from streaming services, whose growing influence has forced award organizations to reconsider how they evaluate cultural impact. As audiences continue shifting their viewing habits toward at-home entertainment, conventional measures of popularity have become less reliable indicators of a film's reach.
For decades, box-office revenue served as the clearest benchmark for determining whether a movie resonated with the public. Today, that metric tells only part of the story. Millions of viewers can watch a film through a subscription service without generating the kind of ticket-sales figures that once dominated industry discussions.
The challenge is that streaming platforms rarely provide detailed audience data comparable to box-office reporting. Nevertheless, certain films have clearly broken through the crowded entertainment landscape, generating extensive online discussion, critical analysis and social-media engagement.
This reality has become increasingly important for award ceremonies seeking to maintain relevance. Recognition often carries greater weight when audiences are familiar with the nominated works. In the streaming era, visibility may come less from theatrical attendance and more from widespread digital consumption.
Traditional media coverage has at times struggled to adapt to these changing dynamics. Discussions about declining movie-ticket sales frequently overlook the possibility that viewers have not abandoned films altogether but have instead changed where and how they watch them.
The shift has also complicated the distinction between prestige cinema and mainstream entertainment. Streaming companies have invested heavily in attracting acclaimed directors, high-profile actors and ambitious projects that can compete for major awards while simultaneously reaching large global audiences.
At the same time, theatrical blockbusters continue to occupy a unique place in the industry. Large-scale franchise films remain among the few releases capable of generating truly massive box-office numbers and shared cultural moments. Their success has prompted recurring debates about whether award organizations should place greater emphasis on films with broad commercial appeal.
In response, some institutions have expanded nominee fields or revised eligibility rules in hopes of creating a balance between artistic recognition and audience engagement. Whether those efforts have succeeded remains a matter of debate.
What seems increasingly clear is that streaming services now occupy a central position in the awards conversation. Films that generate substantial attention online and attract large home-viewing audiences are becoming difficult for voters to ignore, regardless of their theatrical performance.
The long-term consequences of this shift remain uncertain. Audience habits may continue evolving, and the relationship between theaters and streaming platforms is still being negotiated across the industry.
For now, however, recent award outcomes suggest that voters are acknowledging a simple reality: a film's cultural impact can no longer be measured solely by ticket sales. In an entertainment landscape increasingly shaped by digital viewing, success is being defined in new ways.

Bayern Munich's relentless run of form continued on Wednesday as the German champions secured a convincing 3-1 victory over RB Leipzig to advance to the semifinals of the German Cup.
The win extended Bayern's impressive unbeaten streak and kept alive hopes of winning multiple trophies this season. Already leading the Bundesliga standings and still competing in European competition, the team continues to demonstrate the consistency that has made it one of the most feared sides in world football.
Manager Thomas Tuchel praised his players after the match but was careful not to get carried away by discussions of potential silverware. While supporters have begun speculating about another historic campaign, the coach insisted that the focus remains firmly on the next challenge.
"We are playing well, but there is still a long way to go," Tuchel said after the game. "Football changes very quickly, and success depends on maintaining the same level every week."
The victory once again highlighted Bayern's depth. Several key players were rested, yet the team maintained its intensity and control throughout the match. Young talents stepped in alongside experienced internationals, helping Bayern dominate possession and create numerous scoring opportunities.
Much of the club's success this season has been built on a strong defensive foundation. The partnership at the heart of the defense has provided stability, while the midfield has effectively limited opponents' chances before they develop into serious threats.
At the other end of the pitch, Bayern's attacking players continue to deliver. Goals have come from across the squad, reducing the team's dependence on any single individual and making it difficult for opponents to contain them.
With crucial fixtures approaching in both domestic and European competitions, Bayern remains in contention for a remarkable season. The club's supporters are beginning to dream of another trophy-laden campaign, although players and coaches continue to stress the importance of staying focused on one match at a time.
Their next challenge arrives this weekend against a top-four rival, a match that could further strengthen Bayern's position and bring them another step closer to achieving their ambitions.

(CNN) Parents who push their children toward greatness have often been portrayed as controlling or misguided figures on screen, but "Rising Star" takes a different approach. The sports drama places its focus squarely on a determined father whose relentless belief in his daughter's talent becomes the foundation of the story. Anchored by a commanding lead performance from Michael B. Jordan, the film rises above familiar genre formulas even when it occasionally leans on them.
The movie chronicles the early years of track-and-field prodigy Maya Johnson (played by Nia Thompson), following her development under the guidance of her father, Marcus (Jordan). Long before college recruiters and national attention arrived, Marcus had already mapped out a detailed vision for her future, convinced she could become one of the country's elite athletes.
"We're not chasing medals," Marcus repeatedly tells skeptical observers. "We're building a legacy."
That confidence often borders on stubbornness. Marcus spends much of the film challenging coaches, questioning established training methods and insisting that his daughter progress at a pace he believes is right. His determination frequently creates tension with others, including Maya's mother (Regina Hall), who shares responsibility for her daughter's development but worries about the pressure being placed on her.
Director Gina Prince-Bythewood and screenwriter Aaron Covington present Marcus largely through an admiring lens. He is depicted as a tireless advocate who balances multiple jobs, shields his family from negative influences and remains unwavering in his belief that Maya deserves opportunities often denied to athletes from her background.
Even moments that could be interpreted as excessive—early-morning workouts, strict routines and difficult sacrifices—are framed as evidence of his commitment rather than flaws in judgment. As Maya's talent begins attracting national attention, the film increasingly validates Marcus' unconventional methods.
The story becomes somewhat more conventional in its later stages. Predictable conflicts emerge over sponsorship opportunities, coaching decisions and the demands that accompany growing fame. Inspirational montages and swelling music occasionally push the film toward familiar sports-drama territory.
Still, Jordan's performance remains compelling throughout. He captures Marcus' charisma, intensity and unshakable confidence, creating a character whose certainty can be frustrating while remaining understandable. His portrayal gives emotional weight to scenes that might otherwise feel overly familiar.
The film also appears interested in revisiting public perceptions of ambitious sports parents. By emphasizing Marcus' sacrifices and motivations, "Rising Star" argues that his actions stemmed less from personal ambition than from a desire to create opportunities for his daughter.
That perspective shapes nearly every aspect of the narrative. The result is a movie that functions not only as an inspirational sports story but also as a defense of a father whose methods were often questioned by those around him.
Whether audiences fully embrace that interpretation may vary. What is difficult to dispute, however, is the strength of the central performance. Thanks largely to Jordan's work, "Rising Star" succeeds in turning a familiar sports biography into an engaging character study.
"Rising Star" opens nationwide Friday. It is rated PG-13.

As lockdown measures continued across the country, aid workers in rural Kenya reported a sharp increase in cases of domestic violence, highlighting the unintended consequences of restrictions designed to slow the spread of Covid-19.
One recent case involved a 29-year-old woman from a village outside Eldoret who was admitted to a local hospital after an alleged assault by her partner. According to community outreach workers, the woman had been returning from a nearby market when an argument at home escalated into violence.
Neighbors who heard the disturbance contacted a local support organization, which dispatched volunteers to the scene. By the time they arrived, the woman was seriously injured and required immediate medical attention. With public transportation suspended under emergency regulations, volunteers transported her several miles to the nearest clinic using limited available resources.
Advocacy groups say such incidents have become increasingly common since movement restrictions were introduced. Many families have faced growing financial pressure as businesses closed and employment opportunities disappeared. At the same time, victims often find it more difficult to leave dangerous situations or seek assistance.
Human rights organizations argue that access to support services has been severely disrupted. Travel restrictions, limited transportation, and reduced staffing have slowed emergency responses and complicated efforts to investigate allegations of abuse.
Officials acknowledge the challenges but say resources have been stretched by the demands of the public health crisis. Law enforcement agencies continue to respond to reports, though response times have often increased in remote areas.
Recent data collected by local authorities suggests a substantial rise in reported domestic violence cases during the lockdown period. Child protection organizations have also reported an increase in calls involving abuse and neglect.
Experts caution that the problem extends beyond the immediate health emergency. Economic hardship, unemployment, and social isolation may continue to place strain on vulnerable households even after restrictions are lifted.
For survivors, access to medical care, legal support, and safe shelter remains critical. Aid groups have urged governments to ensure that protection services remain available during future emergencies and that victims can reach help without unnecessary delays.
Several weeks after leaving the hospital, the woman is continuing her recovery with support from relatives. Although the physical injuries are healing, she says the emotional impact of the incident remains difficult to overcome.

(CNN) The spotlight was firmly on the club's newest superstar, but his first appearance in front of home supporters ended with an unexpected moment as manager Carlos Mendes chose to substitute the forward during the second half of a tightly contested league match.
The hosts had fallen behind shortly after halftime when visiting midfielder Diego Herrera finished off a swift counterattack. The home side responded midway through the second period, drawing level through a penalty converted by captain Marco Silva.
Yet the defining image of the evening came with just under 20 minutes remaining. Mendes elected to replace star forward Javier Ortega, a decision that appeared to catch both the player and many supporters by surprise.
As Ortega walked toward the touchline, television cameras captured a brief exchange between the player and his coach. While there was no obvious confrontation, the forward's body language suggested frustration as he took his seat on the bench.
The substitution quickly became a major talking point among fans and pundits, particularly given Ortega's reputation as one of the game's most influential attacking players. Many expected him to remain on the field as the team searched for a winning goal.
Those questions became less pressing moments later when the hosts secured all three points. In stoppage time, striker Lucas Ferreira headed home from a cross delivered by winger Rafael Costa, sending the crowd into celebration.
Speaking after the match, Mendes defended his decision and emphasized that team considerations must come before individual preferences.
"When you have a squad full of talented players, difficult choices are part of the job," Mendes told reporters. "Every decision is made with the objective of helping the team. Sometimes players agree with those decisions, and sometimes they don't."
He added that he had briefly spoken with Ortega after the match and described the conversation as routine.
The forward showed flashes of his quality throughout the game. He tested the goalkeeper with a curling effort from distance and came close to scoring from a free kick that struck the crossbar. Nevertheless, he appeared to be working toward full sharpness after arriving at the club earlier in the season.
Supporters have eagerly anticipated the development of the team's high-profile attacking trio, and while signs of chemistry were visible, the partnership remains a work in progress.
The victory extended the club's strong start to the campaign, but discussion afterward centered less on the result than on the rare sight of a global superstar being withdrawn before the final whistle.

As vaccination campaigns accelerated across much of North America and Western Europe, several countries in Central Asia found themselves still waiting for large-scale deliveries of Covid-19 vaccines.
While wealthier nations had already secured millions of doses through advance purchase agreements, countries such as Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, and Uzbekistan faced slower rollouts and uncertainty over future supplies. Many governments in the region joined international initiatives designed to improve vaccine access, but delivery schedules remained unpredictable.
Health officials warned that delays could leave vulnerable populations exposed for longer periods and slow economic recovery efforts. Several countries had planned vaccination campaigns around expected shipments that had yet to arrive, forcing authorities to revise their timelines.
Analysts said the situation highlighted broader inequalities in global vaccine distribution. Nations with greater financial resources were often able to negotiate direct deals with pharmaceutical companies, while smaller economies relied heavily on international partnerships and donation programs.
As shortages persisted, other powers moved to expand their influence in the region. Russia supplied doses of its Sputnik V vaccine to several neighboring countries, while China increased exports of its domestically produced vaccines. These deliveries were welcomed by governments eager to begin immunization programs and reduce pressure on healthcare systems.
In some cases, vaccine agreements carried diplomatic significance beyond public health. Observers noted that successful vaccine deliveries could strengthen political ties and improve perceptions of partner countries at a time when global competition for influence remained intense.
Despite logistical challenges, officials across the region expressed optimism that supplies would gradually increase in the coming months. Additional shipments were expected through international programs as production capacity expanded worldwide.
Public health experts argued that equitable vaccine access was not only a humanitarian concern but also a practical necessity. As long as large populations remained unvaccinated, they warned, the virus would continue to circulate and create risks for neighboring countries.
For governments still awaiting substantial deliveries, the hope was that international cooperation and increased production would eventually narrow the gap between countries that had secured vaccines early and those left waiting on the sidelines.

Story highlights
	•	A reader asks whether extreme heat contributed to recent migration pressures in Central America
	•	Researchers say climate change has increased the likelihood of prolonged droughts in the region
	•	Some experts argue environmental stress alone cannot explain migration and political instability
	•	Governments and international organizations increasingly describe climate change as a risk multiplier
(CNN) Can climate change help explain why large numbers of people leave their homes and migrate elsewhere?
That's the question submitted by a CNN reader as part of our ongoing climate series. Specifically, the reader asked whether worsening drought conditions in Central America have played a role in recent waves of migration toward the United States.
A growing number of policymakers and researchers believe the answer is at least partly yes.
Several international organizations have pointed to climate-related pressures, including prolonged droughts, crop failures and water shortages, as factors that can intensify existing social and economic problems. While these environmental stresses are rarely viewed as the sole cause of migration, experts say they can make already difficult circumstances much worse.
Supporters of this view often point to the so-called "Dry Corridor," a region stretching across parts of Guatemala, Honduras, El Salvador and Nicaragua. In recent years, farmers there have experienced repeated harvest failures linked to irregular rainfall patterns and unusually high temperatures.
According to reports from humanitarian organizations, many rural families depend heavily on small-scale agriculture for food and income. When crops fail repeatedly, households may struggle to meet basic needs, prompting some residents to move to urban areas or seek opportunities abroad.
Scientific research has strengthened concerns about the role of climate change in these developments. Several studies have found that rising global temperatures are increasing the likelihood and severity of drought conditions in parts of Central America. Climate models also suggest that some regions may experience more frequent periods of extreme heat and water stress in the future.
The argument is not that climate change directly causes migration. Rather, researchers describe a chain of effects. Reduced agricultural productivity can contribute to economic hardship, which may increase food insecurity, debt and displacement. In communities already facing poverty, crime or weak institutions, those pressures can become particularly significant.
Still, not all experts agree on the strength of the connection.
Critics note that migration decisions are influenced by many factors, including economic opportunities, family networks, public safety and government policies. They argue that focusing too heavily on climate risks can oversimplify complex social realities.
Others point out that droughts occur in many regions around the world without producing comparable migration patterns. In their view, environmental conditions are only one piece of a much larger puzzle.
Many researchers acknowledge those concerns. They emphasize that migration rarely has a single cause and that climate-related factors usually interact with political, economic and demographic conditions already present in a society.
Even so, governments and international agencies increasingly view climate change as a force that can amplify existing vulnerabilities. Security analysts often describe it as a "threat multiplier," meaning it can worsen tensions and challenges that already exist rather than create them from scratch.
The discussion comes as countries continue debating how to address global warming and prepare for its consequences. Beyond rising temperatures and sea levels, policymakers are paying growing attention to how environmental change may affect food systems, economic stability and human migration.
Whether climate change is a primary driver or a contributing factor, many experts agree that understanding its role will become increasingly important as the planet continues to warm.

The pie charts compare the nutritional composition of two meals: a grilled chicken sandwich and a bowl of rice with vegetables.
In both meals, carbohydrates represent the largest proportion of the nutrients. They account for 40% of the rice and vegetable meal and 30% of the grilled chicken sandwich.
For the grilled chicken sandwich, protein makes up 28% of the total nutritional content, making it the second-largest component. Saturated fat accounts for 18%, while glucose represents 12%. The remaining 12% consists of other nutrients.
In contrast, the rice and vegetable meal contains a much lower proportion of protein, at 15%. Saturated fat is responsible for 10% of the total, whereas glucose makes up 20%. Other nutrients account for the remaining 15% of the meal.
Overall, carbohydrates are the dominant nutrient in both meals, although the grilled chicken sandwich contains more protein and saturated fat, while the rice and vegetable meal has a higher percentage of glucose and other nutrients.

Modern society is highly competitive. From school examinations to career advancement, individuals are constantly encouraged to outperform others. Competition has undoubtedly contributed to economic growth and technological progress. However, while it may benefit society as a whole, its impact on individuals is often less positive. This essay argues that excessive competitiveness can be harmful because it reduces creativity and weakens cooperation among people.
To begin with, a highly competitive environment may discourage creativity and individual differences. In most competitions, success is measured using a limited set of criteria, which means people are rewarded for meeting specific standards rather than exploring new approaches. As a result, individuals tend to follow established methods instead of taking risks. For example, many organizations evaluate employees mainly according to measurable outcomes such as sales figures or productivity targets. Although these indicators are useful, they may overlook qualities such as innovation, leadership, and artistic talent. Consequently, workers may focus only on activities that improve their evaluation scores, reducing diversity in ideas and approaches.
